This method was chosen because several DeFi have a closing price between 0 and 1, which makes it impossible to calculate returns following Campbell et al. (1997p.11) . However, the daily returns of the VIX, S&P GSCI crude oil index, and S&P GSCI gold index were calculated following Campbell et al. (1997p.11 ) method:

where Pit is the closing price of index i (VIX, S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index, or S&P GSCI Gold Index) at moment t. VIX, S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index, and S&P GSCI Gold Index have trading days without trade (Saturday and Sunday), but DeFi has trades in those days. Thus, we suppose that VIX, S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index, and S&P GSCI Gold Index have the same closing prices on days without trade as the last day with trade (Friday).

Descriptive statistics for panel data with 9854 observations are presented in Table 1 . The references in Telegram chats and Twitter have a lower number of observations because there are no mentions every day and because of the daily variation calculation. DeFi returns are represented by a dichotomous variable, where '1 ′ is for positive or zero returns, and '0 ′ for negative returns.

To measure the relationship between assets in a nonlinear way, we use a logit-probit model, a widely used econometric method (Lennox, 1999) . This nonlinear methodology has been applied to stocks and volatility by Thomas et al. (2014) and many others. We will take into consideration the results in the same way as other authors have done (Zelner, 2009) .

where Rn it represents the daily returns of DeFi (dichotomous variable), Rv it is the daily return of VIX, Rc it represents the daily returns of the S&P GSCI crude oil index, Rg it is the daily returns of the S&P GSCI Gold Index, TE it is the daily variation of mentions in Telegram chats, and TW it represents the daily variation of mentions on Twitter.

Table 2 shows the correlations between the variables. The VIX and variation in Twitter mentions are negatively correlated with DeFi returns. This suggests that when volatility increases, DeFi returns decrease, and vice versa. In contrast, Twitter and Telegram chat references are positively correlated to DeFi returns. That is, an increase in Twitter or Telegram mentions could cause an increase in DeFi returns. Furthermore, the correlations between the S&P GSCI crude oil index returns and S&P GSCI gold index returns are positive, suggesting that an increase in these indices could cause an increase in DeFi returns. These suggest that DeFi movements are similar to market movements.

Table 3 shows the logit-probit results for the whole sample. The results were similar for both regressions, with coefficient estimates for all variables except the S&P GSCI Crude Oil Index returns and the daily variation of Twitter mentions are significant. DeFi reacts inversely to market volatility (VIX), moves in the same direction as gold, while the coefficient estimate of gold is significantly higher than the coefficient estimate of VIX. Results also suggest a strong correlation between DeFi and the references in Telegram.

The results suggest that DeFis can act as a safe haven, like gold. As shown by the positive correlation and significance of the logit-probit model estimates, parallelism with gold in terms of volatility is a hot topic in crypto research. Some authors such as Dyhrberg (2016) support it, while others such as Klein et al. (2018) do not. Both use GARCH methods, so this could be a methodology for future research. Indeed, our results are significant, but not in the higher range of significance, so other methodologies could help support hypothesis H3.

Following the seminal article by Whaley (2000) , which outlined the methodology used in calculating VIX, Diavatopoulos et al., (2007) confirmed that implied volatility is superior to realized volatility in determining future returns. Interestingly, it has also been shown that the VIX is influenced by a range of characteristics demonstrated by non-technical investors in social media (López-Cabarcos et al., 2017) . As implied volatility is our main interest, the VIX is an important variable to check if traditional stock volatility could be related to DeFi volatility. Our correlation and logit-probit model results support the inverse relationship, because the decrease in DeFi causes an increase in VIX, and vice versa. Our results are contrary to the findings of Lopez-Cabarcos et al. (2021) on a positive causality between VIX and returns on Bitcoin, and are more attuned to findings that VIX has a negative impact on risk premiums (Durand et al., 2011) or returns on stock indices (Giot, 2005) . Here, we used the S&P GSCI Gold Index to track the COMEX gold futures. While it is widely tradeable, its close relationship with spot prices is not affected (Jena et al., 2018) . The positive and statistically significant coefficient estimate of the gold proxy implies that Dyhrberg's (2016) claims that Bitcoin is positioned between gold, as a store value, and the US currency, and hence, can be put forward as a hedging instrument, could also be partially applied to DeFi. H1 and the more significant H3 seem to reinforce the idea of a safe haven for DeFi tokens.

The GSCI crude oil index showed no significant influence on the studied DeFi, indicating that H2 was not proven. Other studies comparing the influence between oil assets and Bitcoin have shown that the production of oil positively influences Bitcoin (Kaabia et al., 2020) , or support the idea of Bitcoin as part of a diversified portfolio due to its low correlation with commodities, especially oil (Symitsi and Chalvatzis, 2019) . The different time frames and methods make the comparison difficult.

The relationship between volatility and user-generated content shows that the daily variation of Telegram chats mentions influence DeFi returns positively (H4). Contrary to Pinero-Chousa et al. (2021) , we find that the influence of Twitter mentions is insignificant (H5). These results are in line with other studies (López-Cabarcos et al., 2017) , which show the influence of UGC and volatility depending on the type of investor and social network. In this study, Telegram chat groups of the DeFi project are more appealing to investors and people interested in the project. Contrastingly, Twitter is a more open platform for people with close interest in this type of project. As this is a subjective appreciation, more research can be conducted on the influence of UGC on DeFi.

As was mentioned, this study makes an important contribution to the extant body of knowledge by examining the impact of selected sentiment variables on DeFi returns. Telegram chats support our claim, contrary to some previous studies, whereas Twitter is not a potential tool for trading in DeFi. While commodity markets and stock market volatility are relevant in this study, the S&P 500 Index is not an important variable in determining DeFi returns.

In a very low or negative interest rate environment, investors are forced to take more risks or look for safe havens. Cryptocurrencies are a risky investment. However, this study found that gold influences DeFi, and VIX inversely influences DeFi. This makes DeFi a hedge against the volatility of the stock market and a safe haven. Consequently, these assets are useful in investment portfolios to balance risk and volatility.

We live in the information era, and everybody can write anything on any web or platform on the internet. Telegram is a private communication channel that usually focuses on one topic, whereas Twitter is a public and multi-topic channel. According to the results of this study, information from private and topic-focused platforms is more relevant for DeFi projects in terms of its correlation with token prices. As most crypto projects are present in both channels, this study helped assess their appropriateness.

The main objective of DeFi is to remove middlemen from financial services. Its adoption has exploded and it seems to be just starting. Depending on the implications of the investment and communication perspective, DeFi can be considered either an opportunity or a threat to the existing financial system.

In terms of volatility, we analyse the influence of some traditional assets on some very specific crypto assets-decentralised finance tokens. As they are quite new, we thoroughly discuss these assets in the literature review. These results lead us to conclude that: (1) VIX inversely influences DeFi, (2) GSI crude oil has no influence on DeFi, and (3) the gold index influences DeFi, prompting us to claim that DeFi acts as a safe haven. This study can be added to a very broad and debated topic, and the alignment with methodologies and timeframes of other studies could be part of future research for better comparison.

The results obtained in user content generation are as follows: (4) Telegram chats influence DeFi and, (5) Twitter activity does not influence DeFi. As both assets and UGC platforms change significantly over time, the conclusion should be taken as specific to this case, requiring a broader study in terms of time, assets, and platforms.

As a general conclusion and taking into account that the technologies of DeFi assets are in their infancy, we can confirm there are relationships amongst some traditional assets, user-generated content, and DeFi. This influence may imply that DeFi tokens are safe havens, hedges for stock market volatility, and are linked to activity in Telegram's private groups.

The success of decentralised finance has attracted widespread interest from investors. Growth during the last months can be a sign for investors with a risk appetite looking for significant profit and a safe haven, or be included as high-risk investment instruments of a diversified portfolio. This should also be a warning for policymakers trying to avoid the situation that the ICO created several years ago. This situation of considerable returns from weak-value propositions leads to a rush of projects that lack solid foundations, or are even scams. As DeFi seems to have linked properties to some traditional investments and has the goal of recreating traditional finance services, we strongly recommend analysing it carefully.

DeFi is a novel product with limited dataset availability, and changes in the regulatory environment can have an impact on out-of-sample results. Thus, the sample size is affected by the lack of longer time-series data. In this study, we include data from the pre-COVID 19 period, and the results could vary if analysed during and after the current health crisis. With an increase in the number of DeFi, a larger sample size is possible in the future and illiquidity issues can be addressed more meticulously. It is also advisable to include the variations in current sentiment variables and examine the introduction of new ones with the advancement of AI. In future research, it will be necessary to examine not only the COVID-19 period, but also the post-pandemic period. In summary, the DeFi landscape of projects and their behaviour as a category of crypto assets is rising and may change significantly in the future. This demands much more research effort.

This paper analysis the dynamic of the volatility connectedness between three main categories of the cryptocurrency market (mineable coins, non-mineable coins and tokens). 1 Furthermore, it investigates factors (internal and external) that can help to predict the net directional and total volatility connectedness indices. We find that the CC market is highly connected where mining coins dominate the CC market and are typically net transmitter of shocks. For the non-mining coins, a low connectedness with all mining coins and tokens versus a high connectedness with Stellar are highlighted. The results show also that, unlike previous studies, Ethereum is the dominant contributor to volatility spillover and the only CC net pairwise transmitter in our sample. Interestingly, we find that most of the tokens are highly connected with their platform (Ethereum) rather than bitcoin. Finally, we find that both financial and macroeconomic indicators play an important role in explaining the total and net directional connectedness, a result that can significantly help cryptocurrency market participants and investors improve their risk management and the benefits from diversification.

Over the last few years, CCs 2 have attracted the interest of several types of market participants including miners, investors, portfolio managers, academic researchers and policy makers. The specific features and characteristic of CCs such as their high returns and high time varying volatilities are the main motives behind this (Corbet et al., 2020; Yousaf and Ali, 2021; Mariana et al., 2021; Maouchi et al., 2021; Montasser et al., 2021) . In fact, beyond the high level of returns that can offer CCs, investors have been also interested by the financial opportunities that can generate these new types of assets (digital assets) such as financial diversification and hedging strategies (Corbet et al., 2018 (Corbet et al., , 2020;; Charfeddine et al., 2020; Yousaf and Ali, 2021; Mariana et al., 2021; Maouchi et al., 2021) . Empirically, while the number of research studies investigating the financial aspects (stylized facts, intra-and inter-dependence, and financial diversification) has exploded only recently that academic researchers have been interested to explore the level of financial connectedness involving CCs (Yi et al., 2018; Koutmos, 2018; Ji et al., 2019a) .

This paper try to contribute to this gap in the digital financial literature by investigating the dynamic features of connectedness between different categories of CCs. Moreover, it examines both internal and external factors that can help to predict the total and net directional connectedness indices. An important motivation of this study is the absence of a common classification of different types of CCs. For instance, according to Hardle et al. (2020) , cryptocurrencies may be grouped into seven broad classes. The first class, originally designed as a transaction mechanism, which include for example Bitcoin and Litecoin. Ethereum, Tezos, EOS, and DFinity fall into the second class known as a distributed computation token. The third class of cryptocurrency is called a utility token, which is a programmable blockchain asset (for example, Golem, Storj, Sia and FileCoin). The fourth class of cryptocurrency is a security token. The fifth class is called fungible tokens (for example, ERC-20). A nonfungible token is the sixth classification. In this case, each token is unique and not interchangeable with another (For example, Cryptokitties and Decentraland (LAND). The final class of cryptocurrencies is called stablecoins (For example, tether (USDT), Circle's USDC, LBXPeg).

In this paper, we adopt a more general classification approach as we categorize all cryptocurrnecies into two main categories namely: (1) coins, and (2) tokens. In addition, we divide the coin category into two sub-categories namely mining coins (such as Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), DASH (DASH), Litecoin (LTC)) and the non-mining coins (such as Ripple (XRP), Binance Coin (BNB), EOS (EOS), Stellar (XLM)). Regarding the tokens category and compared to coins, the interest in this second category can be explained by the fact that they have been only recently emerged. The well known and most traded tokens include: Maker (MKR), Basic Attention (BAT), Chainlink (LINK), and Augur (REP) .

Despite that all the CCs (coins and tokens) share some important common features such as the blockchain technology, there exist also some other important characteristics that can distinguish coins from tokens and vis-versa. For instance, coins are primary designed for purpose of decentralized peer-to-peer payments. 3 The main differences between the mineable and non-mineable coins is that contrary to the non-mining coins where the supply of coins is managed by the company offering these coins, the supply for the case of mining coins is endogenously determined by the mining process. The mining process is the process where the miners (individuals or companies) are rewarded when they validate the mining coin transaction. Yet, the tokens are a new form of crowdfunding that offer tokens to investors with the only potential gain is the increase in the price of tokens. Token offerings are smart contracts programmed on distributed ledger technology (Momtaz, 2020) . Furthermore, tokens are applications built upon existing blockchains, whereas coins are created on their own blockchain (Momtaz, 2019) . For further information on the market evolution, offering mechanisms, and token types, see the paper by Momtaz (2019) .

This study has two main objectives. First, it analyzes the intra-cryptocurrency market volatility spillovers. Second, it investigates whether macroeconomic and financial indicators can help in predicting the dynamic behavior of the net directional and total volatility connectedness indices. Several reasons motivate the focus on this research question. Firstly, while there have been a plenty of studies that have tried to explore the level of connectedness between the CCs and traditional financial assets (Corbet et al., 2018; Koutmos, 2018; Yi et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2019a; Billah et al., 2022) , only few have analyzed the connectedness between different type of CCs. Secondly, the recent rapid gaining of interest of non-mining coins and tokens in the last two years has question the dominance and influential position of the mining coins in term of net transmitter/receiver volatility spillover. Thirdly, the recent emergence of a large set of tokens (such as DeFi, decentralized finance) offer some new other potential benefits for markets participants but open new questions, including whether Ethereum, as blockchain platform for almost all the tokens foot_0 will be the more influential in terms of net transmitter/ receiver spillover. Finally, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that attempts to fill these gaps by exploring the relationship and volatility connectedness between different CCs types (coins versus tokens, and mining versus non-mining for coins). Our study classify the different CCs based on their principal characteristics of coins versus tokens, and of mining versus non-mining for the coins. In addition, we explore whether the tokens platforms influence their volatility connectedness.

A further motivation is the lack of studies that have investigated the common factors that can explain the dynamic spillovers between different digital assets. Thus, in addition to the evaluation of the spillovers between the considered cryptocurrencies, this study aims to identify factors (internal to CC market, macroeconomic, financial of the dynamic connectedness cryptocurrencies. We first investigate the determinants of total connectedness. Then, we extend our analysis by investigating the factors affecting the net connectedness for each single cryptocurrency.

In this paper, we report several key findings. First, mining coins dominate the CC market and are typically net transmitter of shocks while the tokens are mostly followers. Secondly, the non-mining coins exhibit a low connectedness with all mining coins and tokens versus a high connectedness with Stellar are highlighted. Further, unlike previous studies, we found that Ethereum is the dominant contributor to volatility spillover and the only CC net pairwise transmitter in our sample. More interestingly, our findings reveal that most of the tokens are highly connected with their platform (Ethereum) rather than bitcoin. Finally, the findings of our paper indicate that several financial and macroeconomic factors significantly contribute in the dynamics the total and net directional connectedness.

In the last few years, a large number of studies in the finance literature has been devoted to studying financial connectedness (returns and volatility) among various assets types. The main motives is their important implications on portfolios performance (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2012, 2014) . Recently, a special attention has been attributed to studying connectedness involving digital financial assets. The growing interest on digital financial assets was mainly due to the high financial returns of this new class of financial assets and the financial opportunities that can offer for investors and market participants in terms of hedging and portfolios diversification (Akhtaruzzaman et al., 2019; Bouri et al., 2017; Guesmi et al., 2019; Platanakis and Urquhart, 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Charfeddine et al., 2020) .

The overall connectedness literature involving digital financial assets can be regrouped into two strands of literature: (1) connectedness between traditional financial assets and digital assets; and (2) connectedness involving only digital financial assets. The first strand of literature concerns studies investigating how Bitcoin is correlated with traditional financial assets and alternative investments, global and emerging equity (Kajtazi and Moro, 2019) , stock market indices (Benlagha, 2020; Benlagha and El Omari, 2022; Gil-Alana et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019) , implied volatility of financial markets (Akyildirim et al., 2019) , industry sectors and bond index (Akhtaruzzaman et al., 2019; Benlagha and Hemrit, 2022) , a variety of other financial assets, including stocks, fixed income assets, energy and precious metals (Benlagha and Mseddi, 2019; Charfeddine et al., 2020; Corbet et al., 2018; Okorie and Lin, 2020) . Note that most of these studies have focused on a single cryptocurrency market, notably the Bitcoin market as it is the most matured and liquid crypto market. In fact, academic researchers have focused much more on the Bitcoin than on others CCs, although the gap has been narrowed.

Another strand in the connectedness literature that emerged more recently has focused on the cross-cryptocurrency markets (Koutmos, 2018; Yi et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2019a; Balli et al., 2019) . Katsiampa (2019a) provides evidence that time-varying conditional correlations exist between Ether and Bitcoin. This study is extended by Katsiampa (2019b) to include other CCs such as Ripple, Litecoin, and Stellar. Findings show that conditional correlations between different pairs of CCs are dynamic. Another closely related paper is Aslanidis et al. (2019) who document that conditional correlations among CCs (Bitcoin, DASH, Monero, and Ripple) are positive and varying across time. Katsiampa et al. (2019b) identify bi-directional volatility spillover effects between all the three pairs of Bitcoin, Ether and Litecoin and show existence of time-varying conditional correlations with positive correlations mostly prevailing. These findings are corroborated by the results of Katsiampa et al. (2019a) using intra-day data for eight CCs. In the same vein, Canh et al. (2019) find that volatility spillovers exist with strong positive correlations among seven largest CCs? examine the volatility spillover between Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Litecoin using the VAR-AGARCH model. They find that the conditional correlations between all pairs of cryptocurrencies are observed to be higher during the COVID-19 period compared to the pre-COVID-19 period.

Relying on Diebold and Yilmaz (2014) framework of variance decomposition, another set of studies investigating the connectedness of CCs document higher interdependencies between CCs, suggesting that CCs are becoming more integrated. For instance, Koutmos (2018) provides evidence that Bitcoin is the most influential regarding to volatility spillovers in the CC market. Further, results indicate that the intensity of these spillovers has risen steadily over time, due to a higher level of market uncertainty. Relatedly, Yi et al. (2018) find that the level of connectedness among CCs is high and fluctuate cyclically. Their results also reveal that CCs with big capitalizations are more probable to propagate volatility shocks to others. Similarly, Balli et al. (2019) find that the magnitude of short-term connectedness among CCs is much higher than medium and long term, and this connectedness is negatively related to economic uncertainty. Same results are found by Ciaian et al. (2018) applying the ARDL model to examine interdependencies between Bitcoin and Altcoin markets: the relationship is more pronounced in the short-run. Interestingly, Ji et al. (2019a) examine connectedness across six large CCs, by employing the approach of Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2015) . They find that Bitcoin is the dominant contributor of volatility spillovers, followed by Litecoin. More recently, Antonakakis et al. (2019) find that Ethereum is the number one net transmitting cryptocurrency, followed by Bitcoin. They also show that the degree of connectedness is positively associated with market uncertainty. Borri (2019) finds,by estimating the conditional tail-risk, that Bitcoin, Ether, Ripple and Litecoin are highly correlated. In a somewhat similar vein, Xu et al. (2020) obtain similar results for 23 CCs, and also find that the total connectedness increases steadily over time. Their results further reveal that Ethereum is the largest systemic risk emitter, while Bitcoin is the largest systemic risk receiver. In the same line, Tiwari et al. (2020) examine the dependence and contagion risk between Bitcoin, Litecoin and Ripple. Their results provide evidence of significant risk contagion among price returns of major CCs, both in bear and bull markets. On the contrary, Zieba et al (2019) find that variations in the price of Bitcoin do not affect and are not affected by variations in prices of other CCs. Using the multifractal analysis to investigate the herding biases, Mnif et al. (2020) quantify the self-similarity intensity of cryptocurrency (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Ripple, Litecoin and Binance) returns' during the COVID-19 pandemic and report evidence that COVID-19 has a positive impact on the cryptocurrency market efficiency. In a similar vein, Vidal-Tomas (2021) uses a network approach to identify the transitions of the cryptocurrency market over time, and observes that COVID-19 significantly affected cryptocurrencies during a short period of financial panic, giving rise to a remarkable increase of market synchronization for Bitcoin, Ethereum, Ripple and Litecoin.

All in all, the broad picture emerging from this literature is that CCs are highly correlated, and the total connectedness increases steadily over time, suggesting that market integration is a continuing and persistent phenomenon. Furthermore, although much effort has been devoted to examining the cross-correlations between CCs, little attention has been paid to the volatility connectedness or spillover effects among different type of CCs. Thus, our study using the spillover index approach to analyze the volatility connectedness across mining coins, non-mining coins and tokens will fill this gap.

To analyze the volatility connectedness between the twelve most traded cryptocurrencies reflecting the three categories of mining coins, non-mining coins and the tokens, we propose to use the Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) framework. Our choice of the Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) approach is mainly motivated by its several advantages when compared to other approaches (Otranto, 2015) . For instance, beyond fulfilling our main goal of assessing the connectedness levels and directions among the digital assets under investigation, this approach allows the investigation of bilateral connectedness as well as displaying the strength and the dynamic of volatility connectedness. Furthermore, the method allows accurate comparisons among alternative model structures and variable sets. More importantly, compared with concurrent correlation-based methods such as the Wavelet analysis and the multivariate GARCH models, Costa et al. (2021) suggest that Diebold and Yilmaz (2012) methodology is advantageous as it enables inferring direction of spillovers for a wide number of simultaneously interacting variables in a clear and compact way. However, the major shortcoming of the method is that the estimated results might be influenced by the chosen window size. To overcome this limitation, the order of the estimated VAR should be well specified. Furthermore, a robustness check methodology should be appropriately conducted. In this section, we will follow and use the same notations for the connectedness measures as in the original paper of Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) .

The moving average representation is a key of the dynamic of the system under investigation since it allows the calculation of the variance decompositions. This variance decomposition splits the Hstep-ahead forecast error variances of each variable of the model into different parts attributable to the various system shocks. 3.1. Connectedness measures Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2014) propose several measures of volatility connectedness that we group into three main categories based on the type of connectedness (single, total and grand total): (1) own, pairwise and net pairwise directional connectedness, (2) total directional connectedness "from" and "to", (3) net total directional connectedness, and finally (4) the total connectedness index. These four measures of connectedness are summarized in the table below:

The upper-left block of Table 1 reports both the own-connectedness (diagonal entries) and the pairwises directional connectedness from cryptocurrency i to j and from j to i (off-diagonal entries). To calculate the net pairwise directional connectedness, one can just calculate the difference between the two paiwrise directional connectedness. These four individuals connectedness are defined as follow:

The two total directional connectedness "from others" and "to others" are also known as the total directional connectedness "received" and "transmitted", respectively. The total directional connectedness are defined as the share of the H-step forecast error variance of one variable coming from shocks arising in all the other variables. As shown in Table 1 , there are 2N total directional connectedness, an N total directional connectedness "received" and N total directional connectedness "transmitted". As for the own and pairwise directional connectedness, the net total directional connectedness is calculated as the difference between the two "from others" and "to others" total directional connectedness. These three total directional connectedness can be defined as,

The total connectedness index is the grand total of the off-diagonal entries of the upper-left block of Table 1 . This total connectedness measure is viewed as a systemic risk measure (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2012, 2014) . Analytically, it is defined by,

Two measures of market volatility will be used in this study: (1) the Garman and Klass (1980) measure and (2) the Parkinson (1980) measure. While, the results of the first measure will be discussed in details in the paper, the results for the second measure will be only presented as for robustness checking. 5 The Garman and Klass (1980) measure is defined by,

Daily high, low, opening and closing prices are used to calculate the Garman and Klass (1980) and Parkinson (1980) volatility series for the twelve considered cryptocurrencies. All the data was retrieved from https://coinmarketcap.com . Among the thousands of cryptocurrencies available for trading, we focus our analysis on the top CCs in terms of market capitalization and data availability. Precisely, the list of selected cryptocurrencies was subject to two major criteria: (1) we restrict our analysis on the top four mining coins and top four non-mining coins, and top four tokens in terms of market capitalization, and (2) the sample period used should include the last recent period of cryptocurrencies prices boom and bust (end of 2017 and beginning 2018). Moreover, for reason of data availability for the four tokens, we have been restricted to focus our analysis on the period from October 28th, 2017 to April 5th, 2020, which give us a total number of observations of T = 891. The final list of twelve CCs include: (1) the top four mining coins namely Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), Litecoin (LTC) and DASH (DASH), (2) the top four non-mining coins which includes Ripple (XRP), EOS (EOS), Binance coin (BNB), and Stellar (XLM)), and (3) finally the list of the top four tokens that includes Maker (MKR), Basic Attention (BAT), Chainlink (LINK), Augur (REP) (Hardle et al., 2020; Maouchi et al., 2021; Montasser et al., 2021) .

In terms of market capitalization, the twelve CC considered in this study account for more than 81% of the whole CC market as of April 5th, 2020. In our sample, the market value of the mining coins is $145.13b US, which exceeds the 90.9% of the whole market value of the twelve CCs. The non-mining coins and the tokens market values are $13.07b and $1.41b, which represent approximately 8.1% and approximately 1% of the total market value of the twelve CCs, respectively. This clearly shows the dominance of the cryptocurrency market by the mining coins where the sole Bitcoin market share exceeds the 64.4% of the total CC market (more than 5400 cryptocurrencies by the end of April 5th, 2020).

Table 2 lists some other important characteristics of the twelve cryptocurrencies. The most attractive feature is the high variability of CC prices. An analysis by category shows that during our period of analysis, the prices of the mining coins have been multiplied by 285 times for Bitcoin and 5002 times for DASH. For non-mining coins prices have been multiplied between 44 times for EOS and 1127 times for Ripple. Finally, for tokens, prices have been multiplied by 10 times for ChainLink and 74 times for Maker. In terms of ranking, seven out of the twelve cryptocurrencies are ranked among the top 10 crypto-market. Only the four tokens are ranked above the top 20 cryptocurrencies.

Fig. 1 displays the volatility series by CCs category (mining coins, non-mining coins and tokens). We remark that all the three subfigures reported in Fig. 1 show presence of extremes in the daily volatilities of most of the studied cryptocurrencies. For instance, the high volatility observed in 2017 is mainly due to the announcement of the US regulatory to reject the Bitcoin ETF in February 2017. In addition, targeting the Ethereum platform by cyber-attack in September 2017 led to a sharp increase in the volatility of most of the digital assets. However, the rapid increase in the BTC price notably between August ($2797) and December ($19 050), which corresponds to a rise in BCT price of approximately 600%, coincides with the Tweets by John McAfee in November 2017 that Bitcoin will reach $1 million by 2020. On the contrary, the value of BTC shows a decreasing trend between January and December 2018. We can offer possible explanations behind the sharp drop in prices from $ 10 823 to $3429. During this year, two major events took place in the Table 1 Connectedness table (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2014) .

Bitcoin market. First, at the beginning of the year, UBS Chairman Axel Weber and Warren Buffet, CEO of Berkshire Hathaway, warns investors to steer clear of Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies in general. Second, in February, the Chinese government states its intent to ban foreign cryptocurrency exchange platforms in addition to domestic exchanges. Another range of extreme volatility is observed by the end of 2020. Indeed, in that particular period, the Bitcoin was trading at record highs reaching $30 000 and most of the cryptocurrencies behaved similarly. This performance led financial analysts and investors to claim that Bitcoin is a "digital gold" and a store of value. We report also basic descriptive statistics in Table 3 . Several interesting facts emerge from this Table . First, the within day volatility is the lowest for the mining coins and the highest for the tokens. Second, the level of volatility is very high during the period of mid-November 2017, mid-February 2018 and mid-March 2020. Finally, the volatility is persistent according to the estimated fractional long memory parameter (Gaussian semi-parametric estimator of Robinson (1995) ).

In addition, we report the correlation matrix along with the scatter diagram in Fig. 2 for the GK volatility measure. These figures provide a useful preliminary indication about the degree of correlation between the different CCs volatility. Overall, we observe that the correlation is highly significant, e.g. at 1% level of significance, independently from the CC category. In particular, we found that Ethereum has the highest correlations with all the other CCs coins and tokens. Fig. 2 also shows that the intra-mining coins correlations are the highest compared to the intra-non-mining and intra-tokens correlations.
