In the next section, we analyze the market volatility connectedness between the three cryptocurrency categories (intra-cryptocurrency). We start our analysis by discussing the results of the static connectedness based on the different connectedness measures presented in Section 3.1. Then, in the second step, we deeper the analysis by investigating the dynamic connectedness using the rolling window approach. Finally, in the last step, we explore and examine the possible determinants of both the net and total connectedness.

To assess how volatility shocks are transmitted across cryptocurrencies, we begin our analysis by focusing on the "individual" connectedness measures. The results matrix of static connectedness for the twelve CCs are reported in Table 4 for the GK volatility measure. Several important features can be drawn from the table. First, the results show that the own connectedness elements are the largest individual elements of the table. On average, tokens possess the largest own connectedness, followed by non-mining coins and finally mining coins. Precisely, the results show that the own-effects (own share) for the mining coins range between 19.58% for Ethereum and 27.08% for the DASH coin. For the non-mining coins, the own-effects range between 26.42% for EOS and 36.38% for XRP (Ripple). Finally, for the tokens, the own-connectednesse range between 40.44% for LINK and 56.77% for MKR coin to 81.08% for the BAT coin. Comparing our results of own-effects to some recent published articles, we find that the own connectedness is much lower than Ji et al. (2019a) who find values of own connectedness raging from 46.7% for Litecoin to 97.1% for DASH for their 07/08/2015 -22/02/2018 sample and range between 37% to 60.8% for the 01/01/2017-22/02/2018 subsample. There are several possible explanations for this high difference in results: (1) the cryptocurrency market becomes more integrated over time inducing an Fig. 2 . This figure reports on top the (absolute) value of the correlation and the result of the correlation test is given by stars. On bottom, the bivariate scatterplots, with a fitted line. increase in the spillover of shocks in the cryptocurrency market (a similar result is observed by Yi et al. ( 2018 )) (2) the level of the own connectedness is negatively correlated with the number of cryptocurrency and the data period considered in the sample. However, when comparing the own connectedness of the mining coin block to the own connectedness of studies focusing on conventional financial market, we find evidence for strong similarity in the level of the own-effects (see for instance Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) ).

The second important feature observed in Table 4 concerns the pairwise directional connectedness, C H i←j , that are presented by the off-diagonal elements of the upper-left 13 submatrix. The results show that the bilateral connectedness is larger between the mineable submarket coins, followed by the pairwise bilateral connectedness between mining and non-mining coins, and finally between nonmineable and token. The largest observed individual connectedness is from Ethereum to Bitcoin (C H BTC←ETH = 13.81). The second observed largest pairwise directional connectedness is from Ethereum to Bitcoin, (C H LTC←ETH = 13.02). We find also that the third and fourth largest pairwise directional connectedness involve Ethereum as transmitter, (C H EOS←ETH = 12.85) and (C H DASH←ETH = 12.22). The results also show that Ethereum is the only net transmitter to Bitcoin. However, on the other hand, we find that Augur has the lowest pairwises directional connectedness with LINK, XRP, and BTC, (C H LINK←REP = 1.17, C H XRP←REP = 1.38, and C H BTC←REP = 1.66). An interesting result emerges when analyzing the pairwise connectedness between the tokens and their platform compared to their connectedness with Bitcoin. We find that for three tokens (Maker, BAT and REP) out of four cases, Ethereum has the largest values of volatility transmission. These results can be explained by the fact that all these tokens have Ethereum as a platform.

The results of the two pairwise directional connectedness (the upper and lower off-diagonal connectedness table) can be used to calculate the individual connectedness known as the net pairwise directional connectedness. The results show that, in net terms, the directional connectedness take place from Ethereum to the eleven other cryptocurrencies, from Bitcoin to 10 cryptocurrencies (with Ethereum the exception), from Litecoin to 9 cryptocurrency (with Ethereum and Bitcoin as exceptions), and from EOS to 8 cryptocurrencies (except BTC, ETH and LTC). For the four tokens, they are almost net individual receivers. However, for the DASH and the three non-mining coins (XRP, XLM, and BNB), their net pairwise directional connectedness is almost negative with the mining submarket coin and positive with the token submarket.

In nutshell, the results of the off-diagonal pairwise directional connectedness show that BTC, Ethereum, LTC and EOS are almost net pairwise directional transmitter, that all the four tokens are almost net receiver, and that non-mining coins are net transmitter to the tokens but net receiver from mining coins. The results show also that Ethereum and Augur are the net pairwise directional transmitter and the net pairwise directional receiver in the sample, respectively.

The total directional connectedness "from others" and "to others" are defined as the sum of the row and column pairwise directional connectedness, respectively. The results of the total directional connectedness "from others" and "to others" are reported in the last column and the row TO ("to others") of Table 4 , respectively. While the sum of own connectedness with the total directional connectedness "from others" is set to be equal to 100%, the sum of own connectedness with total directional connectedness "to others" is not constrained to be equal to 100%, (e.g. TO = 110.63%).

The results show that mining coins are among the top five highest total connectedness "from others". In particular, we found that Ethereum has the largest value of "from others" total connectedess (C H ETH←• = 79.55%) followed by LTC and BTC. The lowest values are obtained by the four tokens with the lowest value is obtained for BAT (C H BAT←• = 18.91%). Concerning the total connectedness "to others" and similar to the results of total connectedness "from others", we found that the four mining coins are among the top five highest total connectedness "from others". Again, we found that Ethereum has the highest value (TO = 110.63%) and that ETH, BTC and LTC are ranked in the top. The results for the four tokens are also similar to the "from others" total connectedness. In particular, we find again that BAT has the lowest value (TO = 5.14%).

Moving to the net total connectedness which is defined by the simple difference between the total connectedness "to others" and "from others". The results are reported in the last row of Table 4 . The green color is used to indicate that the correspondning cryptocurrency has a positive net total conncetedness meaning that it is a net transmitter. The red color indicates that the corresponding cryptocurrency has a negative net total connectedness (net receiver). The results show that Ethereum leads the way for the net transmitter cryptocurrencies with a positive total net connectedness of 32.21% (C H ETH = C H •←ETH -C H ETH←• = 111.76% -79.55% = 32.21%), followed by Bitcoin (C H BTC = 22.09%), Litecoin (C H LTC = 11.64%), and EOS (C H EOS = 6.78%). For the four tokens, the net total connectedness is found to be negative with the highest in absolute value is found for Augur, followed by Marker, BAT and ChairLink. The net total connectedness for DASH, Ripple, Binance, and Stellar are also negative but weak. Moreover, our results show that the net connectedness of BTC and ETH are higher than that obtained for conventional financial stock markets (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2012, 2014) .

The results show that the total connectedness index of the CC market is equal to 63.03% which is very high compared to some previous studies investigating the only case of cryptocurrency market or the case of mixed financial assets (digital and conventional). For instance, Koutmos (2018) find a TCI of 20% despite the use of 18 cryprocurrencies which expected to increase the level of TCI (see Diebold and Yilmaz (2014) ). Also, Ji et al. (2019a) find also a smaller value of TCI=32.9%. Comparing our TCI to the sutdy of Corbet et al. (2018) who find a TCI of 38%, our TCI is still higher while the results are well expected since the examined sample is a mixed sample that include both cryptocurruencies and conventional assets. foot_1

As noted by several studies, the static analysis reported in Section 4 is based on one single point estimate of connectedness measures, then it cannot be used for a time-varying analysis of the connectedness between different financial assets (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2014, 2015) . In our context, the static analysis will be unable to provide specific analysis on how the several and important internal and external shocks that have affected the crytopcurrency market foot_2 will affect the different measures of connectedness. Consequently, for a better understanding of the dynamic connectedness, we use a rolling sample approach (dynamic connectedness) with 200-days rolling-sample windows and 10-day-ahead forecast to estimate the time series of all the connectedness measures described in Section 3.1. To save space and similar to most previous studies, we focus our analysis on the results of the total connectedness indices, the net pairwise directional connectedness, the pairwise directional connectedness "to others", and the pairwise directional connectedness "from others", plotted in Figs. 3, 6, 4, and 5, respectively. Fig. 3 reports the total connectedness measure when using the GK volatility measure for the whole sample (12 cryptocurrencies), for the mining coins, the non-mining-coins and the tokens. The results show that the total connectedness index os awfully volatile and is evolving overtime. It varies between 43% (reached in October 20th 2018) and 88% (reached in March 08th 2018 and March 15-20th 2020). Fig. 3 shows also that between the end of October 2018 and March 2020, the index show an increasing upward trend. By analyzing the total market capitalization of the cryptocurrency market, we note that the latter two dates coincides with some specific important negative events that have marked the cryptocurrency market. The first huge fall in the cryptocurrency market capitalization of March 08th 2018 is viewed as a continuous drop in the cryptocurrency market after the unprecedented bull of end 2017-beginning 2018. The second period of high connectedness index correspond to the recent stock market crash of mid-March 2020. During this period, most of stock markets around the word have largely dropped. In the Black Monday II (March 16), the Asia-Pacific and European stock markets closed down by about 9.7%. In north America, the Dow Jones Industrial Average, the NASDAQ Composite, and the S&P 500 all fell by approximately 13%. In the commodity market, oil prices fell by 10% and the Cboe volatility index closed with the highest ever closing value at 82.69.

To go further deep in the analysis of the dynamic directional connectendess, we report in Figs. 4-6 the results of the ranking of the directional dynamic connectedness "to others" and "from others" using the heatmap tool, and the total net dynamic directional volatility connectedness.

First, we use the heatmap tool to rank the twelve cryptocurrencies based on their directional connectedness "to others" and "from others". The results are reported in Figs. 4 and 5 for the GK volatility measures. The results reported in Figs. 4 and 5 show the ranking which is presented by different colors ranging from blue to red when moving from 0 to 12 values. The results reported in Fig. 4 clearly show that the level of "connectedness to" is the highest for the mining coins, followed by the non-mining coins and tokens. For the 4 tokens, we observe that the level of "connectedness to" is very low (smaller than 2) for almost all the period of study, except for BAT during the first three month and March 2020. The results of the analysis by coin show that bitcoin has the highest values of directional "connectedness to" is observed during the period from November 2018 to mid-May 2019 and then during March 2020 when the level of directional connectedness reaches high maximum. However, for the Ethereum cryptocurrency, the results show that the level of directional "connectedness to" is overall high for the whole period of analysis except December 2018-January 2019. Similar to BTC, we find that the highest value of the level of directional "connectedness to" is found for March 2020. A similar conclusion, regarding March 2020, is observed for all the cryptocurrencies, which can be explained by the effect of the recent COVID-19 pandemic crisis and the effects of the oil prices War between Saudi Arabia and Russia. For the direction "connectedness to others", the results reported in Fig. 5 show that the level of connectedness is overall medium, ranging between 5 and 7.5 except for XRP and BNB during few months. Similar to the directional "connectedness to", we find that, for tokens, the directional "connectdeness to" others is very low and range between 0 and 4 for almost all the period of analysis.

Finally, Fig. 6 presents the total net directional dynamic connectendness along with its descriptive statistics (mean, median,  maximum, minimum, standard deviation, Skewness and kurtosis). Three important conclusions can be drawn from the time varying net directional connectendess analysis. First, we find that it is almost positive for the three mining cryptocurrency Bitcoin, Ethereum and Litecoin. Second, it fluctuates from positive net directional connectedness to negative directional connectedness for six cryptocurrencies (the four mining coins, DASH and Chainlink). Finally, it is almost negative for the rest three tokens (Maker, Basic Attention Token, and Augur). In addition, similar to our static conclusion, we find that only Ethereum and Augur have positive and negative net spillover respectively, during the whole sample period of our analysis.

In addition, the descriptive statistics results reported in the same figure show that four out of the twelve cryptocurrency are characterized by a positive mean and that the rest of the eight cryptocurrencies are characterized by a negative mean. However, we find that seven out of the cryptocurrencies have a positive median indicating that the magnitude of the negative values in absolute value are more important than the positive ones. Finally, based on the Skweness and Kurtosis values, the normal distroibution cannot be accepted for ten out of the twelve cryptocurrencies where DASH and Chainlink are the only two exceptions.

The analysis of factors explaining the dynamic connectedness between the different studied cryptocurrencies is particularly important for portfolio managers, investors and academic scholars willing to reduce the financial risks and maximizing returns of their portfolio. For this purpose, we first introduce in Section 4.4.1 the linear regression model used to explain the net directional and total connectedness index. Then, in a second subsection, we analyze the obtained results from estimating the different proposed models. For all the estimated models (for net directional and total connectedness index), we use only the trading volumes of the main cryptocurrencies (mining and non-mining coins).

The linear regression model proposed to investigate the key factors affecting the net directional and total connectedness index was developed based on the existing literature (Balcilar et al., 2017; Bouri et al., 2017; Ji et al., 2019b) . Traditional macroeconomic and financial factors (such as global finance factors and major commodities) in addition to measures of economic and financial uncertainty are considered as the main factors affecting the levels of the connectedness across cryptocurrencies.

In this model, the Connect t variables are as estimated in Section 4.3. The variables EI t and GBI t are employed to explore the impacts of major commodities on the two measures of connectedness. The Volume t variables are collected from https://coinmarketcap.com/ . Data about MSCI and VIX are retrieved from https://markets.cboe.com/ and from https://www.quandl.com/ for GBI and EI.

To assess the determinants of the total connectedness, we estimated two models. In the first model, we consider only the trading volumes of the eight cryptocurrencies as explanatory variables. In the second model, we include other additional variables that reflect the global finance policy uncertainties and major commodities factors as cryptocurrency external potential explanatory variables. Table 5 reports the results of the OLS regressions for the total connectedness obtained in Section 4.4.1. The results show that the coefficients associated to the trading volume of all cryptocurrencies are highly significant, e.g. at 1% significance level. Accurately, the coefficients are positive for BTC, ETH, XLM, BNB and EOS and negative for only three cryptocurrencies, namely LTC, DASH and XRP. These results are in accordance with those obtained in Table 4 where BTC, ETH and EOS are found to be net transmitters of volatility to the studied cryptocurrency. The results are confirmed for all the estimated models even after adding the economic and financial factors. Our findings are consistent with Balcilar et al. (2017) and Ji et al. (2019a) regarding the strength of the relationship between the trading volumes and the total connectedness in the cryptocurrency market. The result of the second model, reported in the third column of Table 5 , provides additional evidences on the factors explaining the total connectedness in the cryptocurrency market. First, the results reveal a significant positive relationship between the volatility index (VIX) and the total connectedness. This finding indicates that the increase of uncertainty in the US conventional stock market increases the volatility connectedness in the digital assets. For this later result, a possible explanation is that investors tend to switch into the cryptocurrency market to hedge any possible stock market loss inducing a higher total connectedness between several digital assets in periods of high uncertainty in the conventional stock market (see Bouri et al. (2017) ).

In addition, our results highlight a significant positive impact of the global financial factor proxied by the MSCI. This positive relationship between the total connectedness and the conventional financial market can be explained by the competition that exists between the two types of markets. When investors are expecting to generate profits on the conventional financial markets, the number of transactions and liquidity will diminish on the cryptocurrency market increasing risks in this particular market. Consequently, the growing risk will induce an increase of the total connectedness in the cryptocurrency market.

For further analysis of the determinants of the connectedness between mining, non-mining cryptocurrencies and tokens, we proposed to analyze also factors affecting the dynamic net directional connectedness of each studied cryptocurrency using the same economic and financial factors as for the case of total connectedness index.

Table 6 provides the results of the OLS regressions for the net connectedness acquired using the Diebold and Yilmaz (2014) approach on the GK ′ s volatility measure. As regard to trading volumes, the results show that all net connectedness of the mining cryptocurrencies are influenced by their own trading volumes, except for bitcoin. For the non-mining coins, the own trading volumes affect BNB and EOS but not XRP and XLM. In addition, for all the studied tokens we find a significant relationship between the net connectedness and the own trading volumes, except for REP. More interestingly, our findings show that the relationship between the net connectedness and the own trading volumes are negative for the mainstream cryptocurrencies. Consequently, the decline of the own trading volume tends to increase the net connectedness of a particular cryptocurrency with others. In addition, our results show that trading volumes of BTC and ETH significantly affect the net connectedness of almost all the cryptocurrencies, especially tokens. It is important to remind that these two cryptocurrencies, BTC and ETH, are found to be the leading transmitter of volatility spillover in the previous section. Thus, being transmitter of volatility potentially explains the association between the trading volumes and the net connectedness. Further, Ethereum as the platform for several studied cryptocurrencies, especially for tokens, significantly influences the net spillover via its trading volumes. It is also important to note that the magnitude of the effect of mining coins trading volumes is mostly more important on the net spillovers of mining and non-mining coins than that of the net spillovers of tokens. As illustration, the increase of trading volumes of BTC by one-unit increases the net connectedness of ETH (mining) by 2.256 units; however, for the similar variation in BTC' trading volumes, the net connectedness of MKR (Token) rises only by 0.868 units. Regarding the economic and financial fundamentals variables, the results reveal a significant positive relationship between the volatility index (VIX) and the net connectedness of BTC and all the tokens, except for REP. Accordingly, the increase of uncertainty in the US conventional stock market increases the volatility connectedness for BTC and most of Tokens. However, a negative association is revealed between VIX and the rest of cryptocurrencies. Moreover, the results reveal that VIX negatively influences the net connectedness of the mining and non-mining, except for BTC. Besides, our results highlight a significant positive impact of the global financial factor represented by (MSCI) on the net connectedness of the majority of the studied cryptocurrencies. This positive relationship supports the results obtained in the total connectedness regression. This confirms the hypothesis of the presence of competition between the conventional and digital financial markets. Concerning the energy index, one can think that it is expected to be positively associated with mining cryptocurrencies since the mining process is known to be highly energy consuming process (Corbet et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019) ). Yet, our results reject the presence of such association except for Litcoin. However, the results show significant association between the energy index and net connectedness of all tokens but it is mostly negative. Finally, the results reveal some significant relationship between Gold Bullion index and the net connectedness of the cryptocurrencies. Particularly, we find that gold prices have a negatively significant effect on the net connectedness of BTC. This finding is in line with the existing literature (see for instance Dyhrberg (2016) , Ji et al. (2019a) ). Indeed, bitcoin is qualified as a substitute to gold. Thus, an increase in gold will decrease the demand for BTC leading to a decrease in the net connectedness of the BTC with the other cryptocurrencies.

In addition to the results presented above, we implement several variations on our basic analysis, with an eye toward checking robustness by using: (i) a second measure of volatility as defined by Parkinson (1980) , (ii) different predictive horizon for the underlying variance decomposition, e.g. h = 5 and h = 15, and (iii) different rolling window width, by considering 100,150, 200, 250 and

The determinants of the Net connectedness for GK.  300 window sizes. To save space, we summarize in this subsection the main differences and we refer to Section 1 in the supplementary document for a detailed analysis of the robustness checking of the results.

i. The results of using the Parkinson (1980) measure of volatility show that overall, we reach the same conclusion as when using the GK volatility measure. For instance, there is no significant changes in the cryptocurrencies net transmitters and net receivers. Moreover, the results regarding factors determining the total and net connectedness do not show any changes in the sign and statistical significance of the regression coefficients associated to the trading volumes, and financial and macroeconomic variables. ii. Using the two additional variance decomposition forecast horizons, h = 5 and h = 15, the total connectedness for the entire studied cryptocurrency market are similar to those when using h = 10. foot_3 Interestingly, we find that all the the dates of switch and periods of peaks remained unchanged. On the other hand, by analysing the net directional connectedness of the twelve cryptocurrencies, the results show that there exist no significant changes neither in the values of the net spillovers (positive and negative) nor in the sign. The results also show that the order of cryptocurrencies net transmitter and net receiver have not been changed. iii. Using different rolling Windows, the results show that, except for some few cases, the sign of the net connectedness remain the same whatever the window size used and the volatility proxy considered. Further, the net transmitters and net receiver cryptocurrencies remain almost the same. The few exceptions cases are found for the cryptocurrencies that have a net spillovers that are close to zero which confirm the invariability of the results to changing the window size. Regarding the total connectedness index, the results show that it decreases slightly with the window size. However, we believe that a choice of a rolling window size of 200 seems to be the optimal because it provides a compromise between the possible biases of the results that can occur when using a small window size (100 and 150) and the underestimation/overestimation of connectedness measures due to the use of a large sizes (250 and 300).

This paper contributes to the empirical literature on the financial properties of the cryptocurrency market by investigating the dynamic volatility connectedness between twelve important cryptocurrencies. To the best of our knowledge, this is the unique study where the analysis take into consideration the cryptocurrencies class (e.g. the mining coins and the non-mining coins versus tokens). In addition, our analysis examines whether internal factors to the cryptocurrency market, and macroeconomic and financial factors (Oil prices, Gold, MSCI and VIX) can be helpful to predict the time varying behavior of the total volatility connectedness and net pairwise connectedness.

Applying the Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2014) connectedness framework on twelve cryptocurrencies over a period of more than 2 years which include the major events of high price fluctuation in the cryptocurrencies market, our findings show evidence for several important new results. First, unlike most of previous studies, we find that the cryptocurrency market is highly inter-connected with an average total connectedness, index over the whole period of study, equal to 66.32%. In particular and as expected, we find that the mining coins dominate the cryptocurrency market, where the mining Ethereum coin dominates all the other coins and tokens. The results show that,in our sample, the Ethereum coin is the only coin that has a positive net pairwise directional spillover (net pairwise transmitter). Precisely, we find that the mining coins are typically net transmitter of shocks except for DASH, and that the non-mining coins and tokens are net receivers except for the case of EOS.The results also show that, for the non-mining coins and tokens, the highest bidirectional connectedness is found between ripple and stellar coins. Second, the results provide some evidence on the important role played by the tokens platforms (for example Ethereum in our case) in influencing the bilateral connectedness levels. Finally, regarding the intra-component analysis of connectedness, the results show that the mining coins sub-market has the highest level of connectedness index, followed by non-mining coins and finally tokens.

Moreover, in this study, for a better understanding of the dynamic volatility connectedness and their possible explanations, we proposed in a second step to analyze the determinants of the total and net connectedness indices. Our results reveal that the trading volume of the mining and non-mining coins is a key determinant of the total connectedness spillover index. In particular, the results show that the trading volume of the coins that are found to be net transmitter in the first stage of analysis are positively related to the total connectedness index. In contrast, the trading volume of the LTC, DASH and XRP coins are found to negatively determine the total connectedness index. Regarding the external cryptocurrency market factors, the results show that the volatility index (VIX) increases the level of total connectedness in the cryptocurrency market. A similar positive and significant impact is found for the case of the MSCI index.

Going deeper in the analysis, the results of investigating factors explaining the net connectedness show that in addition to its own trading volume, the net spillover of each cryptocurrency is determined by the trading volumes of Bitcoin and Ethereum. For the financial conventional asset factors, our results reveal that the net spillover of the mining and non-mining coins is negatively affected by the volatility index (VIX) (except BCT) but it positively affects the tokens. Further, we found that the hypothesis of positive association between the energy index and mining cryptocurrencies is highly rejected except for LTC. Regarding the gold prices, a significant negative impact of gold on the net spillover is found for only few net connectedness series. In particular, a negative relationship is found for the case of BTC.

The empirical findings in this study have several important implications on the risk management and diversification strategies of both traditional financial and cryptocurrency investors. For instance, the results related to the analysis of volatility connectedness (intra-cryptocurrency analysis) and the results of factors predictions of total and net volatility indices (external factors to the cryptocurrency market) can help market participants to identify the set of cryptocurrencies and the macroeconomic and financial factors to include in their portfolio to better manage the level of risk.

In fact, the low level of connectedness between non-mining coins and tokens can be interpreted as evidence for possible opportunity for portfolio diversification for cryptocurrency inverstors (intra-cryptocurrency market). By diversifying their portfolios between different cryptocurrency categories, cryptocurrency investors can reduce the risk of their portfolio while maintaining or improving the level of their portfolios returns. However, an important key observation that portfolio mangers and investors should consider is that mining coins shocks have substantial impact globally, with increased volatility leading to substantial global cryptocurrencies market losses. Hence, more attention should be paid to this market. Dynamically, we note that time-varying connectedness can imply timevarying diversification opportunities (Charfeddine and Benlagha, 2016; Fleming et al., 2001 Fleming et al., , 2003) ) On the other hand, the results related to macroeconomic and financial factors predictors of the total and net connectedness indices offer several financial diversification. In fact the results show that in many cases the macroeconomic and financial factors cannot help to explain the evolving behavior of total and net connectedness which can be viewed by financial analyst and financial investors as a mean for risk management improvement.

We quantitatively describe the main events that led to the Terra project's failure in May 2022. We first review, in a systematic way, news from heterogeneous social media sources; we discuss the fragility of the Terra project and its vicious dependence on the Anchor protocol. We hence identify the crash's trigger events, analysing hourly and transaction data for Bitcoin, Luna, and TerraUSD. Finally, using state-of-the-art techniques from network science, we study the evolution of dependency structures for 61 highly capitalised cryptocurrencies during the down-market and we also highlight the absence of herding behaviour analysing cross-sectional absolute deviation of returns.

In one of his public declarations, Terra project's co-founder, Do Kwon, stated that 95% of the companies entering the crypto market were going to die. Indeed, he further affirmed that there was entertainment in watching companies die (Quiroz-Gutierrez, 2022) . About a week after this statement, TerraUSD (UST), the stablecoin that, according to its founders, should have been the new peer-to-peer cash system, lost its peg to the U.S. dollar (USD) and collapsed.

Before its crash, UST was the fourth-largest stablecoin behind Tether (USDT), USD Coin (USDC) and Binance USD (BUSD), with $18 billion in market capitalization (CoinMarketCap, 2022a) . According to Terra's white paper, its underlying protocol relied on a two-coin system, which was not fully backed by traditional collaterals (e.g. fiat currency or gold) (Kereiakes et al., 2019) . On the one hand, Terra was the algorithmic stablecoin whose value was pegged to different fiat currencies, giving rise to fiat-based stablecoins, such as TerraUSD, TerraEUR and TerraKRW. On the other hand, Luna token 1 (LUNA) was the counterweight used to delete (or, at least, reduce) the volatility from UST. Being more specific, LUNA-UST protocol was based on two main concepts. First, the protocol stabilised UST prices by ensuring that its supply and demand were in equilibrium through arbitrage, that is, contracting (or expanding) UST pool by using LUNA pool as counterweigth. Second, through the Terra protocol's algorithmic market module, arbitrageurs were allowed to trade $1 worth of LUNA for 1 UST, and vice versa, regardless of LUNA and UST prices (Shapovalov et al., 2022) 2 .

The most famous project born inside the Terra ecosystem was the Anchor protocol, a lending and borrowing protocol used by UST holders as an high-interest savings account. Before Terra project's collapse, Anchor attracted the 75% of UST circulating supply offering 20% annual percentage yield (APY) for depositors. Aided by its vicious dependence on the Anchor protocol, Terra project immediately appeared to be heavily exposed to extreme market conditions. On April 2022, Swiss-based crypto exchange, SwissBorg, stated that if LUNA's price is under pressure, UST holders could be fearing that the UST peg is at risk and decide to redeem their UST positions. In order to do so, UST is burnt and LUNA is minted and sold on the market. This would exacerbate further the decline of LUNA's price, pushing more UST holders to sell their UST. This vicious cycle is known as "bank run" or "death spiral" (Bonato, 2022) . This "death spiral" effectively occurred to LUNA-UST on May 2022. Social media sources relates its origin to a "coordinated attack", in which market actors strategically used their capital to destabilise the UST peg and generate profits (Morris, 2022) . Specifically, two main events are identified as the fuse for the Terra collapse. First, private market actors short sold Bitcoin (BTC) with the final aim of spreading panic into the market (Hall, 2022; Ashmore, 2022; Locke, 2022) . Second, on 07 May 2022, the liquidity pool Curve-3pool suffered a "liquidity pool attack", which caused the first UST de-pegging, below $0.99 (Ashmore, 2022; Chainanalysis, 2022) . It is worth noting that, on 01 April 2022, Kwon (2022b) announced the launch of a new liquidity pool (4pool) together with DeFi majors Frax Finance and Redacted Cartel. Consequently, 1 Some coin-ranking sites, such as CoinMarketCap and Coingecko, use Terra and TerraUSD for the acronyms LUNA and UST. In this paper, to avoid any misconception, we denote Terra as the fiat-based stable coin and Luna as the counterpart.

2 When UST price was traded below $1 (e.g. $0.99), it implied that supply for UST was higher than demand, thus, it was necessary to contract Terra pool. To rebalance supply and demand, arbitrageurs would have acquired 1 UST for $0.99. Afterwards, they would have swapped 1 UST for $1 of LUNA through the market module, obtaining the corresponding profit (e.g. $0.01). Consequently, 1 UST would have been burned (decreasing its supply and contracting UST pool) and $1 of LUNA would have been minted (increasing its supply and expanding LUNA pool). The arbitrage procedure would have continued, giving rise to an upward pressure on the UST price, due to its continuous supply decrease, until it would have reached the USD peg.

funds were expected to move to the new pool, giving rise to a transitory situation in which Curve-3pool would have been more illiquid and prone to attacks. After the aforementioned "liquidity pool attack", since 08 May 2022, Luna Foundation Guard (2022) (LFG) started to trade its reserves to restore the USD peg foot_0 . As a consequence of this, they were able to obtain some stability around $0.995 until 09 May 2022. On this day, due to the strong and iterated selling pressure, UST lost its peg to USD for the second and last time. On 10 May 2022, LFG sold additional reserves to defend the peg, without success (see Luna Foundation Guard, 2022) foot_1 . Terra reserves included a large amount of BTC, thus, according to social media rumors, attackers could have forced LFG to sell BTC on the market, decreasing its price, and increasing the attackers' profit, whose strategy relied on short selling positions against BTC (Locke, 2022) . To save the Terra project, on 11 May 2022 10:10am (UTC), Terra Team announced the endorsement of the community proposal 1164 (Kwon, 2022a) , allowing a more efficient mechanism to burn UST and mint LUNA 5 . Regardless of this, community's trust on the project was already lost, giving rise to the complete collapse of both tokens. LUNA and UST dropped from a value of $87 and $1, on 05 May 2022, to less than $0.00005 and $0.2, on 13 May 2022, respectively. Profits for potential attackers are estimated to be over 800 millions (Locke, 2022) . On this point, it is worth noting that the "coordinated attack", using BTC to destabilise UST peg, might not have happened at all, since claims of its existence are based on contradictory social media rumors (del Castillo, 2022) . In other words, the attack against Terra, which started with a "liquidity pool attack" on Curve-3pool, and the potential existence of short selling positions against BTC, could have been two unrelated operations executed by different market actors.

The failure of Terra project represents an additional proof of the intrinsic fragility of algorithmic stablecoins which became already evident after the first large-scale crypto "bank run" suffered by Iron Finance in June 2021. Also Iron Finance was a two-coin system, based on an algorithmic stablecoin (IRON) and on a token counterpart (TITAN), which collapsed after the cascade selloff of TITAN due to a significant selling pressure by some "whales" (Iron Finance, 2021a).

Indeed, the strategy used to bring down Iron Finance was very similar to the one potentially observable in the LUNA-UST case. More specifically, Iron Finance suffered from a "liquidity pool attack", losing the peg to USD for a short time. Hours later, unknown market actors increased the selling pressure to spread panic, giving rise to the collapse of both IRON and TITAN (Finematics, 2021) . Both projects (Iron Finance and Terra) shared a very similar blockchain framework, being prone to the same type of attacks. This duality shows that algorithmic stablecoins are prone to failure due to two main reasons. First, as stated by Clements (2021) , they are built on the fragile foundation of relying on uncertain historical variables: they need a support level of baseline demand, they need participation of willing arbitrageurs, and they need an environment of informational efficiency. None of these factors are certain, and all of them have proven to be highly tenuous in the context of financial crises or periods of extreme volatility. Second, to survive a crash, algorithmic stablecoins need an intrinsic worth, i.e. there should be "genuine economic transactions" reliably performed with their tokens (Calcaterra et al., 2020) . This was not the case of Terra, whose stablecoin was mainly used to obtain high interests through the Anchor protocol. On the contrary, history demonstrates that traditional fiat currencies are generally able to survive similar kind of attacks (e.g. Sterling attack in 1992, see Fratianni and Artis, 1996) , since they have an intrinsic value as mean of exchange or storing value.

As an additional cause of reflection, we remark how, nowadays, the role of stablecoins has been only marginally studied by the scientific community. There are only few studies on stablecoins' stability (Grobys et al., 2021) , their role in portfolio diversification (Wang et al., 2020; Baur and Hoang, 2021) and crypto asset price formation (Barucci et al., 2022; Kristoufek, 2022) . To the best of our knowledge, the Terra-Luna case has not been addressed in the cryptocurrency literature. In this paper, we provide the scientific community with the first insights on the Terra project's failure by analysing hourly prices and transaction data through the instruments of network science and herding analysis.

We use hourly closing price data from the CryptoCompare database (CryptoCompare, 2022) for 61 cryptocurrencies from 01 May 2022 00:00 to 16 May 2022 23:00 (UTC) foot_3 . This widely used proprietary data source excludes exchanges containing spurious volumes (see Alexander and Dakos, 2020 and Vidal-Tomás, 2022) , ensuring a high standard of data quality. Specifically, we use data from the Kraken digital currency exchange (the 4 th largest exchange for traded volume according to CoinMarketCap, 2022b). (a) 05 May 2022 12:00, a strong and persistent selling pressure was evident on BTC and LUNA, with negative hourly log-returns foot_4 at 13:00, 14:00 and 15:00, as reported in Table 1 . On (b) 07 May 2022 22:00, UST lost its peg to USD for the first time as a consequence of the "liquidity pool attack" on Curve-3pool (Ashmore, 2022; Chainanalysis, 2022) . In response to this event, the LFG intervened, defending the UST peg and recovering the price to around $0.995. However, on (c) 09 May 2022 14:00, UST lost its peg for the second (and last) time, giving rise to the main decrease in both LUNA and UST prices. Finally, on (d) 11 May 10:00, Kwon (2022a) presented the last attempt to defend the peg endorsing the community proposal 1164. After a beneficial impact that led the UST price to 0.8$, this announcement was interpreted by the market as the signal for the expected death of the Terra ecosystem, giving rise to the final crash in the market. In order to analyse dependency structures in cryptocurrency market during Terra collapse, for each cryptocurrency i, we compute hourly log-returns r i,t . Moreover, to analyse herding behaviour, we also compute equally-weighted (EW) market returns (r m,t ). Table 2 reports descriptive statistics for LUNA, UST, BTC and the EW market, which shows the negative tendency of the market during this period.

To provide a complete overview of Terra project's collapse, we analyse public trades (i.e. transaction data) for BTC, LUNA and UST. Compared to hourly prices, which are retrieved from CryptoCompare database (CryptoCompare, 2022), this new dataset is directly sourced from Kraken digital currency exchange using CCXT (Ccxt, 2022) Python package.

We observe interesting features that could be related to some of the events introduced in Section 1. First, on 05 May 2022 (a), we identify a strong, positive imbalance for BTC, with a value comparable to those detected during the main collapse of LUNA and UST (i.e. from 09 to 11 May 2022 (c-d)). We do not observe comparable high positive imbalance values for LUNA and UST on the same date. Therefore, even though we cannot confirm that short selling positions were opened against BTC as described by different social media sources (Hall, 2022; Ashmore, 2022; Locke, 2022) , we remark the presence of a considerable selling pressure on this cryptoasset. In other words, if short selling positions were truly opened against BTC, 05 May 2022 should be the most plausible day in which this happened. foot_6 Second, focusing on the UST behaviour, we underline the existence of a remarkable selling pressure since 09 May 2022 (i.e. after the second UST de-pegging). This finding is particularly relevant, since the selling pressure after the first UST de-pegging (b) was remarkably lower than the second one, which could be in line with social media news when contending that attackers caused panic by selling whale-size UST holdings (Hall, 2022; Ashmore, 2022; Locke, 2022) . This strategy would be similar to the one used to bring down Iron Finance, given that attackers waited to the second de-pegging, taking advantage of the uncertainty that already dominated the market (Finematics, 2021) . Last but not least, it is relevant to emphasise that we cannot confirm the existence of a coordinated attack executed by related market agents. Indeed, the potential short selling against BTC and the Terra attack could have occurred at the same time by chance, in the context of a global economic uncertainty. In the case of Iron Finance collapse, no coordinated attack using a third cryptoasset was detected (Finematics, 2021) .

Anchor (Anchor, 2022) was a savings protocol designed to allow users from Terra ecosystem to (i) lend capital (UST) and (ii) borrow capital (UST) using bonded assets (bAssets, i.e. tokens representing ownership of an asset on a Proofof-Stake blockchain) as collateral (see Figure 3 ). Anchor protocol was at the base of the fast expansion of Terra project since it offered to its depositors up to 20% APY. This scheme was similar to the one used by Iron Finance, which offered more than 100% APY (Iron Finance, 2021b). 2020 ), Anchor's mechanism was originally designed to allow the borrower to pay less interest as the utilization ratio (borrowed deposits divided by total deposits) decreased, resulting in lower interests for the depositor. However, despite the decrease in the utilisation ratio, the deposit interest rate was kept around 20% since 2021 (Anchor Protocol, 2021; Jung, 2022) . In addition, the deposit interest rate was computed considering the yields of all bAssets used as collateral for borrowing the stablecoin, while its value did not increase significantly since January 2022. The unsustainability of the protocol is confirmed by capital infusions into Anchor's reserves in February 2022 (Kelly, 2022) and by recurrent requests from community members for the modification of the interest rates' mechanism (Anchor Forum, 2022) . Compared to fiat currencies, UST was mainly used for speculative purposes, which implied that it was more prone to extreme market events. Looking at Table 3 , we observe that, from 06 May 2022 to 11 May 2022, the protocol lost around 9.5 billions in UST, i.e. the 69% of the deposits.

To get more insights into Terra project's failure, we describe the evolution of dependency structures among cryptocurrencies during the crash. In order to do this, we use instruments provided by network science. Networks have been extensively used to study financial systems (Mantegna, 1999; Aste et al., 2010; Briola and Aste, 2022) modeling dependencies among assets through correlations. Different correlation measures capture different relationships among assets.

In order to partially mitigate such an effect, we assign a structure of weights to observational events, giving more relevance to the last observations of a given window. Weighted correlations are found to be smoother and recovering faster from market's turbulence than their unweighted counterparts, helping also to discriminate more effectively genuine from spurious correlations. Following the definition in Pozzi et al. (2012) , we define the Pearson correlation coefficient weighted with exponential smoothing as follows:

, ∀t ∈ {1, 2, . . . , ∆t} ∧ θ > 0 represents an exponentially smoothed weight structure such that ∆t t=1 w t = 1 and y w k = ∆t t=1 w t y k t . ∆t corresponds to rolling windows made of 24 hours with steps of 1 hour each and θ is here set to 0.3.

Such a definition can be used to build a range of networks representing dependency structures among cryptocurrencies (Briola and Aste, 2022 ). Here we use the state-of-the-art methodology, namely, Triangulated Maximally Filtered Graph (TMFG) (Massara et al., 2017) . Such a filtering network comes with several advantages compared to its alternatives. It is able to capture meaningful interactions among multiple assets and it is characterised by topological constraints which help to regularise for probabilistic modeling (Aste, 2022) . As a measure of network centrality, we compute the eigenvector centrality, which allows us to measure the influence of a crypto-asset in the system. Intuitively, a cryptocurrency has an higher eigenvector centrality as long as it is connected to other relevant cryptocurrencies, which are also characterised by an high eigenvector centrality. In order to highlight relevant events and reduce the impact of secondary ones, also in this case, we compute an exponentially smoothed rolling average of the signal with a smoothing factor equals to 0.3. 11

In this study, we also use the approach proposed by Chang et al. (2000) to study the possible emergence of herd behaviour during Terra collapse. This method is based on the notion of herding towards market consensus in which "herds are characterised by individuals who suppress their own beliefs and base their investment decisions solely on the collective actions of the market, even when they disagree with its predictions" (Christie and Huang, 1995) . More

where |r m,t | is the absolute term and r 2 m,t denotes the square of market returns. Chang et al. (2000) noted that rational asset-pricing models imply a linear relation between the dispersion in individual asset returns and the return on a market portfolio. As the market's absolute return grows, cross-sectional return dispersion would also be expected to rise, in a linear fashion. Conversely, during periods of euphoria or fear, investors may react in a more uniform manner, giving rise to herding behavior. This phenomenon will decrease the dispersion among returns or at least increase at a lessthan-proportional rate with the market return. Therefore, the emergence of herding behaviour will be consistent with a significantly negative coefficient β 2 in Eq.( 3 ). However, in the absence of herding, we should only observe a positive and linear relation between the cross-sectional return dispersion and absolute market returns, i.e. a significant positive coefficient β 1 , and a non-significant coefficient β 2 . In this line, given that Eq.( 3 ) represents the symmetric form that includes all the market return distribution, following Cui et al. (2019) and Chiang and Zheng (2010) we also divided market returns to distinguish asymmetric herding when the market is up or down,

Results  Therefore, from (c) to (d), we detect the specific period when most of the market reacted to Terra collapse, coinciding with the main decrease in UST and LUNA prices (see Figure 1 ). Afterwards, we observe a decrease in correlations in LUNA, UST, BTC and Kraken digital currency exchange. This new scenario shows that the first two cryptocurrencies were "excluded" from the system given their "death", while the system seems to recover a lower and more stable degree of correlation. After obtaining an initial picture through the study of market correlations, we compute the TMFG to obtain a better description of the system's collective dynamics. Figure 5 reports the evolution of TMFG's eigenvector centrality, through 24 hour rolling windows, for LUNA, UST and BTC. From (a) 05 May 2022 12:00 to (b) 07 May 2022 22:00, we observe that BTC was influencing the rest of the system, given its higher eigenvector centrality. This finding potentially supports the hypothesis of its involvement during the initial down-market since 05 May 2022 12:00. On (b) 07 May 2022 22:00, we identify the effect of the first Terra de-pegging on the cryptocurrency market's dependency structure, since LUNA became part of the core of the network, influencing the cryptocurrency market for a short time (see Figure 6 ). Since that day, LUNA was excluded from the core of the network, gradually lowering its eigenvector centrality. This finding can be observed in the network reported in Figure 7 , during the second UST de-pegging on 09 May 2022 12:00 (c). Finally, after (d) 11 May 2022 10:00, LUNA was completely excluded from the system, being relegated in the lowest eigenvector percentile. foot_10 4 ) shows the results of the analysis of the herding behaviour in the cryptocurrency market during Terra collapse.

Interestingly, despite the fact that the cryptocurrency market is more interconnected over time (Aslanidis et al., 2021) and herding is traditionally found on periods of uncertainty (da Gama Silva et al., 2019), we do not observe herding during the Terra collapse. In particular, we report significant positive coefficients for |r m,t |, (1 -D)|r m,t |, and D|r m,t |, while we underline non-significant coefficients for r 2 m,t , (1 -D)r 2 m,t and Dr 2 m,t . foot_11 This result supports the existence of an heterogeneous performance in the cryptocurrency market during this period. In Table 5 we report average hourly returns from (b) 07 May 2022 22:00 to (d) 11 May 2022 10:00, where we observe that some cryptocurrencies, like BTC and ETH, were characterised by a "better" (less negative) performance, compared to the rest of the market, whose average was equal to -0.0031. Consequently, even though we observe higher Pearson correlation coefficients during the collapse [see Figure 4 ], UST-LUNA failure did not dramatically affected the market due to the absence of herding and the low reaction of BTC [see Figure 6 ]. We could conjecture that investors considered this event as a non-structural shock, which did not posed real risk to jeopardise the future of the cryptocurrency system.
