In addition to the cutoff, we included a buffer window at the end of both the training and testing sets, during which time we ignored new index events. We did this to give each index event a fair amount of time to see an outcome event before the end of the observation period. The buffer length was selected in conjunction with the restricted mean survival times (RMSTs) for each task. The RMST calculates the mean survival time of a dataset up to a specific point in time after the index event. When creating the classification task (see section 4), the RMSTs were calculated for each task for increasing durations until the RMSTs changed by less than five percent. We used the converged RMST values for the classification criteria. Since these values ended up being less than 30 days (see table 5 ), we chose a 30-day buffer for ignoring new index events in the train and test sets.

We implemented four traditional survival regression models: Cox Proportional Hazards [Cox (1972) ], Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) [Crowther et al. (2022) ], Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) [Friedman (2001) ], and XGBoost [Chen and Guestrin (2016) ]. The Cox model estimates hazard rates as a function of user and transaction features, while the AFT model predicts the log-transformed survival time under a Weibull distribution assumption. The GBM approach leverages boosting techniques to capture complex interactions. Finally, the XGBoost model applies an AFT objective to directly handle censored survival data.

We also implemented two deep-learning survival models: DeepSurv [Katzman et al. (2018) ] and DeepHit [Lee et al. (2018) ]. Over the past decade, numerous deep survival models have been introduced, leveraging state-of-the-art deep learning architectures such as feed-forward neural networks and Transformer Models [Wiegrebe et al. (2024) ]. Notably, there are Cox-based models like DeepSurv and discrete-time methods like DeepHit. Deep-Surv uses a feed-forward neural network to model the log-risk function within a traditional Cox regression framework. In contrast, DeepHit treats time as discrete and typically employs classification techniques, with outcomes being binary event indicators for each discrete time point or interval. We implemented these two models using previously published code and compared their results with the traditional models on our data.

The Concordance Index (C-index) evaluated the baseline survival prediction models. The C-index assesses the models' abilities to discriminate between individuals with different survival times. C-index values are better the closer they are to 1, and the lowest possible C-index is 0.5.

To assess which models performed best overall, we computed the mean Borda rank for each model as described in Pavão (2023) . First, we ranked the models on each dataset using the Borda ranking system, which assigns each candidate model a rank from 1 to n, where n is the number of candidates, based on their score. For example, since we have n = 6 models, for any one dataset, the model with the highest c-index is given a rank of 1, the second highest gets rank 2, etc. With models being ranked for each dataset, we computed the mean Borda rank for each model to estimate which models were the best across the whole benchmark. We sort the columns according to this ranking. Lower Borda ranks mean better performance.

We use the same method to rank the difficulty of the datasets. By ranking how well individual models performed across all datasets and subsequently averaging these rankings, we can compare the difficulty of each dataset for our models to learn. We sort the rows in order of increasing mean Borda rank to represent the increasing difficulty of the datasets. Since both the rows and columns are sorted by their mean Borda rank, which is dependent on the number of rows or columns, we divide the mean Borda ranks by the number of rows and columns, respectively, to have the same scale on each axis.

In fig. 3 , we summarize the performance of the six survival models on our datasets (table 7 in appendix A.3 gives the exact values). The rows and columns of the heatmap are sorted based on their mean Borda ranks.

The XGBoost model consistently achieved the highest C-index values and had the highest average C-index overall. This was followed closely by AFT. While the Cox model ranked third overall, it really only performed well on two datasets (withdraw-borrow and withdraw-repay). The rest of the results for the Cox model amount to little more than random guessing. The GBM model performed extremely poorly, consistently attaining c-index values near or below 0.3. Since a c-index of 0.5 indicates random guessing, it would seem that GBM is consistently learning something about the data to predict survival times in a manner even worse than random guessing.

The deep learning methods showed very poor performance. This highlights potential challenges in hyperparameter tuning and optimization and indicates the complexity inherent in applying deep learning to survival analysis tasks. The observed differences between linear and nonlinear models also emphasize the presence of complex nonlinear relationships within the data, underscoring the importance of careful feature selection, representation learning, and model choice.

Overall, these results emphasize the utility of traditional survival analysis methods such as XGBoost and AFT for predicting financial events in DeFi environments. The wide variability in C-index values across different tasks shows this suite of datasets' inherent difficulty and complexity. These results also indicate that, while existing methods can be effective, there remains significant room for improvement in survival modeling, especially with deep-learning models.

With the rows and columns arranged by mean Borda rankings, the heatmap shows the best-performing models on the left and the easiest-to-learn datasets on top. The models perform worse, and the datasets get harder as we move towards the bottom right. There are some interesting things to learn from this ranking. First, all four of the datasets that have withdrawal as the index event are ranked among the easiest six datasets. This feels a little bit counterintuitive since withdrawals involve pulling money out of the lending pool, and it does not feel like they set up an obvious next action for a user. Two of the more obviously meaningful datasets, deposit-withdraw and borrow-repay, are among the most difficult datasets to predict. These events could be easily interpreted as representing the time until a customer churns and the time until a loan repayment, respectively. The fact that they remain difficult to predict shows that user behavior is varied and based on a complex set of factors.

We converted the 16 survival tasks into a corresponding classification problem. Converting survival analysis into classification tasks has gained traction, particularly as deep learning models redefine survival modeling by enabling binary classification within fixed time intervals [Wiegrebe et al. (2024) ]. This approach allows neural networks and similar models to address the challenges of censored and high-dimensional data, which traditional survival methods, like the Cox Proportional Hazards model, often struggle with in complex Models on the left side performed better on average, and datasets towards the top of the heatmap were generally easier for models to learn.

datasets [Salerno and Li (2023) ]. Classification-based survival modeling has been applied effectively in fields such as finance and healthcare, where timely and interpretable predictions are critical [Lee et al. (2018) ]. We now describe our strategy for picking the fixed time interval for each task.

To structure survival data into a classification format, we used a Restricted Mean Survival Time (RMST) to determine a classification cutoff. RMST, which calculates the area under the survival curve up to a specific truncation time τ , provides an interpretable measure of expected time-to-event within a fixed period. This method is particularly valuable for high-dimensional datasets, where traditional survival models, such as the Cox Proportional Hazards model, may struggle with censoring and dimensionality [Wiegrebe et al. (2024) ; Salerno and Li (2023) ]. RMST was selected as it summarizes survival data without requiring the entire cohort to reach the event, offering a practical solution for heavily censored data. Unlike median survival, which depends on at least 50% of events occurring, RMST allows for summary statistics even with high censoring rates, making it a robust measure for our DeFi dataset. This approach aligns with the work of Uno et al [Uno et al. (2014) ], who recommend RMST for survival analysis tasks involving varying levels of censoring and complex event distributions [Uno et al. (2014) ]. RMST-based classification benchmark offers a standardized method for handling high-dimensional, censored survival data, crucial for evaluating model performance across complex financial datasets [Lee et al. (2018) ].

Table 5 shows the RMST value and the number of days τ used for each task. The RMST over τ days is used to convert survival times for each time to classes: class 1 if the survival time is ≤ RM ST and class 0 if the time to event is > RMST. Points that are censored in fewer than RMST days are dropped. The RMST is calculated by measuring the area under the estimated Kaplan-Meier survival curve up to τ days, so RMST(τ ) monotonically increases with τ . Thus, we want to pick a τ small enough to preserve data, but also large enough so that the Kaplan-Meier curve has flattened out. We calculated RMST(τ ) for increasing values of τ in one-day increments and stopped when the change in RMST(τ ) was less than 5%. Class 1 is then defined as events that see their outcome in less time than RMST(τ ). The RMST was calculated using the monotonic spline method, particularly useful for high-dimensional financial datasets, where complex survival patterns require precise modeling [Biometrics (2021) ]. Table 4 shows the number of records and percentage of class 1 data in each FinSurvival Classification task. We note that some tasks are extremely unbalanced, with class 1 rates as low as 0.2%

We evaluated a diverse set of popular classification methods to get a baseline. The models used include Logistic Regression [Tibshirani (1996) ], Decision Tree [Breiman et al. (1984) ], XGBoost [Chen and Guestrin (2016) ], and Elastic Net [Zou and Hastie (2005) ]. In addition to the four traditional models, we used two deep-learning models for classification: Deep-Hit [Lee et al. (2018) ] and a neural net model of our implementation. See the appendix for details of packages used and how models were tuned. To evaluate the performance of our classification models, we use the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC), which is a robust metric for assessing the discriminative capability of binary classification models even when datasets are imbalanced [Bradley (1997) ]. The AUC score quantifies a model's ability to correctly rank examples by their predicted event occurrence probability, independent of classification thresholds. An AUC score closer to 1 indicates strong discriminative power, whereas an AUC closer to 0.5 suggests performance is no better than random guessing. As in the survival prediction task, we also aggregated the results from all 16 datasets to quantify which models were the best in each metric. We used the mean performance across all datasets for each model and the mean Borda rank for the models across each dataset.

Due to some of the datasets being very imbalanced, we used the synthetic minority over-sampling technique (SMOTE [Chawla et al. (2002) ]) on some of our datasets. For any dataset with 15% or less of the data in Class 1 (see Table 4 ), if appropriate for the model being trained, we train two versions of the model: one with SMOTE applied to the training set and one without. We evaluate these two models' performance on a validation set taken from the training set and use the model with the better AUC for testing.

Figure 4 presents a performance comparison of the six classification models across sixteen index-outcome event combinations (table 8 in appendix A.3 gives the exact values). Each cell reports the AUC, capturing how effectively each model handles class imbalance and predictive precision.

Surprisingly, the linear methods of logistic regression and elastic net performed the best. They performed almost identically, with logistic regression just edging out elastic net in mean AUC across all the tasks. The deep learning models of DeepHit and our custom neural net also performed strongly, achieving comparable results to the linear models. In contrast to the results on the prediction task, XGBoost performed very poorly compared to the other models.

Overall, these classification results show that linear models perform well on this task for datasets and demonstrate the importance of model selection for different tasks. The deeplearning models show improved results compared to deep learning models on the prediction task, suggesting that deep learning models are easier to train for classification tasks versus survival prediction tasks. The collective results show that there are a variety of difficulties across these tasks as well.

As with the prediction results, the rows and columns are arranged according to the mean Borda rank of each respective axis. Similar to the prediction results, the two easiest datasets are withdraw-borrow and withdraw-deposit (although in the opposite order). For this task, though, deposit-withdraw and borrow-repay are significantly easier than they were for the prediction task, ranking in the top half in terms of difficulty. This is a significant jump, and interesting in that it suggests that while it can be very difficult to predict how long it will take for users to churn or repay loans, it can be much more feasible to threshold the duration and predict whether the event will happen before the threshold.

The FinSurvival benchmark underscores the challenges of modeling high-dimensional, heavily censored DeFi data. In survival prediction, traditional models such as XGBoost and AFT regression outperformed deep learning methods, with XGBoost delivering the highest discriminative power. XGBoost and AFT actually performed fairly well, and the deep learning methods exhibited quite poor performance. This could be due to poor tuning, but we spent significant time tuning these methods, trying to achieve comparable results, and were not able to do so. These results suggest that conventional approaches remain solid in complex financial settings despite recent deep learning advances, and that there is still a lot of progress to be made in deep-learning models for survival analysis.

For classification tasks using RMST-based thresholds, basic logistic regression and Elastic Net led in AUC score, and deep-learning methods demonstrated moderate performance. However, tree-based methods like XGBoost exhibited mixed or poor results. Although the deep learning models tested show promise, their performance may have been constrained by limited tuning. Further optimization could potentially improve their ability to capture the nuances of the dataset.

Our results demonstrate that the FinSurvival dataset is demanding and complex, offering a rigorous benchmark for evaluating and advancing AI survival models. This work highlights opportunities for improved deep learning strategies and hybrid approaches to better capture the intricate patterns inherent in large-scale financial survival data. Models on the left side performed better on average, and datasets towards the top of the heatmap were generally easier for models to learn.

Our FinSurvival benchmark and datasets fill a critical need for large-scale survival datasets in finance. Unlike traditional survival data limited to small samples or sensitive domains like medical records, our openly accessible DeFi-based transaction data offers millions of records covering loans, deposits, and liquidations. By capturing high censoring rates and complex user behaviors at scale, FinSurvival drives methodological innovation in deep learning for survival modeling. It can also be helpful for quantifying things like credit risks, repayment patterns, etc., in a transparent manner. The openness and scale of this benchmark fill in an important gap that should help improve the accuracy and modeling capabilities of AI methods for survival analysis and help extend survival research to new domains. While there are potential ethical, privacy, and fairness concerns associated with the release and use of traditional financial data, these are largely ameliorated in these DeFi sets. DeFi users inherently consent to make their account transactions public by engaging with a DeFi protocol on a public blockchain. There is no personal or private information associated with account IDs. Also, DeFi protocols such as Aave are inherently fair because every action of the protocol is encoded in published smart contracts that apply identically to all accounts as described by public data on the blockchain. This contrasts with the well-documented biases that have been found in traditional lending, e.g., red-lining in mortgage lending Yinger (2018) . The possibility that the survival models produced could result in unethical and fraudulent behavior is very small.

This suite of survival datasets represents only a small fraction of the transaction data available through DeFi protocols. Not only are there numerous other deployments of the Aave protocol on other blockchains that have far more transaction data than the Ethereum chain used for these datasets, but there are other lending protocols, and other DeFi protocol types like Decentralized Exchanges (DEXs) that generate huge amounts of transaction data for unique problems. Our data creation pipeline can easily be used to convert transaction data from all of these protocols into new, massive survival data to create additional use cases and further challenges for survival modeling.

One way these datasets could be expanded is by adding more features. An interesting avenue of expansion could be incorporating exogenous data such as stock prices and cryptocurrency prices over time, along with other data typically associated with price data, like trade volume, volatility, etc. These could be joined with our existing data based on the timestamp and may help models have more predictive power.

The features created for this work were hand-crafted based on domain knowledge and intuition as to what features might be useful for prediction. This process takes a long time and extensive domain knowledge. One way this data could be used better is through an automatic feature-creation process that leverages AI techniques such as BERT-style transformerbased models to auto-generate more interesting features based on the raw transaction data. Such a model could create embeddings for each index event that encode a user's entire transaction history and capture more mathematically complex relationships in the data.

One limitation of this work, as it currently stands, is that it does not take into account competing risks. Different outcome events can "compete" with one another as possible outcomes for the same index event. For instance, if a user's borrowed funds are liquidated, this competes with their ability to repay that loan, i.e., borrow. Our survival modeling pipeline can include competing events in creating datasets, but we have not included them in this analysis [Green et al. (2024) ]. Incorporating competing events in these analyses would help the models more accurately reflect the dynamics of user behavior within the underlying transaction data.

DeepSurv: The DeepSurv model was published by Katzman et al. (2018) and fits a neural network based on the partial likelihood from a Cox Proportional-Hazards model. Our implementation uses the deepsurv function from the survivalmodels package [Sonabend (2024) ]. After experimenting with various hyperparameter configurations based on experiments from the original paper, we followed its configuration from the "simulated linear experiment." We had difficulties training this model on our full datasets, so we used uniform random sampling to sample 40,000 rows from each training set and 20,000 rows from each testing set to fit and evaluate the model.

DeepHit: The DeepHit model was published by Lee et al. (2018) and fits a neural net based on the probability mass function of a discrete Cox model. Our implementation uses the deepHit function from the survivalmodels package [Sonabend (2024) ]. To train DeepHit, we used the following hyperparameter configuration (any omitted parameters use the package defaults):

All the classification models follow the same default data preprocessing as the prediction models unless otherwise noted. After the data has been transformed into the binary classification task, unless stated otherwise, the models use the following policy for applying Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) [Chawla et al. (2002) ]: If the training data has less than 15% of its data in class 1 (see table 4 for class 1 %s), then a validation set is created from the training data and two models are fit to the training data without the validation set: one model on the raw training data and one model on the training data with SMOTE applied. These models are then tested on the validation set to see whether SMOTE helped. The better of the two models is used to predict on the testing set. All combinations of model type and dataset for which SMOTE was used are indicated in table 8 with an asterisk (*).

Logistic Regression (LogReg): Logistic regression is a simple yet effective linear model that estimates the probability that a given input belongs to a particular class. It works by fitting a weighted sum of the input features to a sigmoid curve, which outputs values between 0 and 1 representing predicted probabilities. We use the glm function from the stats package [R Core Team (2024) ] for logistic regression.

Decision Tree: A decision tree splits the data into smaller and smaller subsets based on simple feature-based rules, eventually making predictions at the leaves of the tree. It is easy to interpret and can capture non-linear relationships, but may overfit if not pruned or regularized. We used the rpart function from the rpart package [Therneau and Atkinson (2023) ] for our decision tree implementation. We used a max depth of 30 and a minimum split of 20.

XGBoost: We used the same implementation of XGBoost as described above in the prediction models, but tuned it slightly differently. For the classification task, we use logloss as the evaluation metric. Our max depth is set to 6. We use eta=0.1, subsample=0.8, colsample bytree=0.8, and scale pos weight is based on the relative proportion of class 0 data to class 1 data in the training set. We train with 200 rounds and early stopping rounds set to 10.

Elastic Net: Elastic Net is a linear model that combines both Lasso (L1) and Ridge (L2) regularizations. It is useful when there are many correlated features, as it tends to select groups of them while also shrinking their magnitudes to avoid overfitting. We used the glmnet function from the glmnet package for our implementation [Friedman et al. (2010) ; Tay et al. (2023) ].

DeepHit: Our implementation of DeepHit for the classification problem uses Python and the reticulate package in R [Ushey et al. (2024) ]. This implementation is based on the code from the original paper, using the model for a binary classification task. We train on 10 epochs with a learning rate of 0.001.

Neural Net: Neural networks consist of multiple layers of interconnected nodes, or neurons, that apply non-linear transformations to the input data. They are highly flexible and can capture complex patterns, but require careful tuning and lots of data to avoid overfitting. We built our own relatively simple neural net architecture for this classification task. The model uses a feed-forward architecture implemented with Keras' Sequential API. It consists of an input layer accepting features of a specified dimension, two hidden layers, each with 64 units and ReLU activation, followed by batch normalization and a dropout layer with a 0.2 dropout rate. Then there's a custom transformation layer that applies a log(1 + x) operation to simulate a transformation relevant to risk modeling. Finally, there's an output layer with two neurons and softmax activation for binary classification. The model is compiled with the Adam optimizer, categorical cross-entropy loss, and gradient clipping to mitigate exploding gradients.

The first phase for creating this dataset was collecting the raw market transaction data from the Aave platform. The underlying transaction data upon which this data is based comes from The Graph 5 , a decentralized protocol for indexing and querying data from blockchains 5. thegraph.com The data for each transaction type is in its table, so we queried each type to get data for all transactions from November 30, 2020, through September 30, 2024. The transaction types we use in our dataset are Deposits, Withdrawals, Loans, Repayments, and Liquidations.

To get all pertinent information for each transaction, we also collect data from the ReserveParamsHistoryItems from the same time span. It provides timestamped information about reserves whenever they are used in a transaction within Aave. This is how we can get information about how much a reserve was worth and what its interest rates were at the time of each transaction.

Finally, we also collected data about the individual coins (the table is called Reserves) to get basic information about each reserve, such as its symbol, what functionality is available for it in Aave, and how many decimal places to adjust its numerical values by. With the information from all of these tables, we were able to create one unified and humanreadable view of the transaction data. We combined all of the transaction-type-specific tables, sorted them chronologically by their UNIX timestamp, and replaced IDs in many columns with more pertinent information so that each transaction could be comprehensible to a human. Information about the coins used in each transaction was added explicitly to each transaction record, so that at each transaction we can see the symbol of the coin(s) involved (e.g., BTC, ETH). The amounts of each currency being used in each transaction were adjusted by the currencies' specific decimal exponent, as well as their conversion factors to USD at each transaction time. Other time-dependent, currency-specific data was added to each transaction to increase the amount of information contained within one record.

The final structure of this transaction-level data is shown in table 9. This table does not include all the columns of the data, as there are too many features to include and many of them are transaction-type-specific. We also provide a table of metadata about these transactions in table 10. The Graph

Phase two of creating this data involved the creation of the pipeline to convert transaction data into survival data. Survival data, also known as time-to-event data, involves observations where the outcome of interest is the time until a specific event occurs. This type of data is characterized by two main components: the observed time T i , which is either the time until the event occurs or the time until the last follow-up (for censored data), and the event indicator δ i , which denotes whether the event has occurred (δ i = 1) or the observation is censored (δ i = 0). Additionally, survival data often includes a set of covariates X i that represent other variables which might influence the time-to-event. Mathematically, a survival dataset for n subjects is represented as {(T i , δ i , X i ) : i = 1, 2, . . . , n}. This structure enables the analysis of both the timing of events and the factors that affect these timings.

The motivating idea for how our DeFi transaction data can become survival data is that each transaction a user performs could be considered an "index event," triggering the start of a record which lasts until a future transaction of interest, which could be considered the "outcome event." For instance, if we want to track how long it takes for a user to pay off a loan of a certain currency, we could treat the transaction where a user borrows that currency as the index event and track that user until they make a repayment of that same currency. With this idea in mind, our pipeline for creating survival data needs the following parameters:

• Subjects: A specified set of one or more columns of the event data that define who/what the subjects will be that we track when creating the survival data. For instance, most of the time we want to see both the "user" and the "coin" columns, because we are interested in how an individual user interacts with a specific coin over time.

• Observation Period: A start and end date and time over which to compute the survival data. By default, this can be the entire duration of the events dataset, but could be set to a shorter time window for more targeted analysis.

With these parameters defined, we create the pipeline for survival data creation. Given all the parameters, we first filter out all events that do not occur within the specified observation period. Then we create a subset of the data that only includes the desired index events and another subset that only includes the desired outcome events. Grouping these subsets by the selected subjects, we then perform a rolling join on the index events with the outcome events, matching the subjects and using the first event after each index event. This creates a table where each row has information on an index event and the first outcome event performed by the same subject, if any, performed after the index event. With this, we can calculate the elapsed time between the two events, using the final time of the observation period as the outcome event time if no appropriate outcome event occurred.

Considering each of the five transaction types as possibilities for index and outcome events was the obvious way to go about this, but liquidation events need to be handled more carefully than the others due to their involving multiple parties. So, first we considered the "basic" transactions of borrows, repays, deposits, and withdraws. If we choose one of these transaction types as an index event (e.g., borrows) and another type as outcome events (e.g., repays), we can create survival data that answers a question like "How long do users take to repay after borrowing?" It is important to note that the choice of subjects here is both the user and the currency involved in the transaction, because if an index event shows a user borrowing e.g. Wrapped Bitcoin, an appropriate outcome event should be that same user repaying Bitcoin, not a different currency for which they might also have a loan. Putting these ideas together, we created 12/16 datasets using the different combinations of basic transactions as the index and outcome events.

We wanted to include survival datasets using liquidations. Liquidations can occur when a user's overall account in Aave has an "unhealthy" balance of deposited assets compared to borrowed assets. These assets can include a variety of different currencies. When a user's account is unhealthy, another user can perform a liquidation transaction, paying off a portion of the unhealthy user's loans to claim an equivalent portion of the user's deposited collateral assets and a small liquidation bonus from the protocol as an incentive. So, liquidation transactions include more than just one user. They include a "liquidator", which is the user who performs the liquidation transaction, and a "liquidatee" whose account is being liquidated. Additionally, a liquidation transaction can involve any one of a user's borrowed currencies as the "principal" currency, and any one of the user's deposited currencies as the "collateral" currency. Given all of this, we handle liquidations differently than the other transaction types. In our suite of datasets, we only consider the case when a user's account is liquidated. We use this event exclusively as an outcome event.

Similarly to user history features, market history features compute metrics for the entire Aave V2 Mainnet market. This is a useful way of approximating a market's relative supply and demand at a given point. For the entire blockchain, features track the number of each type of transaction, the average amount for each type of transaction, and the average and sum amounts of all currencies together in dollars and Ethereum. The full list of these features can be found in table 13 . Time features were computed for every observation in the raw data. The 'timestamp' variable represents the POSIX time (time since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 UTC) in seconds. Circular representations of these time features were also created to capture the cyclical nature of time, such as the closeness of 11:59 PM and 12:00 AM. This resulted in three additional features per time interval: the value, sine, and cosine. The full list of time features can be found in table 14 .

The lack of enforced trading termination in the cryptocurrency market allows for an exploration of the "natural death" of tradable assets. We propose two deep learning survival models to study this phenomenon and a posthoc interpretability algorithm to interpret the results and test the hypothesis. The proposed deep learning survival models outperform the time-dependent Cox regression in both prediction performance and interpretation flexibility. Our results indicate that lower trading volume, market capitalization, social media attention, and higher price volatility predict increased cryptocurrency "death" hazards.

Cryptocurrency technology has achieved remarkable success and attracted substantial developer engagement and investments. Cryptocurrencies are traded on specialized financial exchanges, but one noteworthy phenomenon in cryptocurrency exchanges, which is absent in the traditional stock exchanges, is the gradual cessation of trading (i.e., price and trading volume gradually fall to zero), signifying the "death" or failure of the cryptocurrencies. 1 Such trading discontinuation may occur during market downturns or when the related smart contract applications fail.

The failure of a cryptocurrency may seem comparable to the delisting events in the stock markets. However, there are notable differences. First, in a traditional stock exchange, listed companies must adhere to the standards set by the SEC and the exchanges; their shares will be delisted if these standards are not met [1] . However, due to the decentralized nature of cryptocurrency, regulatory authorities cannot effectively mandate a complete trading termination, although government enforcement actions may limit or discourage it. Therefore, the discontinuation of cryptocurrency trading is the result of a market equilibrium shaped by market dynamics and regulatory influences, rather than by direct regulatory orders. Second, in a traditional stock market, the decision to delist is usually publicly announced [2] , allowing investors to make necessary adjustments. Conversely, in the cryptocurrency market, there is no formal "death notice," and investors do not receive direct warnings before a cryptocurrency's failure. Thus, they must make investment adjustments using their private information. Third, the price of failed cryptocurrencies will eventually fall to zero, but delisted stocks may still be traded at positive prices privately [3] .

In the absence of direct trading termination orders, the cryptocurrency market presents a unique opportunity to examine the "natural death" of tradable assets resulting from market equilibrium. This paper investigates this phenomenon using various survival modeling approaches and compares their model interpretability and predictive performance. First, we estimate a Kaplan-Meier Survivor Function and a time-dependent Cox regression as the baseline. Second, we propose two deep learning survival models based on long short-term memory (LSTM) and transformer neural networks to address certain drawbacks of the Cox regression. Third, we propose a post-hoc interpretability algorithm to explore the lagged and nonlinear effects of the covariates using the trained deep learning survival models. The rationale of the interpretability algorithm is to compute the expected impulse response function and use it to show how perturbations that occur to certain covariates influence the "death" hazard over time. Last, we interpret the non-linear effect of the covariates on the next-day hazard using partial dependency plots of the deep learning survival models. In conclusion, our proposed deep learning survival models offer superior performance in predicting cryptocurrency "death" while also providing more comprehensive and flexible model interpretations, especially regarding the lagged and nonlinear effects of the covariates.

This study makes several important contributions. First, in the traditional stock market, delisting decisions are typically governed by rigid and predefined regulations. Firms failing to meet certain financial and operational standards will be forced to delist from the exchange [4] . Therefore, examining the effect of the financial or operational performance on delisting is pointless, as the delisting decision is deterministic due to regulations. Indeed, the literature mainly focuses on the effect of non-financial/non-operational factors on delisting, such as industry categories [5] , firm longevity [6] , and firm size [7] . Taking advantage of the unique and underregulated cryptocurrency market, this research examines the association between cryptocurrencies' market dynamics and their failure, free from external regulatory enforcement to terminate the trading. As a result, our research manages to investigate the life cycle of a tradable asset only under market equilibrium, which is an unexplored research gap. We analyze the fundamental motivations behind trading behaviors through the lens of market microstructure theory and portfolio theory, and then apply their frameworks to develop hypotheses regarding effective predictors for the cryptocurrency death.

Second, we propose a deep learning survival modeling framework to address two important drawbacks of the traditional time-dependent Cox regression: (1) lagged effects of covariates on the hazard rate are not captured (Bharath and Dittmar 2010) and ( 2 ) it makes strong assumptions about the underlying stochastic process (e.g., the log of the hazard is a linear function of covariates). These restrictions negatively impact the model's performance and interpretation flexibility [8] . To address these issues, we developed two deep learning survival models based on the LSTM network and the transformer network and applied them to the survival data. These models are designed to predict the next-day death hazard using the complete historical time series of the predictors. Such models not only allow past observations to influence future hazard rate but also make no assumptions about the form of the underlying stochastic process (i.e., both the form of the stochastic process and the parameters depend on the covariates).

Last, this paper contributes to the interpretable deep learning literature by introducing an interpretability algorithm based on the impulse response technique to analyze the lagged effects of covariates in deep learning survival models. Although neural network models could approximate roughly any functions given sufficient complexity [9, 10] , interpreting the model and understanding its decision-making process is difficult for humans due to varying model structures and over-parameterization [11, 12] . This study proposes an interpretability algorithm by emulating the neural network's data processing to create connections between inputs and outputs. Briefly speaking, we first introduce a small fixed perturbation (10 % of the standard deviation) to each covariate, one at a time, and then calculate and summarize its effect on the death hazard rate in the following days to assess the influence of each covariate. Using this procedure, we managed to compute the expected impulse response function (hereinafter IRF) for the deep learning survival models to evaluate how cryptocurrency death hazard responds to market shocks over time. This is the first time the impulse response technique is applied to survival analysis to produce model interpretations spanning a delayed period.

In a related effort, we also created partial dependency plots for the deep learning survival models to examine the nonlinear effects of covariates. We introduce varying perturbations to each covariate and visualize their average impact on the next-day death hazard. Traditionally, a researcher may only assess the linear effects of the covariates using the Cox model. However, the actual effects could be non-linear (e. g., concave increasing, convex increasing, bell-shaped), and relying on a generalized linear model may oversimplify the relationships and overlook important details. Our interpretability approach shows the potential of using deep learning models for hypothesis testing, an area typically dominated by interpretable linear models.

The rest of the paper proceeds as follows. Section 2 reviews the related literature and develops hypotheses. Section 3 describes the data collection procedures. Section 4 discusses the traditional and proposed survival analysis modeling techniques. Section 5 presents the prediction performance comparisons. Section 6 illustrates the proposed IRF-based interpretability algorithm. Finally, the paper concludes in Section 7.

Cox regression is a statistical technique used to model the time until an event occurs [13] . It is mainly used in medical and epidemiology research to understand the relationship between a set of covariates and the time until a particular event happens, such as death and disease recurrence [14] .

However, Cox regression has been increasingly used in social sciences. For example, Boršič and Kavkler use it to study the factors influencing the unemployment duration and find that higher education levels reduce the time to find jobs [15] . Agarwal explores the survival of new entrants into an industry and finds that entrants' survival rate declines with increased competitive intensity [16] . Sanizah et al. study the time to divorce decisions and find that the age at marriage, presence of children, couple's educational level, employment status, and household income are significant predictors [17] .

In a more relevant context, Cox regressions are also used in the finance literature to investigate the occurrence of significant financial events. For example, Eilnaz and Meziane study the decision of voluntary delisting and report that firms with high leverage, high insider ownership, and low profitability/growth opportunity/trading volume are more likely to voluntarily delist from stock exchanges [18] . Shumway uses Cox regression to forecast bankruptcy and finds that variables such as firms' market size, past stock returns, and idiosyncratic returns are important predictors. In contrast, many accounting-related variables are statistically unrelated to bankruptcy probability [19] . Maria and Lyn propose a Cox model-based credit scoring system that predicts early repayment and default for personal loans and demonstrates a competitive performance when compared to the industry standard classification approaches [20] .

Although Cox models are widely used in survival analysis across various fields, there are several noted limitations. First, because the Cox model does not have an inherent mechanism to capture inter-period dependencies, investigating the lagged effects of the covariates is difficult unless certain lagged or cumulative covariates are included in the model estimation [21] . Second, even though the inclusion of the lagged or cumulative covariates into the models may address the first issue, these covariates must be custom-designed by researchers and can be rather subjective. For example, studies may use distinct time lags and methodologies to compute the cumulative covariates, and the appropriateness of these measurements relies significantly on the researchers' domain knowledge [22] . Third, the model makes strong assumptions regarding the form of the underlying stochastic process (i.e., the logarithm of hazard is a linear combination of covariates), and nonlinearity is generally not considered in the model [8] . The second and third issues have become more prominent with the advancement of deep learning technologies. In this research, we propose a deep learning survival modeling framework that potentially addresses all three issues. These models employ LSTM and transformer neural network structures to inherently capture the inter-period effects of covariates [23] while eliminating the need for manual feature engineering and assumptions for the underlying stochastic process [24] .

Although there are several studies exploring deep learning-based survival analysis, each of them falls short in certain considerations [4] [5] [6] , for example: (1) the hazard rate or the outcome variable at time t depends on the covariates observed during the same time t period, it is thus less practical to use the model in prediction; (2) the model is not a time-dependent survival model; (3) there is no interpretability strategy proposed to obtain managerial insights; (4) there is no performance comparison between the deep learning survival model and the Cox regression; and (5) the loss function used in model training is not regularized to focus on "death" event prediction, limiting the model's performance. Details will be presented in later sections.

Deep learning algorithms started dominating prediction benchmarks around 2012 in many domains such as banking, retailing, healthcare, recommendation systems, and natural language processing [25] . In addition, deep learning allows machines to automatically discover relevant features and learn data representations that would otherwise be laborious and complex tasks for humans [11] . Such capability is achieved through increasingly complex hierarchies and overparameterization. However, one major drawback is that such models' rationale and decision-making process are usually unclear, reducing their capability to interpret and explain the underlying mechanisms [26] .

The lack of interpretability hinders the adoption of deep learning models because they are difficult to trust, especially in high-stakes applications such as healthcare and financial services [27, 28] . Research has found that deep learning models can be easily "fooled" to misclassify and generate unpredictable outputs in image processing [29] and natural language processing [30] . These undesirable behaviors sparked many recent studies to develop interpretability algorithms to gain users' trust and produce insights about the model's decision-making process [31] .

The type of interpretability algorithm that is most relevant to this particular research is commonly known as the post-hoc interpretability algorithm, in which a separate explaining model is created to explain the main model [11] . There are many existing interpretability algorithms falling under this category. For example, Mukund et al. proposed the "Integrated Gradient" method, which attributes the contribution of each input feature to a neural network's output by integrating gradients along a path from a baseline to the input [32] . Avanti et al. proposed the "DeepLIFT" method, which determines the contribution of each unit based on the difference between its activation value and the reference activation, whereas the reference activation is computed using all units during forward propagation [33] . Marco et al. proposed the "LIME" algorithm (local interpretable model-agnostic explanations). This methodology randomly samples data around inputs, computes their predictions using the black box model, and then trains a separate interpretable model on this perturbed dataset. The black box model is thus explained using the interpretable local model [34] . Scott and Su-in proposed the "SHAP" algorithm (Shapley Additive explanations), which assigns each feature an importance value for a model's prediction by computing fair contribution scores based on cooperative game theory [35] .

The proposed interpretability algorithm is created specifically to capture the lagged effect of covariates for longitudinal analysis. For survival analysis in particular, this method aims to estimate an IRF that depicts how perturbations to a covariate influence the hazard rate over time. The perturbations are introduced to each covariate separately, and their influence on the hazard rate is summarized to generate interpretations and insights.

Delisting from the exchange primarily occurs in stock markets and refers to the removal of a company's stock from an exchange [1] . There are two types of delisting: involuntary delisting and voluntary delisting [3] . Involuntary delisting occurs when a company is forced to delist from an exchange because of an SEC rule violation, failure to meet the exchange's requirements, merger and acquisition, bankruptcy, or migration to another exchange [36] . Voluntary delisting occurs when a company withdraws its stock listings by repurchasing its shares or going to over-the-counter Market [37] . For the two delisting types, research focuses are drastically different. Since the reasons for involuntary delisting are usually deterministic (i.e., violations of government or exchange regulations), most studies investigate the consequences of involuntary delisting, which are mainly equity value decline, decreased liquidity, and lower trading volume [1, 2, 38, 39] . In contrast, research on voluntary delisting primarily focuses on its reasons. According to the literature, companies voluntarily delist from stock exchanges mainly due to the inability to obtain enough funding to rebalance their leverage, limited growth opportunities, significant insider control, and high compliance costs (i.e., audit fees) [4, 18] .

However, no matter how a stock is delisted, it is either due to regulatory requirements or a decision made by management. But what does the "natural lifecycle" of a tradable asset look like? How does a tradable asset reach its "natural death" without enforcement? To the best of our knowledge, there is currently no answer in the existing literature. The cryptocurrency market offers an ideal research setting to address these questions because enforced trading termination is practically impossible for cryptocurrencies due to their decentralized nature. This research aims to investigate the predictive factors for cryptocurrency's "natural death" using survival analysis to shed light on this unexplored area of research.

To study why trading stops, we must first understand the motivations behind stock trading. According to market microstructure theory, the most prominent reasons for trading are the heterogeneous expectations about asset values [40] . Private information and asymmetric information lead to investors' unique insights and market evaluations, contributing to price discovery. Even in an efficient market without private information (e.g., all information is freely available), differences in information interpretation also create heterogeneous beliefs that motivate trades [41] . While according to the portfolio theory, investors trade to rebalance and adjust their assets in response to changes in wealth, risk tolerance, consumption needs, liquidity needs, and investment opportunities [42] , which lead to trading even in the absence of new market information. These theoretical frameworks provide a strong foundation for understanding trading halts in the cryptocurrency market, and with which we will develop our hypotheses.

The daily trading volume of a cryptocurrency refers to the amount of cryptocurrency exchanged during that day. A significant decrease in trading volume has several implications. First, it suggests that the investors barely have heterogeneous evaluations, indicating that the market may have already reached a consensus on the future of the cryptocurrency (i.e., death), which significantly reduces the number of interested buyers on the market. Second, a sharp decline in trading volume also suggests that the buying and selling of the cryptocurrency no longer address investors' portfolio rebalancing needs. This implies that investors see limited investment opportunities and do not expect the trading of the cryptocurrency to meet their consumption needs, liquidity needs, and risk adjustment goals, indicating that its value has already fallen significantly. Therefore, we propose a negative relationship between trading volume and cryptocurrency death hazard.

The business failure literature has demonstrated that higher market capitalization correlates with a lower probability of failure [5, 7, 43 ]. Yet, it remains unknown whether this holds for tradable assets as well. As discussed previously, investors have idiosyncratic investment needs and portfolio rebalancing needs [44] . A cryptocurrency market with a large market capitalization is expected to absorb these needs more effectively without affecting the stability and confidence in the market [45] . Conversely, in a low market capitalization market, large selling orders are more likely to create order imbalances, which are costly to absorb by the market makers. Such order imbalance may lead to large bid-ask spreads, significantly reduced prices, and even panic selling [46] . Therefore, we propose a negative relationship between market capitalization and cryptocurrency hazard.

The emergence of social media renovated retail investors' access to new information [47] . Previously, investors mainly relied on public disclosures such as quarterly earnings releases, analyst reports, and market news to aid their decision-making. As social media becomes prevalent, retail investors increasingly use social media messages to make trading decisions [47] . Social media users are motivated to share information with others due to many factors such as reputation, tenure in the field, and reciprocity [48] . Besides these non-economic motivations, economic motivations also exist, especially in investment communities. For example, information shared by a message poster could influence the viewers' trading decisions and expedite the convergence of market prices to the message poster's expectation [47] .

In light of these arguments, we expect social media attention to be an effective predictor for cryptocurrency failure. If a cryptocurrency receives minimal social media attention, it signifies a lack of investor interest. Sharing information about a diminishing cryptocurrency fails to contribute to the goals of improving reputation, establishing tenure in the field, or impressing peers. Therefore, social media users will be reluctant to post messages about diminishing cryptocurrencies, and we thus propose the third hypothesis.

Most cryptocurrencies are peer-to-peer cash/currency systems. But unlike fiat currencies, cryptocurrencies are usually characterized by high volatility [49] , indicating that speculation plays a major role in the cryptocurrency market rather than its application in trade settlement. For a currency to function effectively as a medium of exchange and a reliable store of value, it needs to be stable and non-volatile [50] . While some businesses, such as Apple and Best Buy, have started accepting cryptocurrency as payment for their products and services, the high volatility and the associated risks of cryptocurrencies significantly limit their adoption by small businesses [51] .

Excessive volatility is also undesirable for an investment asset because investors are presumably risk-averse and dislike volatility [52] . Studies have indicated that the risk premium of volatility tends to be mostly negative across various contexts [52, 53] . According to the portfolio theory, for a portfolio to remain on the "efficient frontier," high volatility must be compensated with high returns [54] . If a highly volatile cryptocurrency fails to outperform others in returns, investors will exchange it for other investment opportunities to optimize their portfolio, negatively influencing the prospect of the cryptocurrency and increasing the hazard.

We downloaded the daily cryptocurrency trading data of 8194 cryptocurrencies, including prices, trading volumes, and market capitalization from July 13, 2010, to June 30, 2024, from CoinMarketCap. We set the beginning of the sample period to be July 13, 2010, because it is the first day that CoinMarketCap provides data about Bitcoin. Coin-MarketCap is a website specializing in cryptocurrency trading data such as prices, trading volume, and market capitalization. According to SimilarWeb, a global website traffic analytics platform, coinmarketcap. com is ranked 865 globally in terms of website traffic (as of April 2025). CoinMarketCap is also the data source for many other cryptocurrencyrelated studies [55, 56] .

According to the classic survival analysis framework, a cryptocurrency either survived beyond the data collection period (i.e., censored by the dataset) or "died" before it. Given the lack of a formal definition in the literature, we define cryptocurrency failure as trading discontinuation. Formally, we consider a cryptocurrency failed or "dead" on day t 0 if the trading volume is zero across t 0 , t 1 , and t 2 . A failed cryptocurrency may resume trading in rare cases, but we consider only the first failure, to be consistent with the traditional survival analysis. Among all cryptocurrencies in the sample, some are barely traded and "die" too soon. We exclude these short-lived cryptocurrencies because cryptocurrency failure could be attributed to an unsuccessful Initial Coin Offering (i.e., unsuccessful project launching), but not to the factors of interest in this study. The influence of Initial Coin Offering is more likely to be factored into the price of those stable cryptocurrencies that have already been in existence for some time. After the screening, 2701 cryptocurrencies that survived >60 days remained in the sample. And their daily return, market capitalization, trading volume, and volatility are obtained.

To capture each cryptocurrency's social media attention, we obtain social media data from Bitcointalk.org, one of the most popular online communities for cryptocurrency investors. Many cryptocurrency studies have used this online community as the primary data source [47, 57, 58] . This online community provides a message board dedicated to "altcoin" social media discussions (altcoin refers to cryptocurrencies other than Bitcoin), and we use this message board as the data source to obtain social media data. We matched each cryptocurrency with its social media messages using the appearance of its ticker symbol in the messages.

Besides social media, we use Google Trend data to control for popularity. Specifically, we use the cryptocurrency's ticker symbol as the Google Trend search terms and obtain the monthly Google Trend score over each cryptocurrency's life cycle to capture their popularity and attention over time.

Kaplan-Meier survivor function estimator (KM estimator) is a nonparametric statistical method to estimate the survivor function [21] . We observe each cryptocurrency i (i = 1…n) during each day t (t = 1,2, 3, …, T, where T is the last observation date). Following the standard survival analysis literature, if cryptocurrency i dies before T, we use t f to denote its failure time. If cryptocurrency i's failure does not occur before T (i.e., surviving beyond the last observation date), then it is censored [59] . Each cryptocurrency i is either dead or censored at the end of the data collection period (June 30, 2024). Consistent with the literature, we define the survivor function as S(t) = Pr ( t f > t ) [21] , which reflects the probability that a cryptocurrency survives beyond t. The KM estimator is simply an estimator of the survivor function, expressed as:

where t 1 < t 2 < … < t are unique days on which at least one death event occurred. During day t j when there is at least one death event, denote f j as the number of death events occurred and n j as the number of cryptocurrencies at risk (i.e., the number of cryptocurrencies that neither died nor were censored).

We estimate the KM survivor functions using different subsamples to provide preliminary support for our hypotheses. These subsamples include (1) cryptocurrencies with above/below the median of the average trading volume; (2) cryptocurrencies with above/below the median of the average market capitalization; (3) cryptocurrencies receiving above/below the median total number of social media mentions; and (4) cryptocurrencies with above/below the median of the price volatility. Fig. 1 illustrates these estimated KM survivor functions, and the results suggest that cryptocurrencies traded more actively, capturing a larger market capitalization, receiving more social media attention, and having a lower price volatility are associated with lower death hazards. These results support all four hypotheses.

An important restriction of this model is that the researchers do not observe the covariates X in continuous time, and they must carry the last observed values of the covariates into future hazard computations.

A hazard ratio greater than (less than) 1 indicates that a particular covariate is positively (negatively) related to the hazard rate. Note that we predict the death hazard of time t + 1 using time t covariates to ensure a properly designed predictive model, and this type of model configuration is in line with the literature [47, 61, 62] .

Although time-dependent Cox regression allows for a longitudinal Fig. The second group of variables consists of social media attention received by the cryptocurrencies, including (1) AnyMention t , which is a binary variable set to 1 if there is at least one social media mention during the past 30 days; (2) NumMention t , which is the number of social media mentions during the past 30 days (set to 0 if AnyMention t = 0); (3) NegSentiment t , which is the total number of bearish words divided by the total number of words in all messages mentioning a particular cryptocurrency during the past 30 days (set to 0 if AnyMention t = 0) [63] ; and (4) DaysSinceLastMention t , which is the number of days since the cryptocurrency was last mentioned in any social media messages.

Apart from the 30-day moving average measurements, we also calculate another set of similar variables using cumulative aggregation (i.e., variables computed using a time window spanning from t = 1 to the current day) to explore the effect of the long-term trend. Under cumulative aggregation, NumHighReturn t and NumLowReturn t are replaced by PctHighReturn t and PctLowReturn t , which measure the percentage of trading days with a return greater (less) than 10 % (-10 %). Last is GoogleTrend t , which is the monthly observed Google Trend data for each cryptocurrency. Table 1 shows the summary statistics of these variables.

We present the model estimation results in Table 2 , which shows the model estimated using 30-day moving average variables in columns (1) and ( 2 ) and the model estimated using cumulative variables in columns (3) and ( 4 ). The result shows that trading volume is strongly associated with the hazard rate. The coefficient estimates for TradingVolume are consistently negative and statistically significant for both models, indicating that a 1 % increase in the 30-day moving average trading volume (cumulative average trading volume) is associated with a 0.87 % (0.46  The coefficient estimate for market capitalization is statistically significant in the cumulative variable model, indicating that a 1 % increase in the cumulative market capitalization is associated with a 0.06 % decrease in the next-day death hazard. These results are consistent with the KM estimator analysis and support Hypothesis 2.

The coefficient estimates for DaysSinceLastMention t in both models range from 0.0001 to 0.0002 and are statistically significant, meaning that on average, one more day without a social media mention is associated with a 0.1 % to 0.2 % increase in the next-day death hazard. This evidence supports Hypothesis 3. However, the coefficients for volatility are not statistically significant in both models and thus Hypothesis 4 is not supported by the Cox regression.

The dataset used in this study contains daily observations of the covariates and the status variable (i.e., "alive" or "dead") over the entire lifecycle of each cryptocurrency, which resembles a time series dataset and can be modeled using certain types of neural networks, such as LSTM and transformer, because they are capable of capturing interperiod dependencies, and thus the lagged effects of the covariates [64] . The LSTM network achieves this purpose by forwarding memory vectors from the past to the future [65] ; the transformer network does this using a matrix reflecting the dependency among different days [66] . Furthermore, deep learning approaches eliminate assumptions about the underlying stochastic process and the need for subjective feature engineering. According to the universal approximation theorem, deep learning models could approximate any function given enough structural complexity and proper non-linear transformations [67] . They are also recognized for their capability to automatically identify relevant feature representations for accurate predictions [24] .

Several considerations must be taken when using LSTM/transformer networks for the purpose of survival modeling. First, after the death of a cryptocurrency, all covariates are no longer observable. To enable batch training, padding the sequence of the dead cryptocurrencies is necessary to match the length of the surviving ones, both for the predictors and for the status variable. Therefore, in addition to the "alive" status and the "death" status, we introduce a filler status "post-death." When training the transformer survival model, we apply a mask to block out the postdeath dates so that the network will not learn dependencies for these post-death dates [66] . Also important is that the loss associated with the filler status "post-death" must not affect the cross-entropy loss function during training, so the model is forced to only improve the "alive" and "death" prediction accuracy.

Second, in our cryptocurrency survival analysis, there is only one "death" event at most (although in rare cases, a cryptocurrency may be "resurrected" after its "death," we only model the first "death"), but many "alive" days before it. Therefore, equally weighting different days in the total loss calculation will result in underperformance in death prediction because the loss contributed from the single death event among all days is negligible. To regularize the network to put more emphasis on "death" prediction instead of "alive" prediction, we introduce a hyperparameter λ to amplify the loss from the death event prediction.

Last, the effect of the technology lifecycle of each cryptocurrency should be considered [68] . The death hazard in different lifecycle stages is likely to differ. We use positional embedding for this purpose. We give each day a fixed vector to control for the time effect. Consistent with the literature, we compute the positional embeddings using the following equations [66] :

In these equations, t indexes the days; 2i and 2i + 1 index the even and odd positions along the embedding vectors, respectively; d is the length of the embedding vector with 0 ≤ i < d/2; and n is a user-defined scalar, which we set to 10,000. After incorporating these considerations, the modified cross-entropy loss function is as follows:

where p t i is the predicted day t + 1 death hazard of cryptocurrency i using day t covariates, q t i is the predicted day t + 1 probability that cryptocurrency i survived, S t i is the true state of cryptocurrency i during day t + 1 (i.e., "alive," "death," or "post-death"), and m is the sample size. Grid search is employed to identify the optimal hyperparameters for the model and the training process. These include λ (ranging from 500 to 1500, in steps of 100), and the number of LSTM/transformer layers in the model (from 1 to 3). All models are trained for a maximum of 200 epochs, and early stopping is implemented to prevent overfitting. Training stops if the test set loss does not improve for 20 consecutive training epochs. In addition, the learning rate is scheduled to be halved if there is no loss improvement in 10 consecutive training epochs. Because the scale of the variables used in prediction varies dramatically, we also normalized each feature using all pre-censored/pre-death data to ensure consistent variable scales and improved numerical stability. Both the training and testing datasets are normalized against the training dataset to ensure that the testing dataset is completely unseen during training.

Fig. 2 depicts the neural network. First, we concatenate the input vector each day with the positional embedding; second, we forward the concatenated input into one or more LSTM layers or transformer selfattention layers; third, we further process the outputs using fully connected feed forward networks; and fourth, we apply Softmax transformation to generate the predicted next-day death hazard. To be consistent with the Cox regression estimation, we use day t covariates to predict the day t + 1 hazard rate.

Unlike the Cox survival regression, LSTM/transformer networks can autonomously extract valuable features and grasp dependencies between different days [24] . Therefore, there is no need to design lagged variables or cumulative variables manually, the model directly takes the normalized daily observed covariates as inputs. However, variables such as DaysSinceLastMention t , AnyMention t , and GoogleTrend t are included directly as inputs because they cannot be disaggregated into daily observed data. The interpretation of these models is presented in Section 6 after we propose the interpretability algorithm.

To gauge the out-of-sample hazard rate prediction performance, we use cryptocurrencies listed before September 15, 2020, on Coin-MarketCap as the training set to train the models (both the Cox survival regression and deep-learning survival models) and use all cryptocurrencies listed after September 15, 2020, as the testing set to evaluate the prediction performance. In this way, we adhere to the existing literature to train the models with historical data and use them to forecast future outcomes. This allows us to reliably assess their prediction performance and generalizability in future applications [5] . We use September 15, 2020, to split the sample into the training set and the testing set because approximately 20 % of the death events (186 death events) occurred after September 15, 2020, and about 80 % (740) occurred before it. This results in an approximate 80 %/20 % train-test split.

After training the model, we apply it to the test set to predict the time t + 1 hazard rate using time t covariates. The first survival analysis evaluation method we adopt is the one commonly used in the bankruptcy prediction literature [5, 19] . Specifically, during each day with at least one death event, we arrange the cryptocurrencies at risk (i.e., those cryptocurrencies that "die" during this day and those that survive through it) into 10 deciles based on their predicted hazard. We then place cryptocurrencies with high predicted death hazards into the first few deciles and cryptocurrencies with low predicted death hazards into the last few deciles. We then count and aggregate the cryptocurrencies in each decile that actually experience a death event during t + 1 through all days in the test set. If the model predicts well, this count should be very high in the top few deciles and very low in the bottom few deciles.

Table 3 presents the prediction evaluation results for both the Cox model and the deep learning survival models. Column (1) shows that 65.4 % of cryptocurrencies that experienced a death event the next day are placed in the top deciles by the Cox model because of their high predicted death hazard, indicating the general effectiveness in death hazard prediction. Column (2) shows that the LSTM survival model slightly outperforms the Cox model in prediction performance by placing 69.6 % dying cryptocurrencies in the top decile. Column (3) shows that the transformer survival model significantly outperformed both by ranking 97.8 % of dying cryptocurrencies in the top decile.

We also use the AUROC (area under the receiver operating characteristic curve) to evaluate the models' predictive performance. Specifically, we compute the AUROC values for each day with at least one "death" event and present the summary statistics across all event days in Table 4 . The result shows that the Cox model and the LSTM survival model attain comparable AUROC around 0.9, while the transformer survival model significantly outperforms both by achieving a much higher AUROC of 0.987. Both evaluation method confirms that the transformer survival model is superior in death prediction.

The high predictive performance of these models is rooted in the drastically abnormal cryptocurrency trading patterns before its "death," which can be easily picked up by the model. Fig. 3 compares the average trends of key covariates for surviving and dying cryptocurrencies over the 180 days prior to their censoring or death event. It shows that trading volume and market capitalization of dead cryptocurrencies drop sharply just before death, while those of the surviving cryptocurrencies remain largely stable. Also, for dying cryptocurrencies, the number of days since the last social media message and the price volatility also rise more than the surviving ones before their death.

In Section 4.2, we conducted a Cox regression analysis and interpreted the estimated model to derive managerial insights. However, because Cox regression relies on an assumed stochastic process where  the time t + 1 hazard is the logarithm of a linear combination of time t covariates, its interpretability is not flexible, leading to limitations in the types of queries it can address. For instance, how does a shock to one of the covariates predict the hazard rate change over time? Does this influence diminish or intensify? Moreover, what are the more subtle nonlinear effects of the covariates? In this section, we examine two ways to extend the interpretability of survival analysis: the lagged effect of the covariates and the non-linear effect of the covariates.

The impulse response technique is often used in vector autoregressive (VAR) systems to interpret the time series models. We will first illustrate the fundamental reasons why a VAR system is interpretable via IRF. Suppose a VAR system has p endogenous variables denoted as y t . Further assume a lag order of 1. Then the VAR system can be written as:

where both y t and ε t are p × 1 vectors, and Π is a p × p coefficient vector. Suppose a shock to the j-th endogenous variable occurs at time t (i.e., there is a small change to ε j,t ); then, the impact of this shock on all endogenous variables at a future point t + h is simply the derivative of y t+h with respect to ε j,t , as Eq. ( 7 ) shows:

where the second equality follows from iterative substitutions using Eq. ( 6 ) and the third equality follows from the elimination of all y terms and ε terms not observed at t during the differentiation. The IRF is simply the j-th column in the Π h-1 matrix. Thus, the IRF derived for the VAR system only depends on the coefficient estimates Π and is independent of any intermediate y quantities from t to t + h. This is a desirable property because the interpretation of the model does not depend on the realization of the intermediate variables and thus is easily generalizable. For example, when assessing the impact of a shock to a variable on another variable h days later, the only information needed is the estimated coefficients while the realization of all intermediate variables or input variables from t to t + h do not matter. Such interpretation may be an oversimplification, but it is easy to implement and understand. However, when modeling a time series using deep learning models, the same IRF will depend not only on the coefficient estimates but also on all the inputs over time, as the functions involved in the computation are typically complex and non-linear, and differentiation will not entirely eliminate the intermediate inputs. First, take the LSTM survival model as an example. Suppose y t+1 is the predicted hazard rate during day t + 1, x t is the day t covariates, and m t-1 is the memory state of day t -1. Then y t+1 = LSTM Output (x t , m t-1 ), and m t-1 = LSTM Memory (x t-1 , m t-2 ) 2 . If a shock occurs to x t , the IRF h days later can be written as:

The second equation holds by substituting m t+h-2 by LSTM Memory (x t+h-2 , m t+h-3 ), and m t+h-3 in the expression can be substituted further by LSTM Memory (x t+h-3 , m t+h-4 ). This substitution process continues until the initial memory state m init is reached in the first period. As Eq. ( 8 ) suggests, the IRF is determined by a series of nested LSTM Memory computations. Due to the non-linear nature of LSTM computations, taking derivatives with respect to x t do not eliminate any intermediate inputs from x t+1 to x t+h-1

. Eventually, we must use the chain rule to back-propagate to the gradient at x t , which will be a function of the entire sequence x t to x t+h-1 . Thus, the IRF can be expressed in the following form:

where g(⋅) represents the nested LSTM functions in Eq. ( 8 ) and β represents the estimated parameters in the neural network. Because the IRF depends not only on the coefficient estimates but also on the entire input sequence x t , …, x t+h-1 , making traditional interpretations such as "if x t increases by ε, the hazard will increase by a certain amount" is not possible. However, if we know the joint probability distribution of x t ,…, x t+h-1 , such as f(x t , …, x t+h-1 ), we can compute the expectation of the differentiation as:

To calculate the expectation, we use the observed time series of covariates for all cryptocurrencies to discretely approximate the joint distribution f(x t , …, x t+h-1 ) and then to empirically compute the value of Eq. (10) . The time series of the covariates for a single cryptocurrency represents a single random draw from the joint distribution f(x t , …, x t+h-1 ). We then compute the expected IRF by averaging the effect of the shock on the hazard across multiple draws (i.e., the full sample).

The transformer network is in a similar situation. Suppose there is a series of covariates observed from time t to t + h, denoted as x t , …, x t+h-1 , and a simple self-attention layer is applied to compute the hazard over time, denoted as y t+1 ,…y t+h . Let
