The findings indicate that the abovementioned cryptocurrencies can be beneficial to protect investors and hedge against risks during the COVID-19 pandemic. They also discovered that these three cryptocurrencies possess similar features to conventional assets. As a result, investors could not employ them to hedge against risks during the COVID-19 pandemic. In other words, they did not possess safe-haven features during the COVID-19 pandemic, when the implied volatility index was relied upon as a proxy for market volatility. Rubbaniy et al. (2021) used daily data from January 2015 to June 2020 to examine the herding behavior toward the cryptocurrency market-specific implied volatility index (VCRIX) as a proxy for cryptocurrency-specific market fear. The paper reported many interesting findings. First, it found no evidence of herding in times of greater degrees of cryptocurrency specific market fear, meaning that investors regained their trust in asset pricing models during shutdowns. Furthermore, the paper documented herding behavior toward VCRIX during the COVID-19 pandemic, except for the shutdown period. The latter finding implied that investors can benefit from this finding not only to make reasonable investment decisions in times of high levels of market fear and uncertainty but also to grasp the impact of herding on their investment decisions. Das et al. (2020) studied the safe-haven features of Bitcoin against crude oil implied volatility (OVX). The findings indicate that Bitcoin is not a good hedging asset against OVX. As a result, investors may choose other assets to invest in over Bitcoin to hedge against OVX in different market states. Fang et al. (2020) used the GARCH-MIDAS model to study the impact of news-based implied volatility (NVIX) as a proxy for investors' expectations of future uncertainty on the lengthy volatility of the major five cryptocurrencies, namely, Bitcoin, Ethereum, Ripple, Litecoin, and the New Economy Movement. The findings show a significantly negative effect on the lengthy volatility of the abovementioned cryptocurrencies. This implies that investors' expectations possess more predictive power based on lengthy cryptocurrency volatility compared to economic fundamentals. Akyildirim et al. (2020) investigated the relationship between cryptocurrency volatility and both US implied volatility (VIX) and European implied volatility (VSTOXX) as proxies for market fear. The findings show that cryptocurrencies not only increase with investor fear, but also that there is an interdependence between cryptocurrency volatility and financial market volatility. Furthermore, this interdependence grows substantially when the implied volatility of financial markets increases. Kim et al. (2021) constructed an index for cryptocurrencies implied volatility (VCRIX), a "Fear Index", to capture the investor's expectation of future uncertainty in the cryptomarket in the absence of cryptocurrency derivatives for the vast majority of cryptocurrencies. Chi and Hao (2021) predicted the volatility of Bitcoin and Ethereum. To do so, they compared four GARCH-family models with implied volatility induced by an option pricing model to examine the accuracy of the prediction of market volatility for each. They found that GARCH-family models work better than option-implied volatility in forecasting future realized volatility.

Future researchers could try to work on the realized extreme volatility that derivative indices such as VIX can help us attain and which is expected to be induced by the behavioral component of option pricing (Kim et al., 2021) . Moreover, institutional investors such as cryptocurrency exchange-traded funds (ETFs) have recently grown substantially in the cryptomarket. Another venue for future research could be investigating whether pricing efficiency improves over time after the introduction of derivative options and the growing existence of such investors (Chi and Hao, 2021) . Furthermore, more research is needed on the accuracy of forecasting using implied volatility indices and the information content of these indices in the cryptomarket. In addition, to the best of our knowledge, little is known about the determinants of implied volatility in the cryptomarket. Finally, employing jump diffusion processes and continuous-time diffusion is of particular importance to measure the dynamics of indices of implied volatility over time.

Stochastic volatility is the most commonly used method of modeling time-varying volatility in financial markets (Shephard and Andersen, 2009) . It can be defined as the price movements or fluctuations of asset prices not being fixed, as assumed in the Black-Scholes option pricing model, but rather following time-dependent stochastic processes (Privault, 2021) . Similar to implied volatility, a handful of research papers have studied stochastic volatility in cryptomarkets. Four groups of research streams are identified with respect to the stochastic volatility of cryptocurrencies. The first focused on the existence of stochastic volatility properties in the cryptomarket. For example, Phillip et al. (2018) and Phillip et al. (2019) examined the stylized facts of cryptocurrency volatility and provided evidence of the existence of many stylized facts, including stochastic volatility, that we should be aware of to evaluate the investability of cryptomarkets. Similarly, Chaim and Laurini (2018) and Kliber et al. (2019) used a stochastic volatility model with dynamic conditional correlation and confirmed that the standard linear GARCH is less flexible and does not suit the unique features and dynamics of Bitcoin. As a result, reliance on stochastic volatility models is essential. Dimpfl and Elshiaty (2021) provided evidence that Bitcoin contributes the most to the aggregate volatility of the cryptomarket.

The second group focused on the use of multivariate stochastic volatility models. Chaim and Laurini (2018) used multivariate stochastic volatility with discontinuous jumps to examine the volatility dynamics of the major nine cryptocurrencies. They discovered two periods of large volatility, namely in 2017 and early 2018. After that, the findings show a gradual reduction in volatility towards the end of the sample time span. Shi et al. (2020) extended the previous work by using a multivariate factor stochastic volatility model (MFSVM) to examine the stochastic volatility of the major six cryptocurrency prices, namely, Bitcoin, Ethereum, Ripple, Litecoin, Dash, and Stellar. The findings show that volatility increased substantially in the last few months of 2016 and mid-2017, while it leveled off from January 2018 onwards. It is also observed that there is a significant change in volatility from year to year.

The third focused on the inclusion of stochastic volatility to improve the quality of forecasting. Cross et al. (2021) demonstrated that the inclusion of stochastic volatility and a heavy-tailed distribution improves the predictive power of volatility models and outperforms the random walk benchmark. Hafner (2020) added the deterministic and stochastic components to Spline-GARCH, which improves the performance of statistical tests of bubbles.

The fourth group focused on the relationship between stochastic volatility and other factors such as EPU and the day-of-the-week effect. Yen and Cheng (2021) extended the stochastic volatility model proposed by Wang and Yen (2019) to examine the impact of the EPU index on cryptocurrency volatility. Interestingly, fluctuations in the Chinese EPU are a good predictor of cryptocurrency volatility, while fluctuations in the US, Japanese, and Korean ones are not. Moreover, fluctuations in the Chinese EPU are significantly and negatively related to the future volatilities of Bitcoin and Litecoin. Therefore, both Bitcoin and Litecoin are effective hedging instruments against Chinese EPU risk. Ma and Tanizaki (2019) used a stochastic volatility model with time-varying variance to examine the effect of the day-of-the-week on Bitcoin volatility from January 2013 to December 2018. They found empirical evidence of large volatility on Thursday and Monday.

From the studies above, we notice that most papers used small numbers of cryptocurrencies, except Phillip et al. (2018) and Phillip et al. (2019) . As a result, replicating the previous studies using a wider range of cryptocurrencies would be of interest to future researchers. Secondly, the use of high-frequency data to capture intraday dynamics is almost absent in the current literature on the stochastic volatility of cryptocurrencies. Hence, it is of particular importance for future researchers to take up this task. Thirdly, efficient techniques, such as the range-based stochastic volatility models proposed by Alizadeh et al. (2002) , maximum likelihood estimation of stochastic volatility models proposed by Aït-Sahalia and Kimmel (2007) , and dynamic factor multivariate stochastic volatility (DFMSV) proposed by Han (2006) , deserve some attention from future researchers. Finally, researchers are recommended to merge regime-switching into stochastic volatility models, as introduced by Vo (2009) . This enables us to model the stochastic volatility process when there is a shift in the mean.

In this section, we explain the factors that may play a role in predicting cryptocurrency volatility. In this context, we distinguish between factors related to rational expectation theory and factors related to the behavioral theory of finance. Studies that adopted the former one suggested factors like adverse selection costs (Tiniç et al., 2020) , S&P 500 returns and VIX returns (L´opez-Cabarcos et al., 2021) , global geopolitical risk (GPR) (Aysan et al., 2019) , global real economic activity for bullish and bearish market states (Walther et al., 2019) , leverage effect and EPU (Yu, 2019) , news coverage (Lucey et al., 2022) , day-of-the-week effect such as Monday and Thursday (Ma and Tanizaki, 2019) , macroeconomic news (Corbet et al., 2020a (Corbet et al., , 2020b)) , transaction activity (Gkillas et al., 2021) , oil market shocks (Yin et al., 2021) , CBOE gold ETF volatility index (GVZ), VIX, Google trends, global geopolitical risk (GPR) and gthe lobal economic policy uncertainty index (GEPU) (Liang et al., 2022) , and the trade weighted USD currency index (Bystrom and Krygier, 2018) as good predictors of cryptocurrency volatility. Moreover, Karaömer (2022) shows that a negative correlation exists between the volatility of cryptocurrency policy uncertainty and the volatility of cryptocurrencies. In contrast, Smales (2022) documents a positive association between cryptocurrency policy uncertainty and volatility. Wang et al. (2022) use the LASSO model to examine the drivers of volatility and find that macroeconmic factors are stronger than technical factors in predicting cryptocurrency volatility. Nguyen et al. (2022) document a negative relationship between both the Chinese interbank rate and the US federal funds rate, and stablecoin volatility. Salisu and Ogbonna (2022) show that incorporating the news effect improves the predictive power of the model during the COVID-19 pandemic relative to the historical average model.

Meanwhile, studies that adopted the behavioral theory suggested factors like investor sentiment (Güler, 2021; L´opez-Cabarcos et al., 2021; Bukovina and Marticek, 2016) , investor-driven internet search (Bystrom and Krygier, 2018) , investor attention (Sabah, 2020; Al Guindy, 2021) , Twitter-based sentiment (Kyriazis et al., 2022) , and herding behavior (Mandaci and Cagli, 2022) as good predictors of cryptocurrency volatility.

Many research gaps have been found in the current body of knowledge. For instance, examining the ability of monetary environment factors to predict cryptocurrency volatility is somewhat missing in the current literature. Moreover, other macroeconomic variables, such as gross domestic product (GDP), yield spread, inflation, changes in housing prices, US consumer confidence, business cycle factors, unemployment rate, and foreign direct investments, also remain unexplored. Researchers may also examine the ability of some institutional factors, such as corruption, transparency, and democracy, to predict cryptocurrency volatility. Secondly, future researchers have attempted to investigate the domestic political risk in huge economies, such as the USA and China, as potentially powerful predictors of cryptocurrency volatility, as suggested by Aysan et al. (2019) . On the behavioral side, prior literature focused on only three behavioral factors, namely investor sentiment, investor attention, and herding. Other behavioral biases, such as individualism, uncertainty avoidance, overconfidence, overreaction/underreaction, risk aversion, prospect theory, national culture, investor IQ, mental accounting, and representative heuristics, should be investigated separately to determine their contribution to the predictability of cryptocurrency volatility.

Research on cryptocurrency volatility is still far from being a mature subject of study because knowledge about cryptocurrency volatility is just slightly improved by the current body of knowledge. Thus, a lot more research is needed to build well-established beliefs about this new financial world. This review has raised some impressions. First, advancing cryptocurrencies will greatly depend on developing more sensible theoretical explanations for how this new financial world works, resulting in a better understanding of the nature and key sources of cryptocurrency volatility in general and stylized facts, as discussed in Section 2.2, in particular.

Secondly, there is profound empirical evidence that the dynamics of cryptocurrency volatility are unique. Such dynamics are multifractality (Mensi et al., 2019; Kakinaka and Umeno, 2022) . There is inverted asymmetric volatility compared to stock markets, and reactions to positive shocks outperform those to negative ones (Baur and Dimpfl, 2018; Cheikh et al., 2020; Fakhfekh and Jeribi, 2020) ; there is an unusually high level of uncertainty, and accordingly excessive volatility, compared to conventional assets due to the decentralized and deregulated nature as well as due to being unrelated to fundamentals or monetary policy (Klein et al., 2018; Dyhrberg, 2016; Brauneis and Mestel, 2019) ; cryptocurrency markets are more prone to misbehavior and manipulations that leave significant impacts on prices compared to other markets (Katsiampa et al., 2019) ; they show nonlinear behavior due to their complicated and chaotic nature and display high long-memory behavior, which gives the fractionally integrated GARCH (FIGARCH) model some superiority over other GARCH-family models (Mensi et al., 2019; Fakhfekh and Jeribi, 2020) ; they show heteroskedastic price returns (Katsiampa, 2019b) , have bubbles (Corbet et al., 2018; Fry and Cheah, 2016) , have extreme characterize cryptocurrency volatility because Bitcoin volatility is almost eight times greater than stock market volatility (Baur and Hoang, 2021; Harvey, 2017; Brauneis and Mestel, 2019; Ji et al., 2019) ; they have stochastic volatility (Phillipet al., 2018; Cross et al., 2021) , are heavy tailed (Phillip et al., 2018; Cross et al., 2021) , involve regime change or structural breaks (Ardia et al., 2019) , have mean reversion for minutely, hourly, daily, and weekly data frequencies (Corbet and Katsiampa, 2020; Turatti et al., 2020; Yarovaya et al., 2022) , and contain volatility spillover (Katsiampa, 2019a; Okorie and Lin, 2020) .

Thirdly, most papers in the literature used a version of the GARCH-family models. While these models offer some advantages, such as producing more accurate estimations of volatility and successfully dealing with the heteroskedasticity problem compared to the least squares methods, there is considerable evidence of the inappropriateness of this family of models to deal with the unique and unprecedented dynamics of cryptocurrency volatility. Also, GARCH-family models produce biased estimations where there are structural breaks. As a result, employing stochastic volatility models is duly justified. In other words, researchers are required to provide robust empirical volatility models and to conduct more replications and robustness research to support the currently available evidence, improve its validity and reliability, and stimulate its theoretical development. This may require solid and integrated econometric and financial knowledge as well as dedicated researchers who are willing to improve our understanding of this new financial world.

This review has important theoretical and practical implications for investors, researchers, regulatory bodies, portfolio managers, and policymakers because it impacts asset allocation, portfolio selection and management, risk management, and asset pricing, requiring them to be aware of the dynamics of cryptocurrency volatility. Meanwhile, being aware of the dynamics of cryptocurrency volatility will help cryptomarket participants make better investment decisions. Policymakers may use their understanding of these dynamics to set up new regulations that will improve the efficiency of financial markets and guide investors toward a better allocation of assets, leading to revived economic growth and development.

One prominent implication of this study is whether or not the current research indicates the market efficiency of cryptomarkets. There is prevailing evidence of inefficiency and an absence of random walk in cryptomarkets due to the existence of many stylized facts such as long memory, multifractality, structural breaks in means and variance, volatility clustering, persistence, asymmetry, and some behavioral biases like herding, among others. This inefficiency has influential effects on portfolio diversification, asset allocation, risk management, the valuation process, and the investment decision-making process. As a result, policymakers and regulatory bodies should work toward instituting the legal and regulatory infrastructure of cryptomarkets in order to not only improve transparency and the informational environment but also reduce manipulation and misbehavior to eventually enhance efficiency levels. Recently, the growing attention of financial institutions to cryptomarkets may hopefully attract more rational investors to the market and relatively improve the level of market efficiency.

The second implication of this paper is the safe-haven and hedging capabilities of cryptocurrencies. Hedging capability means a specific asset is negatively related or perfectly unrelated to another asset, while a safe haven asset is an asset that is negatively related or perfectly unrelated to another asset in distressed markets only (Baur and Lucey, 2010) . With respect to the hedging capabilities of cryptocurrencies, conflicting findings have been demonstrated in the literature. Some scholars doubt the capability of cryptocurrencies to act as good hedgers (Bouri et al., 2017; Tiwari et al., 2020; Klein et al., 2018) . Others argue that cryptocurrencies can be good short-term hedgers in extreme market states (Bouri et al., 2017; Demir et al., 2018) . The third stream of research claims that Bitcoin has the same hedging capabilities as gold (Dyhrberg, 2016) . Meshcheryakov and Ivanov (2020) provided evidence that Ethereum has hedging capabilities against the US stock and gold markets. Finally, Qiao et al. (2020) found the long-term hedging capabilities of Bitcoin against other cryptocurrencies.

With respect to safe-haven capabilities, conflicting findings have also been found. The first group argues that Bitcoin does not have safe haven capabilities. Stablecoins such as Tether, USD coin, TrueUSD, Paxos, Dai, and Gemini Dollar can act as a safe haven against Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin, and Ripple (Baur and Hoang, 2021; Mensi et al., 2020) . Kliber et al. (2019) demonstrated that Bitcoin can be a safe haven against economic recession in Venezuela only among the studied countries. Rubbaniy et al. (2021) documented that Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Ripple have safe haven capabilities against risk during the COVID-19 pandemic. Yang and Hamori (2021) provided evidence that the European carbon market possesses safe-haven and hedging capabilities against cryptocurrencies.

In short, it is clear that there is no consensus on the hedging and safe haven capabilities of cryptocurrencies. Much research is needed to help tackle the conflicting and insufficient evidence in the literature and help us better understand the hedging and safe haven properties of cryptocurrencies.

Since providing a future research agenda is the key output of reviewing literature, we introduce some research gaps in the current body of knowledge that may help extending the cryptocurrency volatility theory in the literature. In this section, we suggest some future research directions, as follows: First, replicability, reproducibility, and robustness studies are of importance to cryptocurrency volatility in particular and to the FinTech subject of study in general. Although some scholars and leading journals underestimate such scientific studies because they do not advance knowledge from this point of view, we believe that replicability, reproducibility, and robustness are critical for theoretical development (Dau et al., 2022; Duncan et al., 2014) . For instance, we suggest that future researchers replicate, reproduce, and conduct robustness studies by employing a wider range of cryptocurrencies to overcome the current weakness with respect to the use of a limited number of cryptocurrencies in most research to date. Other ways to replicate, reproduce, and conduct robustness studies could include the use of different data frequencies, different sample sizes, different econometric techniques, different volatility measures, subgroup analysis, different research designs, different time periods, and/or different market states. This way, researchers could enable practitioners and the academic community to develop deep-rooted thoughts and beliefs on the dynamics of cryptocurrency volatility and affirm the very presence of empirical results. Moreover, this would help overcome the limitations of individual research, which extends the available knowledge on cryptocurrency volatility and deepens the understanding of this financial phenomenon. In addition, these kinds of research serve to generalize of the original paper, make policymakers, investors, portfolio managers, and other market participants more certain about the potential impact of this decision, and inform them that their decisions are not based on biased findings.

Second, due to the lack of sufficient investigations and evidence, more focus on high-frequency data (i.e., hourly, minutely and secondly) is essential and should be included as part of the research agenda. This would enable us to understand the intraday dynamics and behaviors of cryptomarkets. The importance of high-frequency data increases the unique features of cryptomarkets., which operate 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and are characterized by being fundamentally different, largely decentralized and totally unregulated with a lack of institutional investors. This may add some computational complexity but will assist in developing intraday trading strategies. Furthermore, there is empirical evidence that the use of low-frequency data may lead to spurious results (Christensen et al., 2014) .

Third, because of the relative shortage of machine learning models such as neural networks in cryptomarkets, we chose them as our third research direction. The digital revolution imposes two serious challenges to conventional statistical techniques: the unprecedented computational power and the massive amount of available data (Brooks et al., 2019) . This widens the use of neural network-based models for financial applications, including forecasting. These models have yet to spread in cryptomarkets. Neural network models provide some advantages over others, such as accounting for nonlinear and non-random dynamics in the forecasting process (Chen et al., 2003) . The use of this bundle of forecasting models is expected to provide investors, portfolio managers, researchers, and other cryptomarket participants with accurate forecasts of the future price movements of cryptocurrencies, which leads to the development of profitable trading strategies. In the same vein, we noticed a lack of attention to prediction models of cryptocurrency's extreme volatility.

Fourth, the recent introduction of cryptocurrency derivatives in 2017 opens new space for a stream of research on cryptocurrency volatility. This includes all research related to implied volatility in the presence of derivatives rather than the absence thereof. This should include the information content of options' "implied volatility" on the expected volatility of the underlying assets. Moreover, the presence of Bitcoin options and futures may lead to an excess volatility phenomenon, which is the fluctuation in asset prices that does not belong to the price discovery. What induces this excess volatility, whether this is related to liquidity, and whether these derivatives would enhance stability and efficiency are interesting areas for research. Also, important issues such as, but not limited to, constructing implied volatility indices, the determinants of implied volatility, the relationship between implied volatility and return, and the relationship between implied volatility and realized volatility, should be considered.

Fifth, how investor behavior can explain the cryptocurrency volatility puzzle will play a critical role in understanding how this new financial world operates. Substantial work should be conducted to constitute a comprehensive picture of why and when crypto investors trade, what cryptos they select to include in their portfolios, how crypto investor behavior differs across different exchanges, what cognitive errors they make when trading, and finally how this behavior impacts crypto prices and volatility. In this regard, there is reasonable justification and evidence to believe that investors can help explain excessive volatility and that bubbles can play a role in the performance of initial coin offerings, especially as rational expectations-based explanation theories have failed to provide sensible and robust explanations of the statistically sophisticated and nonlinear features of asset prices (Olsen, 1998) . Even in cryptomarkets, there is initial evidence that investor behavior is more important than macroeconomic news in forecasting volatility (Fang et al., 2020) . This is consistent with the view that not only the arrival of new information but also market states and group occasions such as herding can push asset prices to fluctuate (Shefrin, 2002) .

Other behavioral biases committed by individual investors, such as overconfidence, disposition effect, loss aversion, mental accounting, ambiguity aversion, regret avoidance, conservatism, and framing, need to be considered in cryptomarkets., especially their role in explaining the stylized facts. Other behavioral phenomena, such as heterogeneous beliefs, investor myopia, the impact of different types of investors (i.e., individual investors, institutional investors, local investors, and foreign investors) on price fluctuations, social interaction, emotions, and optimism, may also play a role in inducing the stylized facts of cryptocurrency volatility.

With the new entry of institutional investors into the cryptomarkets, there is one more space for future research examining the impact of institutional trades on cryptocurrency prices and volatilities. In finance, the behavior of institutional investors is critical in causing price fluctuations and is sometimes more influential than fundamental news (Gabaix et al., 2006) . Future researchers can investigate the impact of institutional trades on liquidity, cryptocurrency price informativeness and volatility, institutional herding, positive feedback trading, the institutional ownership of each cryptocurrency and its impact on price stability, and the national culture of institutional investors and its impact on style investing and price fluctuations.

Sixth, stablecoins (i.e., Tether) are a class of cryptocurrencies introduced in 2014 to provide investors with stable cryptocurrency prices and minimal price fluctuations (Hampl and Gyönyörová, 2021) . These stablecoins are usually backed and secured by other assets such as gold, USD, or a mixture of assets and are introduced to act as good hedgers against traditional cryptocurrencies (Wang et al., 2020) . Investigating the existence of stylized facts, drivers, appropriate estimating and forecasting econometric models, as well as the hedging and safe haven capabilities of these stablecoins, remains an unsolved issue, and we know very little about them. Future research can and should address these issues.

Seventh, despite the economic viability of conventional cryptocurrencies as an asset or as a store of value, they have been widely criticized for their harmful effect on the environment. For instance, a bitcoin transaction produces up to 1018 kg of CO 2 and requires 2143 kWh of electricity. This is equivalent to more than 73 days of electricity consumption for a U.S. household, while the CO 2 emission produced by one bitcoin transaction is more than what 2 million of VISA transactions produce (Ren and Lucey, 2022; Pham et al., 2022; Arfaoui et al., 2023) . Chia, Cardano, Stellar, Tron, Ripple, Nano, Iota, Eos, Bitcoin Green, Algorand, Hedera, Polkadot, Flow, and Tezos are the prominent green cryptocurrencies. So far, very little is known about the price and volatility dynamics (i.e., stylized facts) of these cryptocurrencies. We highly recommend future researchers take care of this issue.

Eighth, similar to the green cryptocurrencies, the gold-backed cryptocurrencies as well as the gold-backed Islamic cryptocurrencies have lacked sufficient attention in the literature. Unlike most conventional cryptocurrencies, the value of gold-backed cryptocurrencies is linked to a tangible asset. As a result, their values are protected against unbearable price fluctuations. This feature is expected to uphold the safe-haven and hedging properties of this kind of cryptocurrency.

Finally, evaluating cryptomarket volatility forecasts is a promising area for future research. In general, volatility forecasts can be evaluated directly through some statistical methods that rank alternative volatility models. These evaluations can be conducted by using conventional techniques, such as those proposed by Mincer and Zarnowitz (1969) or Diebold and Mariano (1995) , and their extensions in cases of realized variance, or by using the loss function when the available forecasts are extracted from more than one model. On the other hand, the indirect evaluation methods of forecasts rely on portfolio selection and value at risk (VaR) because volatility is involved in many economic situations, such as utility maximization, portfolio optimization, hedging and option pricing. Portfolio optimization techniques, dynamic portfolio management, and machine learning and their enormous extensions (either for estimation or modeling) provide much space for future research to assess the precise nature of volatility forecasts. Moreover, with respect to hedging risk, many scholars can depend on VaR and its robust extensions to assess volatility forecasts.

This paper provided a review and synthesis of isolated research points. We conclude that the dynamics of cryptocurrencies are unique and different from other financial and non-financial assets due to the unique foundations of new crypto exchanges and the distinctive features of these new financial products, such as being unauthorized and the presence of practical misconduct with respect to the use of cryptocurrencies. As a result, cryptocurrency volatility is worth extensive investigation.

We cannot claim that this review is without limitations. First, many relevant papers were neglected because they were published in poor-quality journals. In other words, we include papers only if they were published in a trustworthy academic journal. In other words, a paper was considered as long as its journal existed in the ABS journal ranking. Second, we preferred not to rely on some classifications of volatility that are based on the econometric models being used, such as pre-ARCH models, GARCH-family models, long-memory models, regime-switching models, and stochastic volatility models, to reduce the econometric color and to pay more attention to financial applications instead. Also, we cannot claim that this review provides a comprehensive picture of cryptocurrency volatility. It rather improves our understanding of this contemporary topic, which deserves more reviews and more classifications, especially with the huge number of available studies.

To sum up, the literature on cryptocurrency volatility has grown substantially over the few years. Nonetheless, there is still much room for more research to be conducted in this area. To be more precise, the use of high-frequency data, the use of a wider plethora of cryptocurrencies, and the use of advanced computational techniques such as neural networks and machine learning require some regard from future researchers. Moreover, the well-known stylized facts, such as asymmetric volatility, volatility spillover, structural breaks, volatility persistence, tail dependence, and extreme volatility, still have some avenues for future research.

Another important recommendation is whether stochastic volatility models work better than deterministic models in describing the unique dynamics of cryptocurrency volatility. This paper has reviewed some previous studies on this research point, but the door is still open for much more to be conducted. Finally, what determines future cryptocurrency volatility and the competition between conventional and behavioral variables in predicting it are unsolved issues with have fruitful theoretical and practical implications.

Motivated by the cross-sectional and temporal fluctuations in their trading activity, digital assets provide a fruitful environment to investigate the impact of trading activity on expected returns. Portfolio-level analyses and cryptocurrency-level crosssectional regressions demonstrate a negative and significant relation between expected returns and variability of dollar trading volume and turnover. This effect is robust to controls for beta, size, momentum, network premia, lottery demand, liquidity, idiosyncratic, volatility, and downside risks. The effect of trading activity variation is more pronounced among cryptocurrencies that are costlier to arbitrage. A zero-cost strategy exploiting this predictability survives transaction costs, various size and liquidity screens.

It is generally accepted that liquidity is important for understanding asset returns. The existing evidence on liquidity primarily focuses on arguably the most studied and most liquid markets -the equity market in the United States and the global foreign exchange market. With the rising prices and public awareness of Bitcoin, cryptocurrency investments have been attracting increasing attention and trading volumes. Nevertheless, liquidity in the cryptocurrency space is still not well understood due to limited transparency, decentralized structure, and lax regulatory oversight of the crypto landscape. The recent collapse of crypto giant FTX and the evidence of cryptocurrency crashes highlight the importance of understanding time-variation in liquidity in the cryptocurrency market. The main contribution of this paper is to examine the effect of liquidity fluctuations on expected returns.

Our empirical evidence focuses on a moderately large cross-section of cryptocurrencies traded against the U.S. Dollar (USD) from September 1, 2017, to September 1, 2022. We aggregate the daily price and volume data across multiple centralized exchanges. In our empirical analysis, we employ two measures of trading activity -dollar trading volume and turnover -to proxy for the liquidity of individual cryptocurrencies. Given the extreme cross-sectional and temporal heterogeneity in trading activity across digital assets, we employ the coefficient of variation to quantify the variability of volume and turnover. We next implement portfolio-level analyses and cryptocurrency-level cross-sectional regressions to study the relationship between expected returns and the coefficient of variation measures.

Univariate portfolio analyses show that cryptocurrencies with high (low) trading activity variation earn low (high) future raw and risk-adjusted returns. The excess return difference between quintile portfolios with the lowest and highest coefficient of variation of volume (turnover) is economically large and statistically significant regardless of valueor equal-weighted constructions, though the magnitudes are larger in the case of equalweighting. The zero-cost strategy generates a positive and significant alpha after adjusting for the market, size, and momentum factors of Liu et al. (2022) . The results are also robust to using various windows ranging from 7 to 30 days to calculate the coefficient of varia-tion measures. To better understand the nature of this relationship, we look at the average statistics of cryptocurrencies in quintile portfolios sorted on volume (turnover) variability.

The cryptocurrencies with the higher coefficient of variation tend to be smaller, less liquid, and more volatile as well as have higher idiosyncratic and downside risks. Thus, the negative and strong relationship between variations in trading activity and expected returns surprisingly contradicts the risk-return trade-off.

Furthermore, we demonstrate that the effect of volume (turnover) variability persists after controlling for a number of cryptocurrency characteristics in the bivariate portfolio analyses and the cross-sectional regressions of Fama and MacBeth (1973) . We first demonstrate that the risk associated with fluctuations in liquidity is not explained by established cryptocurrency anomalies -market beta, size, and momentum (Liu et al., 2022) and the network premia captured by the network-to-market ratio (Cong et al., 2021; Liebi, 2022) . Furthermore, the negative relationship between the coefficient of variation of trading activity and expected returns is robust to the level of volume and turnover, volume shocks (Babiak et al., 2022) , and liquidity measures -the bid-ask spread (Corwin and Schultz, 2012; Abdi and Ranaldo, 2017) and (realized) Amihud illiquidity measures (Amihud, 2002; Ranaldo and de Magistris, 2022) . Quantitatively, we find that liquidity proxies partially capture the observed effect, though it remains economically and statistically significant.

Next, motivated by the idea that investors may have a preference for cryptocurrencies with lottery-like payoffs, we investigate the significance of lottery demand in explaining the effect of liquidity variability. For this reason, we compute a common proxy for lottery preferences -a maximum daily return (MAX) (Bali et al., 2011) . Controlling for the MAX effect diminishes the average return differences between cryptocurrencies with the lowest and highest liquidity variability in double sorts and reduces the negative estimate in the cross-sectional regressions. Nevertheless, both the average return and the slope coefficient remain statistically significant at the conventional confidence levels. By construction, high MAX cryptocurrencies will also have high idiosyncratic and realized volatility. We calculate both volatility measures following Ang et al. (2006 Ang et al. ( , 2009)) . Given the recent decline in prices of Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies, we also extend our analysis to account for the downside risk effect in Zhang et al. (2021) and calculate value-at-risk and expected shortfall characteristics. We demonstrate that accounting for volatility and downside risks leads to the same conclusion: the effect of trading activity variability survives in the portfolio analyses and the cross-sectional regressions.

We next provide a plausible explanation of the anomalous effect of variations in trading activity. Several observations point towards the limits-to-arbitrage theory behind our results. Exploiting the liquidity variability effect requires short-selling cryptocurrencies with the highest coefficient of variation of volume (turnover). These digital assets appear to be smaller, less liquid, more volatile, and prone to downside risks. Thus, it may be costly to engage in their short-selling. Furthermore, these cryptocurrencies have a higher idiosyncratic risk, which is regarded as the primary arbitrage cost by the extant literature. To test this hypothesis, we create an arbitrage index capturing different dimensions of arbitrage costs via idiosyncratic volatility and three liquidity proxies. The independent double sorts based on the arbitrage index and the coefficient of variation confirm that the mispricing is concentrated in cryptocurrencies with the highest arbitrage costs.

Our empirical results are based on the screen, which chooses the top 100 cryptocurrencies based on market capitalization each day. This ensures that the results are not driven by tiny tokens. In robustness checks, we confirm that our results are robust to alternative screens including crypto assets with market values above $1 ($2) million following Cong et al. (2021) and Liu et al. (2022) and excluding the least liquid tokens similarly to Zhang et al. (2021) . In yet another exercise, we check that profitability of the zero-cost strategy exploiting the volume (turnover) variability effect is robust to simplistic transaction costs.

Our paper contributes to the growing research on the cross-sectional predictors of cryptocurrency returns. Liu et al. (2020) , Shen et al. (2020) , and Liu et al. (2022) propose the market, size, and momentum factors. Cong et al. (2021) and Liebi (2022) augment the three-factor model to include the value premium. A number of additional studies highlight the network effects to explain cryptocurrency returns (Li et al., 2020; Bhambhwani et al., 2021; Liu and Tsyvinski, 2021) . The literature also proposes various factors affecting cryptocurrency returns including investor attention (Wang and Vergne, 2017; Corbet et al., 2022) , downside risk (Dobrynskaya, 2020; Zhang et al., 2021) , idiosyncratic volatility (Zhang and Li, 2020) , lottery demand (Li et al., 2021) , and higher moments (Jia et al., 2021) , among others. In addition to observable risk factors, Babiak and Bianchi (2021) and Borri et al. (2022) propose a latent factor approach to explain the cross-section of cryptocurrency returns. We contribute to this literature by investigating the role of the second moment of liquidity, proxied by the two measures of trading activity -trading volume and turnover.

Empirically, our paper is motivated by the studies that analyze fluctuations in liquidity in different markets. On the methodological side, our paper is closest to Chordia et al. (2001) studying variations in trading activity in the equity market. We contribute to this literature by providing a comprehensive investigation of the effect of trading activity fluctuations on the cross-section of cryptocurrency returns. In our focus on trading volume, our paper is also related to Babiak et al. (2022) . The authors show that returns from liquidity provision, proxied by the returns of a short-term reversal strategy (Nagel, 2012) , are concentrated in cryptocurrencies with low trading volume. Our paper is different from their analysis as we investigate the information content of the second moment of the trading volume. In relation to liquidity in cryptocurrency markets, Brauneis et al. (2021) provides a horse race of various liquidity proxies in their ability to predict the prices of the two cryptocurrencies BTC and ETH. We differ from this study by examining the crosssectional predictability of the second moment of liquidity. To our knowledge, Leirvik (2022) is the only study focusing on the effect of liquidity volatility. Our paper is distinct from this study because we focus on asset pricing implications for a large cross-section instead of the five biggest cryptocurrencies. Furthermore, our key focus is trading volume and turnover instead of the bid-ask spread employed by Leirvik (2022) .

Our paper is connected to the literature on the limits of arbitrage and price formation in the cryptocurrency markets. Makarov and Schoar (2020) demonstrate persistent deviations in Bitcoin prices across exchanges and suggest that there are significant barriers to exploiting these arbitrage opportunities. 1 Wang et al. (2022) investigate cyclic arbitrages in decentralized exchanges. Similarly to this literature, our results suggest limits-to-arbitrage as the likely explanation of mispricing documented in our paper. The recent literature also aims to understand the economics of cryptocurrency trading through the lens of theoretical models including Sockin and Xiong (2018) , Cong et al. (2021), and Cong et al. (2021) .

We retrieve daily data on prices and trading volume from CryptoCompare and onchain activity from IntoTheBlock. The full sample covers the period from June 26, 2014, to September 1, 2022, with a day defined at a start time of 00:00:00 UTC. The prices and volume are aggregated across more than 80 different centralized exchanges. 2 The aggregation across different exchanges uses a volume-weighted scheme, which accounts for the exchange-specific trading activity. Thus, larger and more established exchanges tend to have relatively more weight in the aggregation of prices and trading volume compared to smaller and peripheral exchanges. This volume-weighted scheme is standard in the literature (Liu et al., 2022) . The cryptocurrencies use USD as the quote currency and are included in the sample if all data from CryptoCompare and IntoTheBlock are available.

We introduce various filters to mitigate the impact of erratic or suspicious trading activity. First, we exclude observations with a zero trading volume or a zero price. Second, for each day we compute the ratio of trading volume to the market capitalization of each cryptocurrency and exclude observations with a ratio greater than 1. This simple filter screens out cryptocurrencies with potentially fake volume. Third, we exclude (1) all cryptocurrencies that are backed by or track the price of gold or any precious metal; (2) wrapped coins (e.g., WBTC), because they represent copies of existing tokens; (3) stablecoins, including those that are centralized (e.g., USDT, USDC) and algorithmically stabilized (e.g., DAI, UST) for any fiat currencies; and (4) synthetic derivatives (e.g., stETH, stSOL), as they track the value of an existing cryptocurrency. Our screening follows the classification provided by CoinMarketCap. Appendix A presents a summary of the procedure and additional details of the filters implemented by CryptoCompare to mitigate the impact of fraudulent trading activity and malfunctioning API for individual exchanges.

These steps mitigate the effect of fake volume and substantially reduce the exposure of the empirical analysis to misreported trading activity for some exchanges. The two comments are noteworthy. First, fake trading is typically performed via crypto-to-crypto trading on individual and relatively smaller exchanges that inflate trading volume in order to attract Initial Coin Offering's (ICO) listings or to manipulate the market (Li et al., 2018; Cong et al., 2021) . We consider the cryptocurrencies traded against a fiat currency and then aggregate the price and volume data over a large cross-section of exchanges. Therefore, the risk that the manipulation of relatively small exchanges could affect the overall market activity is substantially mitigated. Second, the fact that we focus on transactions taking place on regular trading exchanges should mitigate the concern that market activity is primarily driven by illegal activities. The latter typically do not take place on registered centralized or decentralized exchanges but through peer-to-peer transactions on the blockchain (Foley et al., 2019; Griffin and Shams, 2020) . This screening process yields an unbalanced panel of 405 cryptocurrencies. Figure 1 illustrates the market value of cryptocurrencies in our sample relative to the total market capitalization. Although our sample covers a smaller number of cryptocurrencies than currently available -there are more than 19,000 different tokens at the time of writing according to CoinMarketCap -our coverage of the market value is substantial. In relative terms, our dataset constitutes 95% of the total market value from 2014 until late 2019 and above 70% at the end of the period under consideration. As shown in Figure 1 , the initial size of the cross-section is small with a handful of available cryptocurrencies before 2017. This number rapidly increases from around 50 pairs in August 2017 to around 400 pairs by the end of 2019 and then slightly decreases to around 300 by the end of the sample.

In our empirical analysis, we focus on the sample of 405 cryptocurrencies covering the period from September 1, 2017, to September 1, 2022. We choose the starting date of September 1, 2017, because the number of available cryptocurrencies exceeds 50 at that time, which enables us to perform univariate and bivariate portfolio sorts.

The key focus of our empirical investigation is the variation of trading activity and its relation with future returns. We focus on two proxies of trading activity: dollar trading volume defined as the total dollar value of coins transferred across wallets within and across centralized exchanges, and turnover defined as the ratio of dollar trading volume to the current supply (Datar et al., 1998) . The current supply is the total number of coins or tokens that have been mined and includes all cryptocurrencies in public hands, either circulating in the market or locked/vested. Following Cong et al. (2021) , we compute the average daily volume (VOL) and turnover (TURN) over the past seven days for each cryptocurrency. We calculate the coefficient of variation of volume (turnover), which is defined as the ratio between the standard deviation and the mean of daily dollar trading volume (turnover) over the past k days and denoted by CVVOLk (CVTURNk). We consider four choices of k = 7, 14, 21, 30 days and verify the robustness of our results to different lengths of a rolling window.

A significant relation between variations in trading activity and expected cryptocurrency returns can be explained by other fundamental risks or cryptocurrency-specific attributes that highly correlate with changes in trading volume and turnover. We account for this possibility by controlling for additional characteristics of digital assets that have been shown to predict future returns by the existing literature. Table 1 provides the description of cryptocurrency characteristics. Liu et al. (2022) propose that three risk factors -market, size, and momentum -explain the cross-sectional expected cryptocurrency returns. Motivated by this evidence, we estimate the CAPM regression of daily cryptocurrency returns on the value-weighted market returns using a 30-day rolling window (Lewellen and Nagel, 2006) . For each cryptocur-

rency, we save conditional alpha (ALPHA) and beta (BETA) from these short-window regressions. We compute each cryptocurrency's market capitalization (SIZE) as the product of the current supply and the current USD price. We also calculate past k-day momentum (MOMk) as the cumulative return over the past k = 7, 14, 21, 30 days. Recently, Cong et al. (2021) and Liebi (2022) show that blockchain fundamentals -the fundamental-to-market ratio called the crypto value factor -determine crypto asset prices. Thus, we compute the network-to-market value (BM) of the individual cryptocurrency as the ratio of a cumulative number of unique addresses over the current supply, which is multiplied by the current USD price.

Next, we control for a set of liquidity measures. Using daily open, high, low, and close prices, we construct two approximations of the bid-ask spread following the methodology of Corwin and Schultz (2012) and Abdi and Ranaldo (2017) . We then take a simple average of the two proxies to construct the bid-ask spread (BA). Following Amihud (2002) , we construct the Amihud illiquidity measure, defined as the ratio between the sum of absolute daily returns and the average daily volume over the past seven days (ILLIQ). Next, following Ranaldo and de Magistris (2022) who develop a refinement of the standard Amihud illiquidity measure, we compute the realized Amihud illiquidity (RILLIQ) as the ratio between realized volatility and the average daily volume over the past seven days. Babiak et al. (2022) demonstrate that returns from liquidity provision are mainly concentrated in cryptocurrencies with lower levels of trading activity. Motivated by this evidence and the information content of abnormally low volume in traditional currency markets (Cespa et al., 2022) , we control for volume changes by calculating volume shocks (VSHOCK), defined as the (log) deviation of volume from its trend -the log average daily volume over the past 30 days.

Furthermore, we uncover a potential connection between the variations in trading activity and lottery demand, idiosyncratic and realized volatility, and downside risk. Bali et al. (2011) demonstrate the importance of lottery-like payoffs in asset pricing. Adopting their proxies of lottery demand, we compute the maximum daily return over the last k = 7, 14, 21, 30 days (MAXk). Following Ang et al. (2006) , we calculate idiosyncratic volatility (IVOL) as the standard deviation of residuals from the regression used to obtain conditional alphas and betas. We construct realized volatility (RVOLk) as the standard deviation of daily realized returns during the past k days. Zhang et al. (2021) document a positive relation between downside risk and future returns. Thus, we calculate value-atrisk and expected shortfall at 95% (VaR95 and ES95) estimated based on daily returns over a 90-day rolling window.

Finally, a large strand of the academic literature suggests that cryptocurrencies are primarily driven by psychological factors. As prices of digital assets move up and down, trading activity may reflect shifts in the demand of retail and institutional investors. The clientele hypothesis posits that assets with greater investor following should earn higher expected returns (Merton, 1987) . Although we do not have the data on cryptocurrency ownership, we partially explore this hypothesis by exploiting the blockchain data on new and active addresses similar to Chordia et al. (2001) . For each day, we compute the number of unique addresses that appeared for the first time in a transaction of the native coin in the network (NEW). We further calculate unique addresses that were active in the network either as a sender or receiver (ACT).

In our empirical investigation, we implement a variety of single-sorted and doublesorted portfolios. We consider value-weighted (based on market capitalization) and equalweighted portfolios formed daily using various cryptocurrency characteristics. In our analysis, we consider only those cryptocurrencies that on a particular day have non-zero market capitalization and non-missing characteristic(s) used for portfolio selection as well as a non-missing return on the next trading data. In doing so, we ensure that we are able to form value-and equal-weighted portfolios at the end of the current day and track the returns of each portfolio on the following day. Furthermore, each day we select the top 100 cryptocurrencies based on the current market capitalization. This implies that assets employed in portfolio construction are dynamically adjusted to exclude the smallest coins.

In robustness checks, we implement alternative screens that include cryptocurrencies with a market capitalization larger than $1 million similar to Liu et al. (2022) and Cong et al. (2021) , or exclude the least liquid cryptocurrencies according to the Amihud illiquidity measure similar to Zhang et al. (2021) . As shown in Section 3.7, our key conclusions remain unaltered when we apply different portfolio screens.

To begin our analysis, we form quintile portfolios based on the coefficient of variation of volume (turnover). We implement single-sorted portfolios on a daily basis between September 1, 2017, and September 1, 2022. We further look at the long-short portfolio buying and selling quintiles of cryptocurrencies with the lowest and highest variations of trading volume (turnover). Table 2 presents summary statistics of the value-and equalweighted returns of CCVOL portfolios. Panels A, B, C, and D showcase portfolios based on the coefficient of variation calculated over 7-, 14-, 21-, and 30-day rolling windows.

As shown in Panel A, a value-weighted portfolio combining cryptocurrencies with the lowest CVVOL values earns a monthly average return of -1.68%. The mean returns tend to decrease when we move from the lowest to the highest CVVOL portfolios. In particular, the fourth and fifth quintiles generate -8.61% and -18.04% per month. The value-weighted average raw return difference between the low CVVOL portfolio and the high CVVOL portfolio is 16.36% with a significantly positive Newey and West (1986) adjusted t-statistic of 5.08. This illustrates that cryptocurrencies with higher variation in trading activity earn significantly lower expected returns. The difference in average returns translates into the (almost) monotonically decreasing pattern in Sharpe ratios. For example, the lowest CVVOL portfolio produces a Sharpe ratio of -0.06 per month, whereas the two portfolios with the highest CVVOL measures have risk-adjusted performances of -0.27 and -0.44.

Next, we examine whether standard asset pricing models can explain the portfolio returns sorted on the variations in trading volume. Table 2 presents the monthly alphas after adjusting for the market, size, and momentum factors of Liu et al. (2022) . Panel A for value-weighted portfolios indicates that the lowest CVVOL portfolio generates an alpha of -7.61%, whereas the highest CVVOL portfolio produces an alpha of -27.80% per month.

Moreover, the three-factor alphas exhibit the same pattern as in returns, with the highest (lowest) alphas for the lowest (highest) CVVOL portfolio and a sharp decline in values starting from the third quintile. Most importantly, the alpha difference of quintiles with extreme variations in trading activity equals 20.19% on a monthly basis, which is both economically and statistically significant. The right part of Panel A presents results for the equal-weighted portfolios and our findings are qualitatively similar. Quantitatively, one can observe a stronger underperformance of quintiles comprising cryptocurrencies with a higher coefficient of variation measures. The larger spread in raw and risk-adjusted excess returns is visible as well.

Table 3 reports results when using turnover instead of volume as a proxy of trading activity. Panel A reports a monthly value-weighted average return of -3.62% (-17.27%), a monthly Sharpe ratio of -0.12 (-0.45), and a monthly three-factor alpha of -9.59% (-26.27%)

for the lowest (highest) CVTURN quintile. This yields an average return difference between the first and fifth quintile portfolios of 13.65% with a t-statistic of 3.91 as well as economically significant risk-adjusted performances. Note that there is a sharp decline in excess returns, Sharpe ratios, and alphas of the fourth and fifth quintiles, which contain cryptocurrencies with the highest CVTURN quantities. The equal-weighted quintile and long-short portfolios exhibit performance metrics of a similar magnitude. Panels B, C, and D report portfolio results for longer rolling window sizes and confirm that our findings remain qualitatively and quantitatively similar. Overall, the presented evidence demonstrates a negative relation between expected cryptocurrency returns and the variability of trading activity as measured by dollar trading volume or turnover.

Figure 2 augments results by illustrating the cumulative returns of CVVOL portfolios for the case of a 30-day rolling window. Consistent with the average returns of quintiles, the cumulative performance exhibits a decreasing pattern moving from cryptocurrencies with the lowest coefficient of variations to those with the highest. The zero-cost strategies exploiting variability in trading volume generate a similar cumulative return of 1000% during the considered period for the value-and equal-weighted schemes. A value-weighted long-short portfolio, however, produces higher volatility and experiences larger declines in bust cycles as indicated by the negative returns during the "crypto-winter" of early 2018, the COVID-19 crash in March 2020, and the Bitcoin meltdown in 2021. Figure 3 demonstrates that the predictive power of turnover variations on future returns is slightly weaker. Indeed, the cumulative performance of an equal-weighted zero-cost strategy diminishes to around 900%, whereas a value-weighted investment produces a cumulative return of around 750% by the end of the sample.

We now examine the properties of cryptocurrencies that belong to different quintiles based on the coefficient of variation measures. This analysis may shed more light on the cryptocurrency-specific characteristics that can potentially explain the significant negative relation between expected returns and variations in trading activity. To save space, we consider the case of CVVOL and CVTURN measures estimated over the 30-day rolling window. To do so, we divide cryptocurrencies into quintiles using the corresponding metrics, compute the median values of various cryptocurrency-specific characteristics for each quintile, and calculate the time-series averages of the cross-sectional medians.

The volume shock characteristic is strongly negative (slightly positive) for cryptocurrencies with a low (high) coefficient of variation. This observation indicates that low variability of trading activity is associated with trading volume below its trend, whereas high variability reflects abnormally high trading volume.

Second, one observes a monotonic decline in beta and size as the variation of trading volume increases. For instance, the average market capitalization of cryptocurrencies in the lowest (highest) CCVOL portfolio is around $364.6 ($107.7) million. The first quintile has a slight value tilt in beta, however, the average beta is decreasing from 1.04 to 0.95 when moving from the second to the fifth quintile. 3 The network-to-market value is also decreasing in CCVOL. Considering that value cryptocurrencies yield higher average returns than growth cryptocurrencies (Liebi, 2022) , blockchain fundamentals associated with the value premium may drive a significant portion of our findings. In relation to network characteristics, one also observes that cryptocurrencies with lower (higher) trading variability tend to have a bigger (smaller) number of new and active addresses.

Third, the average raw and risk-adjusted past performance is increasing in the coefficient of variation. That is, the cryptocurrencies with abnormally high trading activity tend to have a strong recent performance. Thus, agents may over-value those assets and are willing to pay more for cryptocurrencies that exhibit extreme variation in trading activity, which leads to lower expected returns in the future. Additionally, the average daily maximum returns increase steadily from the first to the fifth quintile. This measure reflects investors' demand for assets with lottery-like payoffs (Bali et al., 2011) .

Fourth, there is a strong increasing pattern in the average liquidity measures across the five portfolios. The average bid-ask spread almost doubles from 0.04 to 0.07 between the bottom and top quintiles. The two illiquidity measures -Amihud and realized Amihudalso increase from 0.04 and 0.01 for the lowest variation of trading activity cryptocurrencies to 1.69 to 0.31 for the highest. These results are consistent with the observation that the level of trading activity tends to increase with asset liquidity. For instance, the average trading volume in the first quintile is $1.93 million compared to just $0.20 million in the fifth quintile. Considering the prior findings in the literature and the observed liquidity patterns across quintiles, one may attribute the profitability of the CVVOL strategy to limits-to-arbitrage.

Finally, several risk measures -idiosyncratic and realized volatility as well as value-atrisk and expected shortfall -tend to increase with the coefficient of variation. In equity markets, Ang et al. (2006) document a negative relation between idiosyncratic risk and expected stock returns. In cryptocurrency markets, however, the aforementioned risk measures are found to positively predict future returns (Zhang and Li, 2020; Zhang et al., 2021) . This likely limits the possibility to provide a risk-based explanation of the volume variability effect.

In Section 3, we demonstrate that the effect of the variation of trading activity remains significant (although it diminishes in some cases) after accounting for other cryptocurrency characteristics in conditional bivariate portfolio sorts. To better understand the effect, we construct an arbitrage index that captures different dimensions of arbitrage costs from various proxies. The independently double-sorted portfolios based on the arbitrage index and the coefficient of variation of volume suggest costly arbitrage as an appropriate explanation of the observed effect.

In this section, we compute the k-day-ahead portfolio transition matrix to evaluate the cross-sectional persistence of the coefficient of variation of volume. 4 We calculate the average probability that a cryptocurrency in a specific quintile will be in another quintile k days from today. For a better illustration of portfolio persistence, we examine the transition probabilities at three lags of k = 1, 14, 30 days.

Table 5 reports the transition probabilities for quintile portfolios sorted on the volume variation measures estimated over the 7-day (Panel A) and 30-day (Panel B) rolling windows. If the variations in trading volume are random, the probabilities in the matrix should be approximately 20%. However, Panel A indicates that the lowest quintile cryptocurrencies have a probability of 78.9% to be in the same quintile the next day. This probability decreases monotonically to 42.2% and 37.9 % as we move from a 1-day lag to longer periods of 14 and 30 days. Interestingly, the highest quintile cryptocurrencies For each measure, we compute transition probabilities at the lag of k = 1, 14, 30 days. Specifically, we form quintile portfolios of cryptocurrencies based on the variation of the trading volume on the day t. We then form quintile portfolios of cryptocurrencies based on the variation of the trading volume on the day t + k.

For each quintile formed on the day t, we compute the percentage of cryptocurrencies that belong to each quintile formed on the day t + k. For each horizon k, the table reports the time-series averages of these transition probabilities. The rows correspond to quintiles formed at time t, and the columns correspond to quintiles formed at time t + k.

Panel A: CVVOL7 remain in the same quintile after 1, 14, and 30 days with similar probabilities of 78.8%, 40.6%, and 37.1%. Panel B reports results for the coefficient of variation measure using a 30-day rolling window. It demonstrates that the transition probabilities of staying in the lowest (highest) quintile become even higher and are monotonically decreasing from 92.7%

(90.4%) to 61.3% (56.9%) and 46.6% (39.8%) when we look at the lags from 1 day to 14 and 30 days. In general, cryptocurrencies belonging to the portfolio with the lowest (highest) coefficient of variation tend to stay in the same quintile and have the highest chances to move to the nearest quintile. This suggests that the coefficient of variation of volume is a highly persistent metric. Nevertheless, one can still speculate that transaction costs can limit the significant abnormal returns of the zero-cost strategy, which is rebalanced on a daily basis. We investigate this possibility in robustness checks.

In this section, we examine the relation between variations in trading activity and expected returns after controlling for the well-known characteristics used to explain cryptocurrency prices: market beta, size, past performance, network-to-market premium, and alpha. Our goal is to disentangle the effect of trading activity variability from common risks. To do so, we construct bivariate portfolios by sorting cryptocurrencies into three unequal-sized groups (30% lowest, 40% middle, 30% highest) using the control variable and then creating quintiles within each third based on the coefficient of variation measures. By construction, the first (fifth) quintile consists of cryptocurrencies with the lowest (highest) variation of the trading volume in each control group. We then calculate the average return of a particular quintile across three groups of a control variable.

Table 6 reports value-and equal-weighted portfolio average returns across the three control groups. Controlling for size, Panel A (Panel B) shows that the average return spread between the extreme CVVOL7 (CVVOL30) quintiles is 17.15% (17.56%) with a significantly positive t-statistic of 7.60 (7.96). In both cases, the return difference also generates a significantly large and positive three-factor alpha of 8.04% and 8.25% per month. Thus, market capitalization cannot explain the abnormal returns of the strategy exploiting the variations in trading activity. Controlling past performance, the results indicate that the cumulative return over the past seven or thirty days cannot explain the return difference of quintiles with the lowest and highest coefficients of variation. Additionally, conditional alpha and beta fail to substantially diminish the average return spread. Finally, we control the network-to-market ratio of individual cryptocurrencies. As shown in the previous section, the network-to-market measure monotonically decreases across portfolios and, hence, the variation of trading volume can be conceived as a proxy for the network effects.

In sum, the comparison between the results of univariate sorts in  This table reports double-sorted, value-and equal-weighted portfolios formed daily based on the coefficient of variation of volume after controlling for market beta (BETA), market capitalization (SIZE), past 7-and 30day momentum (MOM7 and MOM30), alpha (ALPHA), and the book-to-market ratio (BM) between September 1, 2017, and September 1, 2022. We now investigate the connection between variations in trading activity and volumerelated cryptocurrency characteristics. First, we construct dependent bivariate portfolios controlling for the level of dollar trading volume and turnover. The reason is that the coefficient of variation measures may be highly correlated with the level of trading activity, which can explain our results. Second, fluctuations in trading volume may be driven by abnormally high or low trades relative to the historical data. Thus, we capture this channel This table reports double-sorted, value-and equal-weighted portfolios formed daily based on the coefficient of variation of volume after controlling for volume (VOL), turnover (TURN), volume shocks (VSHOCK), bidask spread (BA), Amihud and realized Amihud illiquidity measures (ILLIQ and RILLIQ) between September 1, 2017, and September 1, 2022. Third, we control for liquidity effects by using the bid-ask spread, Amihud and realized Amihud illiquidity measures.

Table 7 reports the average returns of the CVVOL portfolios. It shows that the differences between the low and high CVVOL quintiles remain economically and statistically significant, ranging from 12.95% (8.34%) per month with a t-statistic of 5.43 (3.23) for volume shocks to 15.29% (13.13%) per month with a t-statistics of 5.80 (4.99) for realized Amihud (bid-ask spread) in case of the value-weighted CVVOL7 (CVVOL30) strategy. The corresponding values for the equal-weighted return differences are generally higher. These results demonstrate that volume-related characteristics cannot explain the low returns to high CVVOL cryptocurrencies, through they partially capture the observed effect.

Thus, cryptocurrencies with the highest variation in volume (turnover) tend to generate lottery-like payoffs. If investors have a preference for such assets, they would be willing to pay higher prices, leading to lower future returns. Thus, lottery demand among investors would explain the negative relation between CVVOL (CVTURN) and expected returns.

Table 8 tests this hypothesis by controlling for the maximum daily return during the past 7 and 30 days in double-sorted portfolios. When controlling for maximum daily returns, the raw return and alpha differences based on the CVVOL30 measure become smaller relative to the CVVOL7 case. However, they remain economically large and statistically significant at all conventional levels.

Furthermore, Table 4 demonstrates striking monotonic patterns in idiosyncratic and realized volatility (IVOL and RVOL) across quintiles. Thus, it is plausible that IVOL and RVOL are proxying for the effect of trading activity variability. Table 8 explore this possibility. There is a stronger effect of controlling for idiosyncratic risk and realized volatility measures in value-weighted portfolios. The average raw and risk adjusted difference returns tend to decrease more in the case of the coefficient of variation estimated over the past 30 days. For instance, the value-weighted CVVOL30 (CVVOL7) difference returns controlled by IVOL, RVOL7, and RVOL30 have the mean of 7.28%, 10.33%, and 10.16% (12.86%, 14.12%, and 13.61%) per month with t-statistics of 2. 74, 3.74, and 3.87 (4.74, 5.74, and 5.11) , respectively.

By construction, maximum daily returns, idiosyncratic and realized volatility measures are highly correlated and, hence, it is not surprising that all of them simultaneously have a strong effect. This evidence coupled with liquidity results further suggests limits-toarbitrage as a likely explanation of our findings. Indeed, idiosyncratic risk is the primary arbitrage cost according to the extant literature. Intuitively, idiosyncratic risk delays mispricing because undiversified arbitrageurs take small positions in high idiosyncratic risk assets. We now explore the possibility that investor interest can help explain the relationship between the variation of trading activity and cryptocurrency returns. This hypothesis is motivated by the clientele effect in equity returns (Merton, 1987) . In cryptocurrency markets, we do not have information on the holdings of individuals or institutions. For each case, we first sort the cryptocurrencies into three unequal-sized groups (30% lowest, 40% middle, 30% highest) using the control variable, then within each third, we sort cryptocurrencies into quintile portfolios based on the coefficient of variation measures calculated over the 7-day (Panel A) or 30-day (Panel B) rolling windows. As shown in Section 2.5, the average number of new and active addresses is decreasing in the variability of trading activity. In unreported results, we document, at best, a weakly positive relation between new (active) addresses and expected returns. Consequently, a larger user network could be associated with lower abnormal variation in trading activity, which would explain the negative relation of the latter with future returns. Table 9 presents double-sorted portfolios when controlling for the average number of new and active addresses over the past 7-or 30-day period. The results are not materially different from those provided for the univariate sorts.

We also test whether downside risk can explain the negative relation between the variation of trading activity and expected returns. Zhang et al. (2021) demonstrate that cryp-tocurrencies with higher downside risk have higher returns. As shown in Section 2.5, the highest (lowest) CVVOL and CVTURN assets tend to have higher value-at-risk and expected shortfall statistics. Thus, if downside risk was a plausible explanation of the documented effect, we should have observed the opposite association, making a downside risk premium an unlikely explanation. Table 9 shows that controlling for VaR95 and ES95 only slightly reduces the average return and alpha of the difference returns, having a stronger effect on the CVVOL30 portfolio.

The single-and double-sorted portfolios demonstrate a significant negative relation between variations in trading activity and expected returns, which cannot be subsumed by other cryptocurrency characteristics. We now examine this cross-sectional relation using the standard Fama and MacBeth (1973) regressions. 5 Each day, we estimate a crosssectional regression of one-day-ahead cryptocurrency returns on the coefficient of variation of volume and control variables, one at a time. We then compute the time-series averages of the intercept and slope coefficients, their Newey and West (1986) adjusted t-statistics, and average R-squared statistics.

Table 10 reports the results. The cross-sectional analysis confirms a statistically significant negative relationship between variations in trading activity and future cryptocurrency returns. In the univariate regression of one-day-ahead returns on CVVOL, the average slope coefficient is -0.0052 (-0.0028) with a t-statistic of -9.28 (-7.39) for the case of the 7day (30-day) rolling window. When we add control variables one at a time, the CVVOL coefficient tends to decrease but remains significant in all cases. Consistent with dependant double-sorted portfolios, the economic magnitude and statistical significance of the CVVOL slope weaken the most when controlling for volume shocks, past maximum daily return, liquidity and volatility measures. Although the CVVOL effect becomes weaker, it remains statistically significant at all conventional confidence levels. We further multiply the CVVOL estimates by the spread in median CVVOL characteristics between the lowest and highest CVVOL quintiles to proxy for the CVVOL risk premium. The last row in Table 10 .
