Considering that the failure of large stablecoins built upon inappropriate decentralized finance frameworks (e.g. lack of collateral and dependencies with liquidity providers) could give rise to potential financial stability risks, we believe that our results are relevant for both investors and policymakers. Applications of stablecoins within the crypto space remarkably increased since 2020, while poorly supported by adequate regulation. Initially, they were used as: (i) bridge between fiat currencies and crypto-assets; (ii) "parking space" for crypto volatility (Adachi et al., 2022) . Nowadays, they are also used to provide most of the liquidity in DeFi applications such as decentralised exchanges and lending protocols (e.g. UST and Anchor protocol). Consequently, stablecoins play a critical role within the crypto space, where they are involved in almost 75% of the entire trading (Adachi al., 2021) . Largest stablecoins represent a primary source of risk according to the European Central Bank (ECB), which states that a "bank run" on Tether could disrupt trading and price discovery in crypto-asset markets, which could turn disorderly (ECB, 2022). Therefore, even though the collapse of UST was not able to rise relevant herding effects, the failure of larger stablecoins could generate systemic effects on the whole crypto universe. In addition, considering the growing interlinks between crypto-assets and the traditional financial system, the failure of stablecoins could also have implications external to the crypto space. These scenarios remark the need for a Global Stablecoin Standard and continuously updated regulations able to guarantee (i) a process for fulfilling holders' redemption claims, (ii) proper transparency of reserve asset composition and (iii) development of appropriate risk-management frameworks. Relevant regulatory standard-setting bodies (Financial Stability Board and Bank of International Settlements; see, e.g. Bank for International Settlements, 2022) and governments (Japan, Hong Kong, United Kingdom, European Union and the United States; see, e.g. Asmakov, 2022 , European Council, 2022) are already proposing robust rules for these digital assets. It would be beneficial that contributions from inside the DeFi community would also be proposed.

Most of the existing literature on stablecoins concerns their stability (Grobys et al., 2021) , their role in portfolio diversification (Wang et al., 2020; Baur and Hoang, 2021) , and crypto asset price formation (Barucci et al., 2022; Kristoufek, 2022) . Due to the nascent interest in this research field and the rapid growth of decentralised finance, gaps in the literature still exist. Recent events around Terra project highlights the need to analyse specific topics that have been neglected until now: (i) stablecoins as a means of exchange, (ii) stablecoins' role within the crypto space, (iii) regulation on stablecoins and related protocols and (iv) potential risk to financial stability. First, stablecoins could not be appropriate as a mean of exchange due to their vulnerability to "bank runs" and high transaction costs compared to non-blockchain alternatives (Mizrach, 2022) . Second, the role of stablecoins as "bridge" in the crypto space could be considered a double-edged sword since they could jeopardise the system's stability once becoming systemic. Third, the dependence of UST on Anchor protocol remarks the need for better scrutiny of third party protocols related to any stablecoin. Finally, it would be necessary to consider scenarios in which specific fragilities within the stablecoins' ecosystem may give rise to systemic financial stability risks.

In this paper, we review the Terra project's main features and describe the mechanisms that led to its failure. Our contribution to the existing literature is threefold. We first systematically organise news from heterogeneous sources to reconstruct a reliable timeline for the Terra project's collapse. We hence identify four main breakpoints for the crash and, starting from hourly data for 61 cryptocurrencies from Kraken digital currency exchange, we quantitatively characterise them. We exploit the power of smoothed weighted correlations to build state-of-the-art graph structures which demonstrate to be both able to efficiently capture dependency structures among cryptocurrencies and robust to extreme market conditions. As a further element of novelty, we enforce our analysis using transaction data to detect relevant micro-structural market events.

Combining these last two approaches, we uncover BTC's reference role during the first phase of the collapse. In particular, we outline the existence of intense selling pressure on this crypto asset and identify 05 May 2022 as the potential fuse for the process that led to the Terra project's failure. We remark that it is impossible to conclude that this event and the Terra project's collapse were part of a coordinated strategy (del Castillo, 2022) . The weakness of the current global economy (e.g. Russia -Ukraine conflict (Boungou and Yatié, 2022) , bear markets in the main financial indices (Krauskopf, 2022) , and higher federal funds rates (Cox, 2022) ) could have caused "the perfect storm" in the cryptocurrency market. Moreover, it is relevant to consider that the vicious dependence of the Terra project on the Anchor protocol could have increased its exposure to heterogeneous speculative strategies that occurred simultaneously by chance. We also show how, after 07 May 2022, LUNA is quickly marginalised by the rest of the network's components. This last finding enforces the conjecture that investors considered this collapse a non-structural shock, which is supported by the impossibility of detecting a herding behaviour during the down market.

The cryptocurrency market harbours a hidden risk: assets that silently disappear from trading, leaving investors stranded. These 'zombie' cryptocurrencies technically exist but become temporarily untradable on exchanges, ranging from weeks to permanent delisting. This study predicts which cryptocurrencies are at risk of becoming zombies using predictors derived from return statistics, trading volume, market capitalisation, and asset-specific features. Our sample includes cryptocurrencies listed for at least 210 days between January 2015 and December 2022. We employ various machine learning algorithms and novel explainable AI (XAI) tools, including permutation-based feature importance and partial dependence plots (PDPs), to identify and analyse key factors influencing zombification risk. Our machine learning models achieve 84% out-of-time balanced accuracy in predicting whether a cryptocurrency will become a zombie within the next 28 days. Tree-based approaches, particularly random forests, significantly outperform traditional econometric methods. Trading volumes and past returns emerge as the most influential predictors.

Cryptocurrencies are rapidly evolving over time, becoming increasingly prominent in global financial markets (Ahmed et al., 2024; Fan Fang & Wu, 2021) . While cryptos attract significant interest from both investors and academics (Almeida & Gonçalves, 2023) , most studies focus on the best-performing cryptocurrencies -the market leaders (Będowska-Sójka & Kliber, 2021; Beneki et al., 2019; Gaies et al., 2024; Yousaf et al., 2024) . Far less attention is paid to those who struggle or disappear from the market (Galati et al., 2024; Martins, 2024) . In this study, we examine cryptocurrencies that become temporarily untradable, a phenomenon first highlighted in financial media (Kharif, 2022) as 'crypto zombies'. We expand this concept to include all cryptocurrencies that lose their tradability, regardless of their listing status or potential for market recovery.

We contribute to the existing literature in several ways. First, while previous studies have developed models to predict cryptocurrency bankruptcy (Fantazzini, 2023; Feder et al., 2018; Grobys & Sapkota, 2020; Ma et al., 2022) , our research broadens this scope to include cryptocurrencies we classify as zombies, which experience no trading for up to four weeks without being officially delisted. This expanded focus captures both permanent and interim inactivity, offering a more holistic view of risks specific to cryptocurrencies. Second, we enrich the approach of Gu et al. (2020) , Ma et al. (2022) and Milunovich and Lee (2022) by performing a comparative analysis of machine learning methods. Similar to Grobys and Sapkota (2020) , we focus on the prediction of the emergence of zombies based on historical data on prices and volumes, but also extend the range of variables, including less obvious measures. Contrary to their work, we show that distinguishing between proof-of-work and proof-of-stake does not add any value in predicting which crypto will become a zombie. Moreover, we show that adding more features to the model has limited benefit in predicting becoming zombies. Third, to make our machine learning models interpretable (Tang et al., 2024) , we employ eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) tools to analyse these black box algorithms. Thus, we can indicate the most important predictors which are behind becoming a zombie. Our results show that the most important variables are based on volume. Fourth, we contribute to the literature by analysing a relatively long period from the emergence of cryptocurrencies until the end of 2022 and consider all cryptocurrencies traded within this period, thus avoiding the survivorship bias.

To achieve this, we constructed a database consisting of weekly snapshots of nearly 9.5 K cryptocurrencies listed in the period from January 2015 to December 2022. We then perform the empirical exercise, in which we apply several machine learning techniques such as logistic regression, LASSO, random forests and extreme gradient boosting to predict the 'zombie'-state of the crypto within the next 28 days. A variety of performance measures are used to indicate the best model in terms of prediction accuracy.

The detection of potential zombies is important for several reasons. First, it helps to protect investors from misguided investments. Second, avoiding those cryptocurrencies that may disappear from the market or become non-tradable for a given period is critical for risk management. Third, knowing which cryptocurrencies are at risk of becoming zombies would be beneficial for anyone building a portfolio of cryptocurrencies. Tools to identify such risk are important, especially in a rapidly evolving market with constantly changing regulations (Arner et al., 2024 ). foot_0

In our analysis, we use classification models to estimate the probability that a particular crypto-asset will become a zombie during the next 𝑘 days. To better capture the zombification process, we use 𝑘 ∈ {7, 28}, i.e. we perform the prediction for the next week or the next four weeks. This dual-horizon approach allows us to capture both short-term and more sustained periods of inactivity, providing a built-in robustness check on our findings. Let 𝑇 denote the number of temporal observations recorded and 𝑡 0 the first time point used for analysis. Then at the end of every Sunday in our sample (𝑡 ∈ [𝑡 0 , 𝑇 ]), we define the binary variable 𝑌 𝑘 𝑖,𝑡 for crypto-asset 𝑖 as follows:

where 𝐗 𝑖,𝑡 represents the features matrix for cryptocurrency 𝑖 at time 𝑡 (the features are detailed in Section 3). The models are estimated and evaluated using cross-validation on a rolling basis with 7 iterations. The whole dataset is divided into training and testing samples based on time. All model development and validation steps are conducted separately for each zombie definition (𝑘 = 7 and 𝑘 = 28), and results for both thresholds are compared throughout the study.

In each iteration, we use a rolling 3-year period to train the model, and then check its performance on the out-of-time validation sample covering the next 6 months, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Averaged results on the seven out-of-time validation periods (2019H1 -2022H1) are used to tune hyperparameters with the grid search and select the best modelling approach (Hastie et al., 2009) based on the balanced accuracy metric. Depending on the algorithm, we consider from 200 to almost 300 variants of the model (combinations of the values of the hyperparameters). 2 The final training sample from 2019H2 to 2022H1 (dark green background on the plot) was used to re-estimate the best model on the newer data and verify its performance on the final test sample (2022H2).

We implement a range of widely adopted machine learning algorithms (Almeida & Gonçalves, 2023; Dudek et al., 2024; Nagula & Alexakis, 2022) and compare them using various performance metrics. First, we apply the standard logistic regression (henceforth GLM), which serves as a simple benchmark. We also use the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) (Tibshirani, 1996) , which is an extension of the GLM, that penalises features that have low predictive power by shrinking their coefficients towards zero. Some of the coefficients might even be set to zero, which is why LASSO applies variable selection. We also apply two tree-based models, such as random forests (RF) and extreme gradient boosting (XGB). Random forest (Breiman, 2001) combines the predictions of multiple independent decision trees, while extreme gradient boosting (Chen & Guestrin, 2016) combines the predictions of multiple decision trees estimated sequentially.

Comparing various ML approaches in fraud detection (Achakzai & Juan, 2022) or mispricing identification in financial markets (Hanauer et al., 2022) is a common practice. For each approach, a variety of classification performance measures is applied. The area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) is a single metric indicating the performance of a model independently of the selected cut-off point. We also take into account balanced accuracy, which is the arithmetic average of sensitivity and specificity and better reflects the predictive performance of models in the case of highly imbalanced data than simple accuracy (Yan et al., 2022) . In the context of our study, specificity counts the share of correctly predicted non-zombies among all real tradable coins, while sensitivity counts the share of correctly predicted zombies among all real zombies. This metric is crucial because misclassifying a zombie as an active cryptocurrency carries higher potential costs for investors than the opposite error.

Lastly, but not least, we apply explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) tools to identify the most important features as predictors of being a zombie and understand their impact on the outcome variable. We use model-agnostic methods that are easily comparable independently of the structure of a model. First, the permutation-based feature importance metric is used to identify important predictors. It calculates how the selected model's performance measure changes if the values in a particular feature are randomly permuted. Second, we use partial dependence profiles that show how the expected model prediction changes with respect to the values of the most important explanatory variable, keeping all other predictors constant (Molnar, 2022) .

Our sample is based on all cryptocurrencies listed on CoinMarketCap, available at www.coinmarketcap.com from January 1, 2013, to December 31, 2022. CoinMarketCap provides data on cryptocurrency prices, volumes, market capitalisation and circulating supply. It utilises different algorithms to filter out outliers and avoid inaccurate data (CoinMarketCap, 2023) . This database combines information from hundreds of various crypto exchanges to gather a wide range of data. Despite some comments on the veracity of the information contained (Alexander & Dakos, 2020) , it continues to be the most widely used database in the research community (Anghel, 2021; Burggraf, 2021) .

It is important to acknowledge that, while CoinMarketCap is a leading aggregator and is widely used in academic research, its data coverage and listing criteria have evolved over time. These changes may introduce biases in historical series -particularly regarding the inclusion or removal of assets and the timing of market coverage for certain cryptocurrencies (Alexander & Dakos, 2020) . Such dynamics may affect the completeness, continuity, and comparability of our sample, especially for earlier years or less prominent assets. To assess the robustness of our findings, we have compared summary statistics and inactivity patterns with CoinGecko, another major data aggregator, and found that core trends -such as the relative frequency of inactive assets and the prominence of volume-based predictors -are consistent across sources. Nonetheless, we explicitly acknowledge that certain microlevel discrepancies or changes in data aggregation could affect fine-grained results, and we encourage future work to explore direct exchange-level data or blended sources to further validate these findings. Out of all the cryptocurrencies available in the sample period, we chose those that were listed uninterruptedly for at least 210 days. We require sufficiently long time series to obtain reliable estimates of higher-order statistical measures, including kurtosis, skewness, and the parameters of the 𝛼-stable distribution. Cryptocurrencies that maintain continuous market quotation coverage on CoinMarketCap are classified here as 'active'. Based on the available data, we were able to indicate when particular cryptos were traded and when they stopped being covered and became 'inactive'. If the loss in coverage lasted for at least 28 days, they were classified as zombies.

Apart from historical quotations, CoinMarketCap also provides some metadata on each symbol, including the date of its first and last quotation. This information was used to calculate the number of added (or new) cryptocurrencies, actively traded cryptos, as well as those which become untradable for each day of our sample.

Fig. 2 shows the cumulative number of cryptocurrencies that gained coverage on CoinMarketCap and those that lost it, which made them zombies. We also show a number of currently traded cryptos obtained as a cumulative difference between the former and the latter. These assets exhibit regular price movements and substantial trading volumes, indicating consistent market participation. In contrast, inactive cryptos experience periods without market quotation coverage, typically characterised by extended intervals of zero trading volume and static prices. The total number of cryptocurrencies has increased exponentially over time, whereas the number of zombie cryptocurrencies, after an initial rise, has stabilised since 2020. Active cryptos predominate in the market, reflecting the ongoing development and maturation of the cryptocurrency ecosystem. Zombie cryptocurrencies, which have lost market quotation coverage, represent a smaller proportion of the total sample, confirming the developing status of the market. This imbalance between the number of active cryptos and zombies requires applying the correction mechanisms in the empirical part.

The distribution of the life length of zombies is presented in Fig. 3 . It shows that the possibility of losing market quotation coverage decreases the longer it maintains price tracking in the market. This argues for the inclusion of a variable measuring the length of quotations in the model.

We have created a database of weekly snapshots comprising all cryptocurrencies with available historical data, including those that subsequently lost market quotation coverage. For every Sunday between 2015-01-04 and 2022-12-25 and every cryptocurrency, we obtained a set of measures based on historical returns, trading volumes, and market capitalisation from the preceding 28 days (4 weeks) and 182 days (26 weeks, ca. half a year). The set of measures consists of statistics including mean, median, minimum, maximum, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis, first quartile of the distribution (Q1) and third quartile (Q3).

In addition, based only on returns and only on the previous 182 days, we calculated the Hurst exponent, 𝛼-stable tail index, expected shortfall (ES) and value-at-risk (VaR). The Hurst exponent, which captures long-range dependence, has been observed to vary with the stage of market development. In Bitcoin's early years, the Hurst exponent was high, indicating strong long-range dependence. Over time, it has stabilised and diminished, suggesting the market has transitioned to a more developed stage (Bariviera et al., 2017) . The 𝛼-stable distributions, commonly used to model heavy-tailed and asymmetric data, can provide a robust framework for analysing cryptocurrency market behaviour (Raubitzek & Neubauer, 2021) . Furthermore, ES and VaR are crucial risk measures that can help evaluate potential losses beyond the expected risk level (Dutta et al., 2020) . The variables described above are used in the basic specification of the model (S1).

Next, we expand a list of predictors by four fixed categorical predictors used in similar studies (Fantazzini, 2023; Grobys & Sapkota, 2020; Gu et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2022) . Specification 2 (S2), in addition to S1, includes the category of the crypto-asset, namely coin versus token, two dummy variables indicating whether a symbol has a Twitter (X) and separate subreddit account, and the age of the cryptocurrency in days (since its first quotation). The widest Specification 3 (S3) includes three additional dummy variables based on tags provided by CoinMarketCap, indicating if a symbol is mineable, proof-of-stake (pos) or proof-of-work (pow).

The list of all characteristics of cryptocurrencies used as predictors in our analysis is presented in Table 1 . We divide them into two categories: continuous predictors and categorical ones. We end up with a set of 58 predictors for Specification 1, 62 for Specification 2, and 65 for Specification 3. While some predictors may exhibit high mutual correlation, this issue is addressed by the regularisation features inherent in the machine learning algorithms we use in the empirical part.

In this section, we present the empirical results of our analysis using the rolling window approach to train and validate the models. Before we delve into these results, it is important to note that our dependent variable distribution is highly imbalanced. The share of cryptos becoming untradable within the next 28 days by week is illustrated in Fig. 4 . At the beginning of the analysed period, around 5% of cryptos were becoming zombies. By mid-2019, the share of untradable symbols decreased to ca. 2.5%. Starting from the second half of 2019, the share of zombies dropped below 0.5%. Due to the highly imbalanced distribution of the dependent variable, we apply three alternative approaches to mitigate the problem and rebalance the training data. These are: simple downsampling (in short DOWN), Synthetic Minority Over-Sampling Technique (SMOTE) (Chawla et al., 2002) , and Random Over-Sampling Examples (ROSE) (Menardi & Torelli, 2012) .

Results presented in Table 2 are obtained from the rolling validation samples approach. They show the performance measures for models predicting becoming a zombie within the next 28 days -averaged over seven validation periods. We find that the simplest specification S1, including predictors based only on the statistical properties of returns, volumes, and market capitalisation, performs as well as or better than more complex models using additional categorical features and the age of the cryptocurrency (S2 and S3).

In terms of the AUROC statistic, random forest seems to slightly outperform the other approaches for all three specifications, in particular for SMOTE and DOWN. However, models trained on simply downsampled training data are based on limited information (a smaller sample) compared to the remaining approaches. Random forest trained with SMOTE rebalancing has, in turn, the lowest balanced accuracy and sensitivity out of all compared alternatives. Considering these two metrics, and sensitivity in particular, it seems that a random forest trained with ROSE rebalancing is the best-performing approach, followed by the xgboost (also with ROSE).

The results indicate that the tree-based machine learning models, which catch potential non-linearities and interactions between predictors, perform better than traditional econometric models (GLM) and their extensions (LASSO). We select a random forest trained with ROSE rebalancing as the single best approach consistently for all three specifications and use it later for reasoning about feature importance and the shape of relationships between predictors and the probability of becoming a zombie.

We compare the performance metrics of all applied algorithms and specifications for the ROSE approach on the test sample (Table 3 ). These metrics confirm that the single best random forest still performs very well with a balanced accuracy of 84%. Although its AUROC is lower compared to GLM or LASSO, it is still very high (88%-90% depending on the specification). We can also see out-of-time sensitivity equal to 100%.

The high predictive accuracies naturally raise the concern of potential overfitting. We explicitly acknowledge this issue. Two aspects of our research design help mitigate it. First, model development relied on a rolling-window scheme with strictly out-oftime validation before evaluating performance on a final test period. This design prevents information leakage across subsamples and ensures that reported test results are based on genuinely unseen data. The observed perfect sensitivity in the test set reflects the extremely rare incidence of zombie cases during this period (16 out of 59,000 observations), all of which were captured by the models. While this may suggest ''too good to be true'' performance, it is largely a consequence of the class imbalance and the limited number of positive cases rather than an indication of overfitting. Second, we carefully evaluated different rebalancing methods because imbalanced data can lead to models that appear over-optimistic in fit but generalise poorly. the models to learn decision boundaries that are sensitive to the minority class, which in turn mitigates the risk that excellent performance is driven merely by the overwhelming majority of non-zombie cases. Nevertheless, we caution that the limited number of zombie events in the test period restricts the precision with which sensitivity can be evaluated. Future work with richer test samples or at the exchange-level could further validate the robustness of our findings.

For comparison, we show the results obtained for predicting whether a symbol will become untradable during the next 7 days. Table 4 shows the performance of all algorithms in the 7 averaged validation samples. Overall, the results do not differ much from the results of predictions for 28 days ahead. The values of all metrics are slightly higher than for 28 days, which, in line with intuition, indicates that predicting the default of a cryptocurrency a week before is easier than 28 days in advance. Simpler specifications with fewer predictors also seem to have higher predictive performance than more complex models that include more information about the cryptocurrencies.

Again, the performance of tree-based models, particularly the random forest trained on a ROSE rebalanced sample, is much higher than for the linear specifications. This suggests that they better identify the shape of relationships than the traditional fixedspecification models. Tree-based models are adept at capturing complex, non-linear interactions between variables, which likely accounts for their superior performance in these predictive tasks.

The performance metrics of all applied algorithms and specifications for the ROSE approach on the test sample (Table 5 ) confirm that the single best random forest still performs very well, with a balanced accuracy of up to 86%. Although its AUROC is again lower compared to GLM or LASSO, we can also see an out-of-time sensitivity of 100%.

To better understand the results, we analyse variable importance based on the previously mentioned best-performing model predicting becoming a zombie within the next 28 days -random forest, trained on a ROSE rebalanced sample. We use permutationbased feature importance. Fig. 5 shows the top 10 predictors for each specification. The top two predictors are the same, regardless of the specification: the minimum (log) volume during the last 182 days (approximately half a year) and the minimum (log) volume in The table reports the performance measures of several machine learning models in the prediction of a cryptocurrency becoming a zombie in the 7 averaged validation samples based on 3 alternative specifications, S1, S2 and S3, differentiated by the lists of predictors and three alternative approaches to rebalancing (rebal.). the last month (28 days). The importance of these two features is much higher than that of the remaining ones. This is an important indication for investors -the inactivity in cryptocurrency trading is often preceded by a decline in trading volume. This is justified in the financial literature -volumes are often used as proxies for liquidity (Będowska-Sójka, 2019; Domowitz et al., 2001) , and the decrease in liquidity is usually a bad signal for investors. The maximum (log) volume from the last 182 days ranks as the third or fourth most important predictor. Among the predictors based on returns, the median return from the last 182 days appears to be one of the most important features in the first two specifications. The only variable not based on the statistical properties of returns, volumes, or market caps that appears among the top predictors is the category (in the second specification). Knowing which features influence the probability of becoming untradable the most, we want to identify the shape of their relationships with the outcome of the model. To assess this, we calculated their marginal effects (ceteris paribus) and presented them as Partial Dependency Profiles (PDPs) in Fig. 6 . The analysis is based on relationships observed in the final test sample (2022H2) within the first specification, S1, and the final best random forest model. For comparison, we also show PDPs based on the LASSO model trained with the ROSE rebalancing.

Fig. 6 shows that for the minimum logarithms of volumes, both in the last 182 days (log_volume_min182) and in the last 28 days (log_volume_min28), the relationship is decreasing and monotonic. The larger the minimum trading volume, the lower the risk that a cryptocurrency will become a zombie soon. This is in line with the previous findings in the literature showing that volume helps predict the behaviour of prices (Anastasiou et al., 2021; Balcilar et al., 2017) . Our findings enrich the previous ones, showing the impact of the short-term (28 days) and long-term (128 days) volume measures. However, in contrast to the LASSO approach, which suggests an almost constant slope and thus a linear shape, a random forest shows a clear threshold for the 6-month minimum or several thresholds for the minimum of the last 28 days. If the minimum trading volume from the last 6 months is above approximately 2000 (log volume above 7.5), the risk of becoming a zombie is at a stable and very low level. For lower values, it sharply increases, reaching a plateau of approximately 38% for a minimum volume below 15 (log volume lower than 2.5). The first part of the variable name refers to a quantity, such as volume, market cap, or returns (r), the second to a descriptive statistic, such as minimum, maximum, mean, median, Q1 or Q3, and the last to the number of days for which the statistic is determined. Among the variables, volume_min_182 is used for the minimum volume calculated within 182 days. volume_min_28 stands for the minimum volume within 28 days, volume_q1_28 is for the first quantile of the volume distribution, etc. case of the last month (28 days), the probability of becoming a zombie is extremely low only for symbols with a minimum volume above 150k (log volume higher than 12).

The relationship between the maximum logarithm of volume from the last 182 days (log_volume_max182) and the outcome probability has a non-monotonic shape based on a random forest. In contrast, LASSO shows a lack of influence of this variable on the risk of becoming a zombie, represented by a flat line. This illustrates the advantage of using non-linear tree-based models. The random forest confirms that the risk of becoming untradable does not depend on the maximum volume for a wide range of its values. However, a cryptocurrency with an extremely large maximum volume in the last 6 months seems to be less risky. Extremely high volume indicates strong market interest and liquidity, making it easier for the project to survive market downturns (Będowska-Sójka, 2019; Madhavan, 2000) . Large volume spikes might reflect institutional interest, providing more financial stability (Goh & Jeon, 2025) . We also find that the risk appears to decrease for very low maximum volumes, which is less intuitive and thus requires further explanation. Cryptos in specific, stable niches that serve particular solutions or utilise specialised decentralised finance (DeFi) protocols might have lower volumes in general (Gadzinski & Liuzzi, 2025) . Such projects may have dedicated user bases that do not require high trading volumes to remain viable. Low-volume cryptos might be more focused on development and utility rather than speculation (Dunbar & Owusu-Amoako, 2023) . They also might indicate concentrated ownership by committed long-term holders. Note: PDPs for random forest and LASSO for comparison, both trained with ROSE rebalancing and applied on the test data. log_volume_min182 stands for the minimum log volume from the last 182 days, log_volume_min28 is for the last 28 days, log_volume_max182 is the maximum of log volumes from the last 182 days, while r_median182 stands for median returns based on recent 182 days.

Last but not least, the relationship between the median of returns during the last 182 days (r_median182) and the risk of becoming a zombie also shows a non-monotonic shape, resembling an inverted parabola with a peak around zero. This result suggests that cryptos whose prices do not move much for an extended period or fluctuate around the same level for 6 months are potentially more risky in terms of becoming inactive. This is in line with findings of Będowska-Sójka (2021), according to which zero returns are usually related to a lack of trading activity and thus lower liquidity. As the latter is considered a risk factor, the probability of becoming a zombie is higher.

In Fig. 7 , we also present the Partial Dependency Profile for the category of crypto, which was slightly important in our second specification. However, based on the random forest, the difference in predicted probability of becoming a zombie (having all other characteristics the same) is only marginally higher for the tokens as compared with the coins. Overall, the analysis shows that all of the identified features can be useful indicators of potential risk related to a particular cryptocurrency.

In this study, we have conducted an empirical exercise using several machine-learning approaches intended to forecast the probability of a cryptocurrency becoming a zombie within the next period of 7 or 28 days. We deal with risks that investors face due to the disappearance of cryptocurrencies from the market, trying to predict such a situation.

Our results support both hypotheses presented in the Introduction. We were able to predict if a cryptocurrency would become a zombie within the next 28 days with a relatively high, balanced accuracy rate of 84%. We found that tree-based algorithms, which are capable of identifying complex, potentially non-linear, and even non-monotonic relationships, are better choices for detecting zombies. The best-performing algorithm is a random forest, with top variables including minimum volume during the last 182 days and 28 days, maximum volume in the last 182 days, and median return in 182 days. We also discovered that simpler specifications, based purely on the statistical characteristics of prices and volumes, have comparable or even better predictive performance than models incorporating the age of the cryptocurrency or categorical predictors, such as the category of the symbol, having an X (former Twitter) account, being mineable, or being based on POS or POW. The only additional feature identified as slightly important was whether the cryptocurrency is a coin or a token.

Our findings are particularly significant from the investors' perspective. The ability to predict whether a cryptocurrency is likely to become a zombie within the next 28 days provides investors with critical information to make more informed decisions. Investors can use the insights from our model to identify high-risk assets and avoid potential losses. Our model relies on readily available market data such as trading volumes and returns, making it practical and implementable for both individual and institutional investors.

For market regulators, our model offers valuable insights into the risk factors associated with cryptocurrency investments. By understanding the predictors of a cryptocurrency becoming a zombie, regulators can develop better policies to protect market participants. The high accuracy of our model in forecasting zombie cryptocurrencies can help regulators in early detection and intervention, potentially preventing significant market disruptions. Regulators may develop automated monitoring systems that flag cryptocurrencies showing early signs of potential inactivity based on the model's indicators. They may also impose additional disclosures or warnings for cryptocurrencies showing concerning patterns. They may provide information about which cryptocurrencies qualify for institutional investment products. Thus, as regulations in the cryptocurrency market continue to evolve, our model can serve as a tool for regulators to identify and monitor high-risk assets, thereby enhancing market stability and investor protection.

Since volume and price data are readily available to investors, our results can be used to build early warning systems to predict cryptocurrency inactivity. Our findings may help investors avoid investments in non-viable projects, as well as detect suspicious cryptocurrencies. From a forward-looking perspective, directing investment toward viable cryptocurrencies and avoiding failing ones improves overall market efficiency. By avoiding non-viable cryptos, investors reduce overall volatility and tail risks in the financial system. Overall, our model provides a practical and effective means for both investors and regulators to navigate the volatile cryptocurrency market, mitigate risks, and make more informed decisions based on robust empirical evidence.

In this study, we focus on predicting the likelihood of cryptocurrencies becoming zombies, defined as assets that either disappear from the market or become untradable. While our approach provides valuable insights, certain limitations should be acknowledged. We rely on data from CoinMarketCap, a widely recognised and frequently used source in cryptocurrency research. However, its evolving criteria for including assets may introduce some variability into our sample. Our analysis primarily utilises financial metrics, such as trading volume and returns, as predictors. While these factors are central to understanding zombification, incorporating additional qualitative and fundamental variables could enhance the depth of the analysis. Additionally, examining exchange-level details, such as distinctions between centralised and decentralised platforms, could provide more nuanced insights into the dynamics that lead to zombification. In future work, we plan to address these limitations by exploring longer forecast horizons, expanding the set of predictors, and incorporating more granular data on trading activity to improve the understanding of this phenomenon.

This paper analysis the dynamic of the volatility connectedness between three main categories of the cryptocurrency market (mineable coins, non-mineable coins and tokens). 1 Furthermore, it investigates factors (internal and external) that can help to predict the net directional and total volatility connectedness indices. We find that the CC market is highly connected where mining coins dominate the CC market and are typically net transmitter of shocks. For the non-mining coins, a low connectedness with all mining coins and tokens versus a high connectedness with Stellar are highlighted. The results show also that, unlike previous studies, Ethereum is the dominant contributor to volatility spillover and the only CC net pairwise transmitter in our sample. Interestingly, we find that most of the tokens are highly connected with their platform (Ethereum) rather than bitcoin. Finally, we find that both financial and macroeconomic indicators play an important role in explaining the total and net directional connectedness, a result that can significantly help cryptocurrency market participants and investors improve their risk management and the benefits from diversification.

Over the last few years, CCs 2 have attracted the interest of several types of market participants including miners, investors, portfolio managers, academic researchers and policy makers. The specific features and characteristic of CCs such as their high returns and high time varying volatilities are the main motives behind this (Corbet et al., 2020; Yousaf and Ali, 2021; Mariana et al., 2021; Maouchi et al., 2021; Montasser et al., 2021) . In fact, beyond the high level of returns that can offer CCs, investors have been also interested by the financial opportunities that can generate these new types of assets (digital assets) such as financial diversification and hedging strategies (Corbet et al., 2018 (Corbet et al., , 2020;; Charfeddine et al., 2020; Yousaf and Ali, 2021; Mariana et al., 2021; Maouchi et al., 2021) . Empirically, while the number of research studies investigating the financial aspects (stylized facts, intra-and inter-dependence, and financial diversification) has exploded only recently that academic researchers have been interested to explore the level of financial connectedness involving CCs (Yi et al., 2018; Koutmos, 2018; Ji et al., 2019a) .

This paper try to contribute to this gap in the digital financial literature by investigating the dynamic features of connectedness between different categories of CCs. Moreover, it examines both internal and external factors that can help to predict the total and net directional connectedness indices. An important motivation of this study is the absence of a common classification of different types of CCs. For instance, according to Hardle et al. (2020) , cryptocurrencies may be grouped into seven broad classes. The first class, originally designed as a transaction mechanism, which include for example Bitcoin and Litecoin. Ethereum, Tezos, EOS, and DFinity fall into the second class known as a distributed computation token. The third class of cryptocurrency is called a utility token, which is a programmable blockchain asset (for example, Golem, Storj, Sia and FileCoin). The fourth class of cryptocurrency is a security token. The fifth class is called fungible tokens (for example, ERC-20). A nonfungible token is the sixth classification. In this case, each token is unique and not interchangeable with another (For example, Cryptokitties and Decentraland (LAND). The final class of cryptocurrencies is called stablecoins (For example, tether (USDT), Circle's USDC, LBXPeg).

In this paper, we adopt a more general classification approach as we categorize all cryptocurrnecies into two main categories namely: (1) coins, and (2) tokens. In addition, we divide the coin category into two sub-categories namely mining coins (such as Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), DASH (DASH), Litecoin (LTC)) and the non-mining coins (such as Ripple (XRP), Binance Coin (BNB), EOS (EOS), Stellar (XLM)). Regarding the tokens category and compared to coins, the interest in this second category can be explained by the fact that they have been only recently emerged. The well known and most traded tokens include: Maker (MKR), Basic Attention (BAT), Chainlink (LINK), and Augur (REP) .

Despite that all the CCs (coins and tokens) share some important common features such as the blockchain technology, there exist also some other important characteristics that can distinguish coins from tokens and vis-versa. For instance, coins are primary designed for purpose of decentralized peer-to-peer payments. 3 The main differences between the mineable and non-mineable coins is that contrary to the non-mining coins where the supply of coins is managed by the company offering these coins, the supply for the case of mining coins is endogenously determined by the mining process. The mining process is the process where the miners (individuals or companies) are rewarded when they validate the mining coin transaction. Yet, the tokens are a new form of crowdfunding that offer tokens to investors with the only potential gain is the increase in the price of tokens. Token offerings are smart contracts programmed on distributed ledger technology (Momtaz, 2020) . Furthermore, tokens are applications built upon existing blockchains, whereas coins are created on their own blockchain (Momtaz, 2019) . For further information on the market evolution, offering mechanisms, and token types, see the paper by Momtaz (2019) .

This study has two main objectives. First, it analyzes the intra-cryptocurrency market volatility spillovers. Second, it investigates whether macroeconomic and financial indicators can help in predicting the dynamic behavior of the net directional and total volatility connectedness indices. Several reasons motivate the focus on this research question. Firstly, while there have been a plenty of studies that have tried to explore the level of connectedness between the CCs and traditional financial assets (Corbet et al., 2018; Koutmos, 2018; Yi et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2019a; Billah et al., 2022) , only few have analyzed the connectedness between different type of CCs. Secondly, the recent rapid gaining of interest of non-mining coins and tokens in the last two years has question the dominance and influential position of the mining coins in term of net transmitter/receiver volatility spillover. Thirdly, the recent emergence of a large set of tokens (such as DeFi, decentralized finance) offer some new other potential benefits for markets participants but open new questions, including whether Ethereum, as blockchain platform for almost all the tokens foot_0 will be the more influential in terms of net transmitter/ receiver spillover. Finally, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that attempts to fill these gaps by exploring the relationship and volatility connectedness between different CCs types (coins versus tokens, and mining versus non-mining for coins). Our study classify the different CCs based on their principal characteristics of coins versus tokens, and of mining versus non-mining for the coins. In addition, we explore whether the tokens platforms influence their volatility connectedness.

A further motivation is the lack of studies that have investigated the common factors that can explain the dynamic spillovers between different digital assets. Thus, in addition to the evaluation of the spillovers between the considered cryptocurrencies, this study aims to identify factors (internal to CC market, macroeconomic, financial of the dynamic connectedness cryptocurrencies. We first investigate the determinants of total connectedness. Then, we extend our analysis by investigating the factors affecting the net connectedness for each single cryptocurrency.

In this paper, we report several key findings. First, mining coins dominate the CC market and are typically net transmitter of shocks while the tokens are mostly followers. Secondly, the non-mining coins exhibit a low connectedness with all mining coins and tokens versus a high connectedness with Stellar are highlighted. Further, unlike previous studies, we found that Ethereum is the dominant contributor to volatility spillover and the only CC net pairwise transmitter in our sample. More interestingly, our findings reveal that most of the tokens are highly connected with their platform (Ethereum) rather than bitcoin. Finally, the findings of our paper indicate that several financial and macroeconomic factors significantly contribute in the dynamics the total and net directional connectedness.

In the last few years, a large number of studies in the finance literature has been devoted to studying financial connectedness (returns and volatility) among various assets types. The main motives is their important implications on portfolios performance (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2012, 2014) . Recently, a special attention has been attributed to studying connectedness involving digital financial assets. The growing interest on digital financial assets was mainly due to the high financial returns of this new class of financial assets and the financial opportunities that can offer for investors and market participants in terms of hedging and portfolios diversification (Akhtaruzzaman et al., 2019; Bouri et al., 2017; Guesmi et al., 2019; Platanakis and Urquhart, 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Charfeddine et al., 2020) .

The overall connectedness literature involving digital financial assets can be regrouped into two strands of literature: (1) connectedness between traditional financial assets and digital assets; and (2) connectedness involving only digital financial assets. The first strand of literature concerns studies investigating how Bitcoin is correlated with traditional financial assets and alternative investments, global and emerging equity (Kajtazi and Moro, 2019) , stock market indices (Benlagha, 2020; Benlagha and El Omari, 2022; Gil-Alana et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019) , implied volatility of financial markets (Akyildirim et al., 2019) , industry sectors and bond index (Akhtaruzzaman et al., 2019; Benlagha and Hemrit, 2022) , a variety of other financial assets, including stocks, fixed income assets, energy and precious metals (Benlagha and Mseddi, 2019; Charfeddine et al., 2020; Corbet et al., 2018; Okorie and Lin, 2020) . Note that most of these studies have focused on a single cryptocurrency market, notably the Bitcoin market as it is the most matured and liquid crypto market. In fact, academic researchers have focused much more on the Bitcoin than on others CCs, although the gap has been narrowed.

Another strand in the connectedness literature that emerged more recently has focused on the cross-cryptocurrency markets (Koutmos, 2018; Yi et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2019a; Balli et al., 2019) . Katsiampa (2019a) provides evidence that time-varying conditional correlations exist between Ether and Bitcoin. This study is extended by Katsiampa (2019b) to include other CCs such as Ripple, Litecoin, and Stellar. Findings show that conditional correlations between different pairs of CCs are dynamic. Another closely related paper is Aslanidis et al. (2019) who document that conditional correlations among CCs (Bitcoin, DASH, Monero, and Ripple) are positive and varying across time. Katsiampa et al. (2019b) identify bi-directional volatility spillover effects between all the three pairs of Bitcoin, Ether and Litecoin and show existence of time-varying conditional correlations with positive correlations mostly prevailing. These findings are corroborated by the results of Katsiampa et al. (2019a) using intra-day data for eight CCs. In the same vein, Canh et al. (2019) find that volatility spillovers exist with strong positive correlations among seven largest CCs? examine the volatility spillover between Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Litecoin using the VAR-AGARCH model. They find that the conditional correlations between all pairs of cryptocurrencies are observed to be higher during the COVID-19 period compared to the pre-COVID-19 period.

Relying on Diebold and Yilmaz (2014) framework of variance decomposition, another set of studies investigating the connectedness of CCs document higher interdependencies between CCs, suggesting that CCs are becoming more integrated. For instance, Koutmos (2018) provides evidence that Bitcoin is the most influential regarding to volatility spillovers in the CC market. Further, results indicate that the intensity of these spillovers has risen steadily over time, due to a higher level of market uncertainty. Relatedly, Yi et al. (2018) find that the level of connectedness among CCs is high and fluctuate cyclically. Their results also reveal that CCs with big capitalizations are more probable to propagate volatility shocks to others. Similarly, Balli et al. (2019) find that the magnitude of short-term connectedness among CCs is much higher than medium and long term, and this connectedness is negatively related to economic uncertainty. Same results are found by Ciaian et al. (2018) applying the ARDL model to examine interdependencies between Bitcoin and Altcoin markets: the relationship is more pronounced in the short-run. Interestingly, Ji et al. (2019a) examine connectedness across six large CCs, by employing the approach of Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2015) . They find that Bitcoin is the dominant contributor of volatility spillovers, followed by Litecoin. More recently, Antonakakis et al. (2019) find that Ethereum is the number one net transmitting cryptocurrency, followed by Bitcoin. They also show that the degree of connectedness is positively associated with market uncertainty. Borri (2019) finds,by estimating the conditional tail-risk, that Bitcoin, Ether, Ripple and Litecoin are highly correlated. In a somewhat similar vein, Xu et al. (2020) obtain similar results for 23 CCs, and also find that the total connectedness increases steadily over time. Their results further reveal that Ethereum is the largest systemic risk emitter, while Bitcoin is the largest systemic risk receiver. In the same line, Tiwari et al. (2020) examine the dependence and contagion risk between Bitcoin, Litecoin and Ripple. Their results provide evidence of significant risk contagion among price returns of major CCs, both in bear and bull markets. On the contrary, Zieba et al (2019) find that variations in the price of Bitcoin do not affect and are not affected by variations in prices of other CCs. Using the multifractal analysis to investigate the herding biases, Mnif et al. (2020) quantify the self-similarity intensity of cryptocurrency (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Ripple, Litecoin and Binance) returns' during the COVID-19 pandemic and report evidence that COVID-19 has a positive impact on the cryptocurrency market efficiency. In a similar vein, Vidal-Tomas (2021) uses a network approach to identify the transitions of the cryptocurrency market over time, and observes that COVID-19 significantly affected cryptocurrencies during a short period of financial panic, giving rise to a remarkable increase of market synchronization for Bitcoin, Ethereum, Ripple and Litecoin.

All in all, the broad picture emerging from this literature is that CCs are highly correlated, and the total connectedness increases steadily over time, suggesting that market integration is a continuing and persistent phenomenon. Furthermore, although much effort has been devoted to examining the cross-correlations between CCs, little attention has been paid to the volatility connectedness or spillover effects among different type of CCs. Thus, our study using the spillover index approach to analyze the volatility connectedness across mining coins, non-mining coins and tokens will fill this gap.

To analyze the volatility connectedness between the twelve most traded cryptocurrencies reflecting the three categories of mining coins, non-mining coins and the tokens, we propose to use the Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) framework. Our choice of the Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) approach is mainly motivated by its several advantages when compared to other approaches (Otranto, 2015) . For instance, beyond fulfilling our main goal of assessing the connectedness levels and directions among the digital assets under investigation, this approach allows the investigation of bilateral connectedness as well as displaying the strength and the dynamic of volatility connectedness. Furthermore, the method allows accurate comparisons among alternative model structures and variable sets. More importantly, compared with concurrent correlation-based methods such as the Wavelet analysis and the multivariate GARCH models, Costa et al. (2021) suggest that Diebold and Yilmaz (2012) methodology is advantageous as it enables inferring direction of spillovers for a wide number of simultaneously interacting variables in a clear and compact way. However, the major shortcoming of the method is that the estimated results might be influenced by the chosen window size. To overcome this limitation, the order of the estimated VAR should be well specified. Furthermore, a robustness check methodology should be appropriately conducted. In this section, we will follow and use the same notations for the connectedness measures as in the original paper of Diebold and Yilmaz (2014, 2015) .

3.1. Connectedness measures Diebold and Yilmaz (2012, 2014) propose several measures of volatility connectedness that we group into three main categories based on the type of connectedness (single, total and grand total): (1) own, pairwise and net pairwise directional connectedness, (2) total directional connectedness "from" and "to", (3) net total directional connectedness, and finally (4) the total connectedness index. These four measures of connectedness are summarized in the table below:

The upper-left block of Table 1 reports both the own-connectedness (diagonal entries) and the pairwises directional connectedness from cryptocurrency i to j and from j to i (off-diagonal entries). To calculate the net pairwise directional connectedness, one can just calculate the difference between the two paiwrise directional connectedness. These four individuals connectedness are defined as follow:

The two total directional connectedness "from others" and "to others" are also known as the total directional connectedness "received" and "transmitted", respectively. The total directional connectedness are defined as the share of the H-step forecast error variance of one variable coming from shocks arising in all the other variables. As shown in Table 1 , there are 2N total directional connectedness, an N total directional connectedness "received" and N total directional connectedness "transmitted". As for the own and pairwise directional connectedness, the net total directional connectedness is calculated as the difference between the two "from others" and "to others" total directional connectedness. These three total directional connectedness can be defined as,

The total connectedness index is the grand total of the off-diagonal entries of the upper-left block of Table 1 . This total connectedness measure is viewed as a systemic risk measure (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2012, 2014) . Analytically, it is defined by,

Two measures of market volatility will be used in this study: (1) the Garman and Klass (1980) measure and (2) the Parkinson (1980) measure. While, the results of the first measure will be discussed in details in the paper, the results for the second measure will be only presented as for robustness checking. 5 The Garman and Klass (1980) measure is defined by,

Daily high, low, opening and closing prices are used to calculate the Garman and Klass (1980) and Parkinson (1980) volatility series for the twelve considered cryptocurrencies. All the data was retrieved from https://coinmarketcap.com . Among the thousands of cryptocurrencies available for trading, we focus our analysis on the top CCs in terms of market capitalization and data availability. Precisely, the list of selected cryptocurrencies was subject to two major criteria: (1) we restrict our analysis on the top four mining coins and top four non-mining coins, and top four tokens in terms of market capitalization, and (2) the sample period used should include the last recent period of cryptocurrencies prices boom and bust (end of 2017 and beginning 2018). Moreover, for reason of data availability for the four tokens, we have been restricted to focus our analysis on the period from October 28th, 2017 to April 5th, 2020, which give us a total number of observations of T = 891. The final list of twelve CCs include: (1) the top four mining coins namely Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), Litecoin (LTC) and DASH (DASH), (2) the top four non-mining coins which includes Ripple (XRP), EOS (EOS), Binance coin (BNB), and Stellar (XLM)), and (3) finally the list of the top four tokens that includes Maker (MKR), Basic Attention (BAT), Chainlink (LINK), Augur (REP) (Hardle et al., 2020; Maouchi et al., 2021; Montasser et al., 2021) .

In terms of market capitalization, the twelve CC considered in this study account for more than 81% of the whole CC market as of April 5th, 2020. In our sample, the market value of the mining coins is $145.13b US, which exceeds the 90.9% of the whole market value of the twelve CCs. The non-mining coins and the tokens market values are $13.07b and $1.41b, which represent approximately 8.1% and approximately 1% of the total market value of the twelve CCs, respectively. This clearly shows the dominance of the cryptocurrency market by the mining coins where the sole Bitcoin market share exceeds the 64.4% of the total CC market (more than 5400 cryptocurrencies by the end of April 5th, 2020).

Table 2 lists some other important characteristics of the twelve cryptocurrencies. The most attractive feature is the high variability of CC prices. An analysis by category shows that during our period of analysis, the prices of the mining coins have been multiplied by 285 times for Bitcoin and 5002 times for DASH. For non-mining coins prices have been multiplied between 44 times for EOS and 1127 times for Ripple. Finally, for tokens, prices have been multiplied by 10 times for ChainLink and 74 times for Maker. In terms of ranking, seven out of the twelve cryptocurrencies are ranked among the top 10 crypto-market. Only the four tokens are ranked above the top 20 cryptocurrencies.

Fig. 1 displays the volatility series by CCs category (mining coins, non-mining coins and tokens). We remark that all the three subfigures reported in Fig. 1 show presence of extremes in the daily volatilities of most of the studied cryptocurrencies. For instance, the high volatility observed in 2017 is mainly due to the announcement of the US regulatory to reject the Bitcoin ETF in February 2017. In addition, targeting the Ethereum platform by cyber-attack in September 2017 led to a sharp increase in the volatility of most of the digital assets. However, the rapid increase in the BTC price notably between August ($2797) and December ($19 050), which corresponds to a rise in BCT price of approximately 600%, coincides with the Tweets by John McAfee in November 2017 that Bitcoin will reach $1 million by 2020. On the contrary, the value of BTC shows a decreasing trend between January and December 2018. We can offer possible explanations behind the sharp drop in prices from $ 10 823 to $3429. During this year, two major events took place in the Table 1 Connectedness table (Diebold and Yilmaz, 2014) .

Bitcoin market. First, at the beginning of the year, UBS Chairman Axel Weber and Warren Buffet, CEO of Berkshire Hathaway, warns investors to steer clear of Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies in general. Second, in February, the Chinese government states its intent to ban foreign cryptocurrency exchange platforms in addition to domestic exchanges. Another range of extreme volatility is observed by the end of 2020. Indeed, in that particular period, the Bitcoin was trading at record highs reaching $30 000 and most of the cryptocurrencies behaved similarly. This performance led financial analysts and investors to claim that Bitcoin is a "digital gold" and a store of value. We report also basic descriptive statistics in Table 3 . Several interesting facts emerge from this Table . First, the within day volatility is the lowest for the mining coins and the highest for the tokens. Second, the level of volatility is very high during the period of mid-November 2017, mid-February 2018 and mid-March 2020. Finally, the volatility is persistent according to the estimated fractional long memory parameter (Gaussian semi-parametric estimator of Robinson (1995) ).

In addition, we report the correlation matrix along with the scatter diagram in Fig. 2 for the GK volatility measure. These figures provide a useful preliminary indication about the degree of correlation between the different CCs volatility. Overall, we observe that the correlation is highly significant, e.g. at 1% level of significance, independently from the CC category. In particular, we found that Ethereum has the highest correlations with all the other CCs coins and tokens. Fig. 2 also shows that the intra-mining coins correlations are the highest compared to the intra-non-mining and intra-tokens correlations.
